This is unlike a cylinder or sphere where $r_2$ is constant. As a result, both the meridional and hoop membrane stresses become functions of $\theta$.

The **critical (maximum meridional) stress** occurs at the **innermost meridian**, $\theta = \pi$, where $\cos\theta = -1$ and the denominator $R + (r/2)\cos\theta$ is minimised. The maximum meridional stress is $\sigma_{\phi,\max} = (pr/2t)\cdot 1/(1 - r/2R)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Award **1 mark** for the variation of $r_2$, **1 mark** for the consequence on stresses, **1 mark** for the critical location.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Per the ESE pattern, each Part B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 + (b) 7. Students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 PEMET862, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the membrane stress equations for a thin-walled toroidal pressure vessel under internal pressure. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A toroidal vessel has $R = 500$ mm, $r = 100$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, $p = 2$ MPa. Determine:
   (i) The hoop and meridional stresses at $\theta = 0$, $\pi/2$, and $\pi$.
   (ii) The maximum stress and the stress concentration factor. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions (1 mark):* Thin wall ($r/t \geq 10$), membrane (no bending) regime, doubly-curved shell, internal pressure $p$ uniform.

*Geometry (2 marks):* Principal radii of curvature
$r_1 = r$ (in the meridional plane)
$r_2 = r(R + r\cos\theta)/(R + 2r\cos\theta)$ (in the hoop direction)

*Equilibrium (3 marks):* For a shell of revolution, the two membrane equilibrium equations are:
(i) Normal direction: $N_\phi / r_1 + N_\theta / r_2 = p$
(ii) Meridional direction: integration over the cap from $0$ to $\theta$ yields

$$
N_\phi = \frac{p\,r}{2}\cdot \frac{R}{R + \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta}
$$

Substituting back into (i) and using $r_2$:

$$
N_\theta = \frac{p\,r}{2}\cdot \frac{2R + r\cos\theta}{R + \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta}
$$

Dividing by $t$ gives the **membrane stresses** as derived in Section 3.6 above.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Compute $r/(2R) = 100/(2 \cdot 500) = 0.1$.

*Step 2 (3 marks):* Use the formulas.

At $\theta = 0$ ($\cos\theta = +1$):

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 0.1}{2 \times 0.01}\cdot \frac{2(0.5) + 0.1}{0.5 + 0.05} = 10^7 \cdot \frac{1.1}{0.55} = 10^7 \cdot 2 = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_\phi = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 0.1}{2 \times 0.01}\cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5 + 0.05} = 10^7 \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.55} = 9.09 \text{ MPa}
$$

At $\theta = \pi/2$ ($\cos\theta = 0$):

$$
\sigma_\theta = 10^7 \cdot \frac{1.0}{0.5} = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_\phi = 10^7 \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 10 \text{ MPa}
$$

At $\theta = \pi$ ($\cos\theta = -1$):

$$
\sigma_\theta = 10^7 \cdot \frac{1.0 - 0.1}{0.5 - 0.05} = 10^7 \cdot 2 = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_\phi = 10^7 \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5 - 0.05} = 10^7 \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.45} = 11.11 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):*

Maximum meridional stress $\sigma_{\phi,\max} = 11.11$ MPa at $\theta = \pi$ (inner equator).
Maximum hoop stress $\sigma_{\theta,\max} = 20$ MPa at both equators.

$$
K_t = \frac{1}{1 - r/(2R)} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.1} = 1.111
$$

The SCF of 1.111 indicates an 11.1% amplification over a straight pipe — typical for $r/R = 0.2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in the denominator** — students often write $R + r\cos\theta$ instead of $R + (r/2)\cos\theta$, giving incorrect answers (~2–3 marks lost).
> 2. **Mixing up the angle convention** — $\theta = 0$ at the outer vs inner equator gives opposite SCF directions. Always state your convention explicitly.
> 3. **Neglecting unit conversion** — using mm and MPa directly is fine, but mixing kN and N loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Failing to identify the location** — the question asks *where* the maximum occurs; writing only the numerical answer loses 1 mark.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 PEMET862, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the stress distribution in a toroidal vessel. With the help of neat sketches, compare the membrane stresses with those in cylindrical and spherical vessels of the same radius and thickness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A toroidal pressure vessel with $r = 150$ mm, $t = 8$ mm, $R = 450$ mm is pressurised to $p = 1.5$ MPa. If the allowable stress is 150 MPa, determine the **factor of safety (FoS)** based on:
   (i) Hoop stress
   (ii) Meridional stress
   Comment on which governs the design. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Explanation & Comparison (7 marks):**

*Stress distribution in a torus (3 marks):* In a torus, the second principal radius of curvature $r_2$ is **not constant** — it varies with the meridional angle $\theta$. The membrane stresses are:

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{pr}{2t}\cdot \frac{2R + r\cos\theta}{R + \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta}, \quad \sigma_\phi = \frac{pr}{2t}\cdot \frac{R}{R + \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta}
$$

The hoop stress is approximately constant $\approx pr/t$ around the meridian, but the **meridional stress varies from $\sim pr/(2t)$ at the outer equator to $\sim pr/(2t)\cdot 1/(1 - r/2R)$ at the inner equator**. The inner equator is the **critical design location**.

*Comparison table (3 marks):* For equal $r$, $t$, $p$:

| Geometry | Hoop stress | Meridional/Longitudinal | Maximum |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thin sphere | $pr/(2t)$ | $pr/(2t)$ | $pr/(2t)$ |
| Thin cylinder (closed) | $pr/t$ | $pr/(2t)$ | $pr/t$ |
| Torus (inner equator) | $\approx pr/t$ | $pr/(2t)\cdot 1/(1 - r/2R)$ | $\approx pr/t$ |

The torus is **always heavier than a sphere** and **slightly heavier than a cylinder** for the same pressure capacity. Its design is **governed by the meridional stress at the inner equator**.

*Sketch description (1 mark):* A meridian profile (circle) with arrows showing the meridional and hoop stresses at three stations ($\theta = 0$, $\pi/2$, $\pi$), highlighting the inner equator as the most stressed.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Compute parameters.

$$
\frac{r}{2R} = \frac{150}{2 \times 450} = \frac{150}{900} = 0.1667
$$

$$
\frac{pr}{2t} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.15}{2 \times 0.008} = \frac{225{,}000}{0.016} = 1.406 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 14.06 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Maximum hoop stress (occurs at both equators).

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max} = 2 \cdot \frac{pr}{2t} = \frac{pr}{t} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.15}{0.008} = 28.125 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Maximum meridional stress (at $\theta = \pi$).

$$
\sigma_{\phi,\max} = \frac{pr}{2t}\cdot \frac{1}{1 - r/(2R)} = 14.06 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.1667} = 14.06 \cdot 1.2 = 16.875 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Compute factors of safety.

$$
\text{FoS}_\theta = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{\sigma_{\theta,\max}} = \frac{150}{28.125} = 5.33
$$

$$
\text{FoS}_\phi = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{\sigma_{\phi,\max}} = \frac{150}{16.875} = 8.89
$$

**Comment (in step 4):** The **hoop stress governs** the design (lower FoS of 5.33). For thin-walled toroidal vessels, the hoop stress is consistently the dominant failure criterion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Reporting only one FoS** without stating which stress it corresponds to — 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Computing FoS on average or minimum stress** instead of maximum — 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Failing to comment** on which stress governs the design — 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Confusing inner vs outer equator** in the meridional stress — a common error worth 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up the angle convention** between derivation and numerical substitution.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A torus is a shell of revolution with a circular meridian; key parameters are **R** (major), **r** (minor), **t** (thickness), **p** (pressure).
- **Two principal radii of curvature** at every point: $r_1 = r$ and $r_2 = r(R + r\cos\theta)/(R + 2r\cos\theta)$.
- **Hoop stress** $\sigma_\theta = (pr/2t)\cdot (2R + r\cos\theta)/(R + r\cos\theta/2)$ — approximately constant $\approx pr/t$ around the meridian.
- **Meridional stress** $\sigma_\phi = (pr/2t)\cdot R/(R + r\cos\theta/2)$ — varies from a minimum at the outer equator to a maximum at the **inner equator** ($\theta = \pi$).
- **Critical location:** Inner meridian ($\theta = \pi$) for meridional stress; both equators for hoop stress.
- **Stress Concentration Factor (meridional):** $K_t = 1/(1 - r/(2R))$. Keep $r/R \leq 0.1$ to keep $K_t$ below ~5%.
- **Limit cases:**
  - $R \to \infty$: torus → straight cylinder, $\sigma_\theta = pr/t$, $\sigma_\phi = pr/(2t)$.
  - $R \to r$: torus → near-sphere behaviour.
- **Efficiency ranking:** Sphere (best, $pr/2t$) > Cylinder (intermediate, $pr/t$) > Torus (worst, with $K_t$ amplification at inner equator).
- **Design rule-of-thumb:** For piping expansion loops, maintain $r/R \leq 0.1$ and add a **reinforcement pad** at the inner equator when $r/R > 0.2$.
- **Validity:** Theory valid only in the **membrane regime** — away from nozzles, supports, and geometric discontinuities. For full elastic analysis at the inner equator, use the **Clark-Bhedwar-Johnson** method or FEA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conical Head
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Conical Head # CONICAL HEAD — INTUITIVE OVERVIEW & FORMAL DEFINITION ## 1.1 Formal Engineering Definition A **Conical Head** (also termed a **Conical Reducer**, **Conical End Closure**, or **Frustum Head**) is a pressure vessel component used as an end closure or transition piece that smoothly changes the cross-section from a large diameter (vessel shell) to a smaller diameter (nozzle/piping) through a truncated cone geometry. It is fabricated as a hollow frustum of a right circular cone and is governed primarily by **ASME Section VIII Div. 1, UG-32(e)** and **IS 2825** design rules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 1):** > *"Design of pressure vessel components — cylindrical shell, spherical and conical heads, transition knuckle, flat heads and covers — under internal pressure per ASME / IS codes."* > Conical heads form a **core, high-weightage topic** and questions routinely appear in **Part B (14 marks)** of the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). The geometry of a conical head is characterised by a **half-apex angle (α)** — the angle between the longitudinal axis of the vessel and the slant surface of the cone. | Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | |-----------|--------|---------| | Half-apex angle | α | Angle between vessel axis and cone slant | | Larger diameter | D_L | Inside diameter at cone-shell junction | | Smaller diameter | D_S | Inside diameter at apex (smaller end) | | Slant length | L | Length measured along cone surface | | Knuckle radius | r_k | Torus transition radius (for toriconical) | | Apex height | h | Axial length of the cone | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ice-Cream Cone Vessel" Imagine holding a **waffle ice-cream cone** with the wide open end facing you. The inside of an ice-cream cone is essentially a **truncated cone** — wider at the top, narrower at the bottom, with the entire wall sloping inward at an angle α from the central axis. > **Real-world mappings:** > - **Waffle cone** ⇒ The conical head (the slanted, tapered wall) > - **The hollow interior** ⇒ The pressurized process fluid pressing outward > - **Your hand holding it** ⇒ The cylindrical shell of the pressure vessel, gripping the cone at the wider end > - **The narrow tip of the cone** ⇒ The piping nozzle where the fluid exits the vessel When a fluid is **pressurised inside the cone**, the slanted walls experience two simultaneous forces: 1. **Hoop (circumferential) stress** — pulling the cone walls apart sideways (like blowing up a balloon inside a paper cone). 2. **Membrane stress along the slant** — pushing the cone walls apart along their length. The sharper the cone (smaller α), the more it behaves like a cylinder. The shallower the cone (α closer to 90°), the more it behaves like a flat plate. This **dual behaviour** is what makes conical head design analytically rich. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Applications of Conical Heads:** > - **Hopper bottoms** of silos and storage tanks (solids discharge) > - **Reducers** between piping of mismatched diameters > - **Reactor vessels** with bottom outlets > - **Separator / knock-out drums** for fluid collection > - **Heat exchanger** transition pieces --- ## 1.3 Classification of Conical Heads Conical heads are classified by the geometry of the cone-cylinder transition junction: **(a) Plain Conical Head (Frustum)** — Sharp transition without knuckle. Used when **α ≤ 30°** because discontinuity stresses are tolerable. Simplest and cheapest. **(b) Toriconical Head (Conical with Knuckle)** — Has a toroidal (rounded) knuckle at the cone-shell junction. Used when **α > 30°** to reduce bending stresses. Mandatory per ASME when α > 30° without reinforcement pad. **(c) Conical Head with Reinforcement Pad** — Sharp junction but an external reinforcing ring or pad is welded at the transition. Used when the geometry must remain sharp but discontinuity stresses need to be redistributed. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember the 30° rule:** > *"**T**hirty degrees is the **T**ipping point"* — At α = 30°, the cone acts essentially like a cylinder, and ASME treats its design using the **same membrane-stress formula** as a cylindrical shell. Beyond 30°, the discontinuity becomes structurally significant. # DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Membrane Stress State in a Conical Head When a conical shell of half-apex angle α is subjected to internal pressure **P**, the principal membrane stresses at any point on the slant are: $$\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{P D}{2 t \cos\alpha}$$ $$\sigma_{L} = \frac{P D}{4 t \cos\alpha}$$ where **D** is the local inside diameter and **t** is the local wall thickness. The **hoop stress σ_θ** governs the design because it is the largest of the two. Solving σ_θ ≤ SE for the required thickness t gives the ASME Section VIII formula. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does α matter so much?** > The factor **1/cos α** in the hoop stress formula means a steeper cone (larger α) requires **proportionally thicker walls**. At α = 0°, cos α = 1 (cylinder case). At α = 30°, cos α ≈ 0.866, increasing required thickness by ≈ 15.5 %. --- ## 2.2 The Half-Apex Angle Rule (UG-32(e), ASME) The half-apex angle α determines whether reinforcement is required at the cone-cylinder junction: | Half-apex angle α | Design Treatment | |-------------------|------------------| | α ≤ 30° | Treated as a thin shell. **No reinforcement required** at the junction. Use simple cone formula. | | α > 30° | **Reinforcement mandatory** — provided either by a toroidal knuckle (toriconical) or by an external reinforcement pad. | --- ## 2.3 Required Thickness — Cone Only (α ≤ 30°) Per **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1, UG-32(e)(1)**, the required thickness of a seamless conical section at any point is: $$t = \frac{P D}{2 \cos\alpha \, (SE - 0.6P)}$$ For α ≤ 30°, the ASME code permits using cos α = 1, simplifying to: $$t_{cone} = \frac{P D}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$$ The design must be evaluated at **both ends** of the cone: $$t_L = \frac{P \, D_L}{2 SE - 1.2 P} \qquad \text{(larger end)}$$ $$t_S = \frac{P \, D_S}{2 SE - 1.2 P} \qquad \text{(smaller end)}$$ The governing (larger) value is used. The final ordered thickness is: $$t_{design} = t_{required} + c$$ where **c** is the corrosion allowance (typically **1.5 to 3 mm**, rounded up to the nearest standard plate thickness). --- ## 2.4 Required Thickness — Toriconical (Knuckle Transition) A toriconical head consists of two parts: **(i) The cone portion** (slant section): designed as above. **(ii) The knuckle (torus) portion** at the cone-shell junction: the design follows the **torispherical/toroidal approximation**. For thin-walled knuckle sections, the required thickness is: $$t_{knuckle} = \frac{P \, R_k}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$$ where **R_k** is the inside radius of the toroidal knuckle. The knuckle radius must satisfy the ASME minimum requirements: $$R_k \geq 0.06 \, D_L \qquad \text{and} \qquad R_k \geq 3 t$$ The thickness of the cone and knuckle portions should be **continuous and uniform**, or transitioned smoothly to avoid secondary stress concentrations. --- ## 2.5 Length of the Cone (Geometry Relations) The slant length **L** of the cone between the larger end (D_L) and smaller end (D_S): $$L = \frac{D_L - D_S}{2 \sin\alpha}$$ The axial (perpendicular) height **h** of the cone: $$h = \frac{D_L - D_S}{2 \tan\alpha}$$ --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU-exam-faithful and aligned with ASME Section VIII Div. 1, UG-32(e) / UG-32(f).** Tables below use `\vert` instead of `\vert` to keep markdown safe. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Conditions / Units | |---|----------|---------|-----------|--------------------| | 1 | Required thickness — cone | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$ | P: pressure (MPa), D: inside diameter (mm), S: allowable stress (MPa), E: joint efficiency | α ≤ 30°, thin shell | | 2 | General cone formula | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 \cos\alpha(SE - 0.6 P)}$ | α: half-apex angle | Any α | | 3 | Slant length | $L = \dfrac{D_L - D_S}{2 \sin\alpha}$ | D_L, D_S: large/small inside diameters | All α | | 4 | Cone axial height | $h = \dfrac{D_L - D_S}{2 \tan\alpha}$ | Same as above | All α | | 5 | Knuckle thickness | $t_{k} = \dfrac{P R_k}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$ | R_k: knuckle radius | Toriconical | | 6 | Min. knuckle radius | $R_k \geq \max(0.06 D_L, \ 3 t)$ | t: cone thickness | ASME UG-32 | | 7 | Reinforcement needed | Yes if α > 30° | — | No if α ≤ 30° | | 8 | Allowable test stress | $S_{test} = 1.5 \, S$ (hydrotest) | S: allowable | Per UG-99 | | 9 | MAWP of cone | $MAWP = \dfrac{2 SE t}{D + 1.2 t}$ | t: actual thickness | Reverse calculation | | 10 | Corrosion margin | $t_{design} = t_{required} + c$ | c: corrosion allowance | c = 1.5–3 mm | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Conical heads are the **structural workhorse of solids-handling vessels** in the **petrochemical, food processing, cement, and pharmaceutical** industries. Their primary function is **gravity-driven discharge**: - **Silo hoppers** at the bottom of grain storage silos use conical bottoms with α ≈ 30°–45° so the grain flows out by gravity. - **Petrochemical separators** use conical bottoms to collect water and sludge at a low point. - **Reactor vessels** use conical sections to provide a smooth flow transition into the bottom outlet nozzle, avoiding dead zones where reactants could stagnate. > **Engineering insight:** The 30° rule isn't arbitrary — it emerges from **shallow-shell theory**. Beyond 30°, the discontinuity bending moment at the cone-cylinder junction scales as **1/cos³ α**, growing rapidly. ASME conservatively limits unreinforced designs to keep this bending stress within the allowable primary plus secondary stress range. # STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Cone Thickness Formula We derive the ASME formula $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 \cos\alpha(SE - 0.6P)}$ from first principles using **membrane theory of shells of revolution**. **Step 1 — Equilibrium of a small element on the cone wall.** Consider an infinitesimal element on the cone slant. The element has two principal curvatures: - $R_1$ = radius of curvature in the meridional (slant) direction - $R_2$ = radius of curvature perpendicular to the slant (= $r / \cos\alpha$, where $r$ is the local horizontal radius) For an axisymmetric cone, the meridional radius of curvature is infinite (straight slant), so $R_1 \to \infty$. The **general membrane equilibrium equation** for a shell of revolution under internal pressure P: $$\frac{\sigma_\theta}{R_2} + \frac{\sigma_L}{R_1} = \frac{P}{t}$$ Since $R_1 \to \infty$ for a cone, the term with $\sigma_L$ vanishes, giving: $$\frac{\sigma_\theta}{R_2} = \frac{P}{t}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $R_2$.** For a cone with half-apex angle α, a horizontal cross-section at height z has radius $r = z \tan\alpha$, and the radius of normal curvature to the slant surface is: $$R_2 = \frac{r}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{D}{2\cos\alpha}$$ where $D = 2r$ is the local inside diameter. **Step 3 — Solve for hoop stress.** $$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P R_2}{t} = \frac{P}{t} \cdot \frac{D}{2\cos\alpha}$$ $$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P D}{2 t \cos\alpha}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the ASME strength condition.** The code requires $\sigma_\theta \leq SE$ with a pressure correction factor of 0.6P for thick-wall compensation (analogous to the Barlow correction): $$\frac{P D}{2 t \cos\alpha} = SE - 0.6P$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the required thickness.** $$t = \frac{P D}{2 \cos\alpha \, (SE - 0.6P)}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2: $$t = \frac{P D}{2 SE \cos\alpha - 1.2 P \cos\alpha}$$ For α ≤ 30°, cos α ≥ 0.866, and ASME permits $\cos\alpha \approx 1$ giving the simplified form: $$\boxed{\,t_{cone} = \frac{P D}{2 SE - 1.2 P}\,}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivative Check — Why Larger Diameter Governs The required thickness varies **linearly with D**. Hence the larger diameter $D_L$ at the cone-shell junction always produces the higher required thickness: $$\frac{t_L}{t_S} = \frac{D_L}{D_S}$$ For a cone with $D_L = 1000$ mm and $D_S = 400$ mm, $t_L / t_S = 2.5$. So the large end is 2.5× thicker than the small end — this is why vessels are often built with **stepped or tapered plates** instead of a uniform cone thickness. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Style) **Problem Statement (KTU Examination Style):** > *"A pressure vessel has a cylindrical shell of inside diameter 1500 mm operating at a design pressure of 2.0 MPa. The vessel has a plain conical head (no knuckle) at the bottom connecting the shell to a 600 mm outlet nozzle. Design the conical head using ASME Section VIII Div. 1, given allowable stress S = 138 MPa, joint efficiency E = 1.0 (for seamless cone), corrosion allowance c = 2 mm. Take half-apex angle α = 25°."* ### Given Data - $D_L = 1500$ mm (larger end diameter at junction with shell) - $D_S = 600$ mm (smaller end diameter at outlet nozzle) - $P = 2.0$ MPa - $S = 138$ MPa - $E = 1.0$ - $c = 2$ mm - $\alpha = 25°$ ### Step 1 — Verify the half-apex angle condition Check whether the cone qualifies for unreinforced design: $$\alpha = 25° \leq 30° \quad \checkmark$$ Since $\alpha \leq 30°$, **no reinforcement pad or knuckle is required** at the cone-cylinder junction. ### Step 2 — Compute the denominator term $$2 SE - 1.2 P = 2 \times 138 \times 1.0 - 1.2 \times 2.0$$ $$= 276 - 2.4 = 273.6 \text{ MPa}$$ ### Step 3 — Required thickness at the larger end $$t_L = \frac{P \, D_L}{2 SE - 1.2 P} = \frac{2.0 \times 1500}{273.6}$$ $$t_L = \frac{3000}{273.6} = 10.96 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 4 — Required thickness at the smaller end $$t_S = \frac{P \, D_S}{2 SE - 1.2 P} = \frac{2.0 \times 600}{273.6}$$ $$t_S = \frac{1200}{273.6} = 4.39 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 5 — Select the governing thickness The required thickness is the **larger** of the two (at the larger diameter): $$t_{required} = \max(t_L, \, t_S) = 10.96 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 6 — Add corrosion allowance and round up $$t_{design} = t_{required} + c = 10.96 + 2 = 12.96 \text{ mm}$$ Round up to the **next standard plate thickness**: $\mathbf{t_{design} = 14 \text{ mm}}$. ### Step 7 — Slant length of the cone $$L = \frac{D_L - D_S}{2 \sin\alpha} = \frac{1500 - 600}{2 \sin 25°}$$ $$L = \frac{900}{2 \times 0.4226} = \frac{900}{0.8452}$$ $$L = 1064.8 \text{ mm} \approx 1065 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 8 — Axial height of the cone $$h = \frac{D_L - D_S}{2 \tan\alpha} = \frac{1500 - 600}{2 \tan 25°}$$ $$h = \frac{900}{2 \times 0.4663} = \frac{900}{0.9326}$$ $$h = 965.1 \text{ mm} \approx 965 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 9 — Verification of MAWP The **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** at the larger end using the actual ordered thickness: $$MAWP = \frac{2 SE t}{D_L + 1.2 t} = \frac{2 \times 138 \times 1.0 \times 14}{1500 + 1.2 \times 14}$$ $$= \frac{3864}{1516.8} = 2.547 \text{ MPa}$$ Since MAWP = 2.547 MPa > P = 2.0 MPa, **the design is safe**. ✓ ### Step 10 — Summary Table | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | |----------|--------|-------|------| | Required thickness (large end) | $t_L$ | 10.96 | mm | | Required thickness (small end) | $t_S$ | 4.39 | mm | | Selected thickness (with corrosion) | $t$ | 14.0 | mm | | Slant length | $L$ | 1065 | mm | | Axial height | $h$ | 965 | mm | | MAWP at large end | MAWP | 2.547 | MPa | | Design margin | — | +27.4 % | — | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Pattern Note:** In a 14-mark problem, examiners expect **all 10 steps** above with units shown at every stage. Skipping the MAWP verification is the most common reason students lose **2 marks** in valuation. --- ## 3.4 Reinforcement Case — When α > 30° For completeness, when α > 30°, ASME UG-32(e)(2) requires either: **Option A — Toriconical head (knuckle transition):** - Insert a toroidal knuckle of radius $R_k \geq \max(0.06 D_L, \, 3 t)$ between the cone and the shell. - Design the cone with $t = P D_L / [2 \cos\alpha(SE - 0.6 P)]$. - Design the knuckle with $t_k = P R_k / (2 SE - 1.2 P)$. **Option B — Reinforcement pad at the junction:** - Weld a ring or pad to absorb the discontinuity. - Calculate the required reinforcement area $A_R$ using the area replacement method (UG-37): $$A_R = \frac{P \, D_L \, t}{2 S E} - t$$ This is more complex and is typically covered in detail under **reinforcement of openings**. # STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Geometry & Nomenclature of a Conical Head (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Cylindrical Pressure Vessel Shell
Inside Diameter DL"] B["Weld Joint at Cone Shell Junction
Required if Alpha greater than 30 deg"] C["Conical Head Slant Section
Truncated Frustum of Right Cone
Half Apex Angle alpha"] D["Smaller End Nozzle
Inside Diameter DS"] E["Slant Length L measured along cone surface"] F["Axial Height h of cone"] G["Knuckle Torus Section for Toriconical
Radius Rk greater than 0.06 DL and 3t"] A --> B B --> C C --> D C -. measures .-> E C -. measures .-> F B -. optional transition .-> G stepA["Design Pressure P input"] stepB["Allowable Stress S input"] stepC["Joint Efficiency E input"] stepD["Corrosion Allowance c input"] stepE["Compute t_L at large end"] stepF["Compute t_S at small end"] stepG["Governing thickness t = max of t_L and t_S"] stepH["Add corrosion allowance and round to standard plate"] stepI["Compute slant length L and axial height h"] stepJ["Verify MAWP greater than design pressure P"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** > - The **upper block row** depicts the **physical geometry** — what a conical head actually looks like in a vessel. > - The **lower block row** depicts the **design calculation sequence** — the order of operations a designer follows. > - Both rows converge to produce a safe, code-compliant conical head. --- ## 4.2 Stress Distribution Across the Cone (Conceptual Block Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR P["Internal Pressure P
Acting Uniformly on Cone Walls"] SH["Membrane Hoop Stress sigma theta
Largest at larger end D sub L"] SL["Membrane Longitudinal Stress sigma L
Along slant direction"] SD["Discontinuity Stress at Junction
Only if alpha greater than 30 deg"] KR["Knuckle or Reinforcement Pad
Reduces discontinuity stress"] P --> SH P --> SL P --> SD SD --> KR sigma1["sigma theta equals PD divided by 2t cos alpha"] sigma2["sigma L equals PD divided by 4t cos alpha"] sigma3["Discontinuity Bending Moment scales as 1 over cos cubed alpha"] SH -. formula .-> sigma1 SL -. formula .-> sigma2 SD -. formula .-> sigma3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Design Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{"Is half apex angle alpha
less than or equal to 30 deg?"} q2{"Is a sharp junction
permitted by client?"} pathA["Use Plain Conical Head
t = P D divided by 2 SE minus 1.2 P"] pathB["Use Toriconical Head
Add knuckle Rk greater than 0.06 DL"] pathC["Use Reinforcement Pad
Compute area AR per UG-37"] q1 -- YES --> pathA q1 -- NO --> q2 q2 -- YES --> pathC q2 -- NO --> pathB ``` > [!TIP] > **How to use this in your answer:** This flowchart is a **3-mark fetch** in Part A if you can draw it cleanly with labels. Examiners reward students who **combine a sketch + decision logic** rather than just writing formulas. # KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is a conical head? Under what condition is no reinforcement required at the cone-cylinder junction?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A conical head is a pressure vessel end closure shaped as a truncated cone (frustum) that smoothly reduces the vessel cross-section from a larger diameter (shell) to a smaller diameter (outlet nozzle). It is designed using membrane-shell theory under internal pressure. **Reinforcement condition:** When the **half-apex angle α ≤ 30°**, no reinforcement (knuckle or pad) is required at the cone-cylinder junction because the discontinuity bending stresses remain within the permissible limits specified by ASME Section VIII Div. 1, UG-32(e). Beyond α = 30°, either a toriconical knuckle or a reinforcement pad must be provided. *(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — sketch mention, 1 mark — 30° condition and code reference.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the ASME formula for the required thickness of a conical head with half-apex angle α ≤ 30°. List the variables with units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$t = \frac{P D}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$$ | Variable | Meaning | Unit | |----------|---------|------| | P | Design (internal) pressure | MPa (N/mm²) | | D | Inside diameter of cone at the section under consideration | mm | | S | Allowable stress of the cone material at design temperature | MPa | | E | Joint efficiency of the cone weld (E = 1.0 for seamless) | dimensionless | | t | Required thickness (without corrosion allowance) | mm | The formula applies at the **larger diameter end** $D_L$, which is the governing location because required thickness scales linearly with diameter. The final ordered thickness is $t_{design} = t + c$, where c is the corrosion allowance. *(1 mark — formula, 1 mark — variables, 1 mark — application note and corrosion allowance.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *Note: As per KTU ESE pattern, students answer **ONE** of the two internal choice questions.* --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`** > **(a)** Derive the expression for the required thickness of a conical head with half-apex angle α subjected to internal pressure, using membrane-shell equilibrium. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A pressure vessel cylindrical shell has an inside diameter of 1200 mm and operates at a design pressure of 1.5 MPa. The vessel is closed at the bottom by a plain conical head with the half-apex angle α = 20°, connecting to an outlet of 500 mm diameter. Taking allowable stress S = 120 MPa, joint efficiency E = 0.85, and corrosion allowance c = 2 mm, design the conical head thickness and compute the slant length. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution (a) — Derivation [7 marks] **Assumptions:** 1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law. 2. Shell is **thin** ($t / D \leq 0.05$). 3. Half-apex angle α is small (≤ 30°) so membrane theory is valid. 4. Internal pressure P acts uniformly normal to the cone surface. 5. Weld joints are fully radiographed (E = 1.0 unless specified). **Equilibrium of a cone element (membrane theory):** The general shell-of-revolution equilibrium equation under internal pressure P is: $$\frac{\sigma_\theta}{R_2} + \frac{\sigma_L}{R_1} = \frac{P}{t}$$ For a cone, the meridional radius $R_1 \to \infty$ (straight slant), so the second term vanishes: $$\frac{\sigma_\theta}{R_2} = \frac{P}{t}$$ The radius of normal curvature to the slant of a cone is $R_2 = r / \cos\alpha = D / (2\cos\alpha)$. Substituting: $$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P \, D}{2 t \cos\alpha}$$ Applying the ASME strength condition $\sigma_\theta = SE - 0.6P$ (pressure-thick wall correction): $$\frac{P D}{2 t \cos\alpha} = SE - 0.6P$$ Solving for t: $$\boxed{t = \frac{P D}{2 \cos\alpha \, (SE - 0.6P)}}$$ For α ≤ 30°, $\cos\alpha \approx 1$, giving the simplified form $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$. --- #### Solution (b) — Numerical Design [7 marks] **Given:** $D_L = 1200$ mm, $D_S = 500$ mm, $P = 1.5$ MPa, $S = 120$ MPa, $E = 0.85$, $\alpha = 20°$, $c = 2$ mm. **Step 1 — Check α:** $$\alpha = 20° \leq 30° \quad \text{(no reinforcement required)} \quad ✓$$ **Step 2 — Compute denominator:** $$2 SE - 1.2P = 2 \times 120 \times 0.85 - 1.2 \times 1.5 = 204 - 1.8 = 202.2 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Required thickness at large end:** $$t_L = \frac{1.5 \times 1200}{202.2} = \frac{1800}{202.2} = 8.90 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Required thickness at small end:** $$t_S = \frac{1.5 \times 500}{202.2} = \frac{750}{202.2} = 3.71 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5 — Governing thickness:** $$t_{required} = \max(t_L, t_S) = 8.90 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 — Add corrosion allowance:** $$t_{design} = 8.90 + 2 = 10.90 \text{ mm} \xrightarrow{\text{round up}} 12 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 7 — Slant length:** $$L = \frac{D_L - D_S}{2 \sin\alpha} = \frac{1200 - 500}{2 \sin 20°} = \frac{700}{2 \times 0.3420} = \frac{700}{0.6840} = 1023.4 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 8 — Axial height (bonus, for completeness):** $$h = \frac{D_L - D_S}{2 \tan\alpha} = \frac{700}{2 \times 0.3640} = \frac{700}{0.7280} = 961.5 \text{ mm}$$ **Final design:** Conical head thickness **t = 12 mm** (standard plate), slant length **L ≈ 1024 mm**, axial height **h ≈ 962 mm**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** > **(a)** Explain the different types of conical heads used in pressure vessels. With a neat sketch, describe the geometry and the function of a toriconical head. When is it preferred over a plain conical head? **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A toriconical head has the following data: Larger diameter $D_L = 1800$ mm, smaller diameter $D_S = 800$ mm, design pressure $P = 1.0$ MPa, allowable stress $S = 140$ MPa, joint efficiency $E = 1.0$, half-apex angle $\alpha = 35°$, knuckle radius $R_k = 250$ mm, corrosion allowance $c = 1.5$ mm. Design the cone and the knuckle thickness. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution (a) — Classification [7 marks] **Three types of conical heads:** **(1) Plain Conical Head (Frustum):** Sharp transition between cone and shell without any knuckle. Used when α ≤ 30°. Membrane-stress formula directly applies. Cheapest to fabricate but limited to shallow cones. **(2) Toriconical Head (Knuckle Transition):** Cone with a toroidal (rounded) knuckle at the junction. The knuckle redistributes the discontinuity stresses that would otherwise concentrate at a sharp junction. **Preferred when α > 30°** because: - The smooth curvature reduces bending stresses - Avoids need for external reinforcement pad - Improves fatigue life under cyclic pressure **(3) Reinforced Conical Head (with Pad):** Sharp junction but with an external ring/pad welded at the cone-shell junction. Used when geometric profile must be sharp but the local bending stress is too high. **Sketch description (verbal, 3 marks):** ``` Cylindrical shell |----------------------| | | | ___________ | ← Larger diameter D_L (junction) | / \ | | / \ | ← Cone (half-apex angle α) | / \ | | / \ | ← Slant length L |/_________ _______\| ← Smaller diameter D_S (knuckle) ``` **Geometry:** Larger diameter $D_L$ at junction, smaller diameter $D_S$ at apex, slant length L, axial height h, half-apex angle α. **Function:** Smoothly transitions between two diameters, reducing stress concentration. The knuckle radius $R_k \geq \max(0.06 D_L, \, 3t)$ per ASME UG-32(e)(2). --- #### Solution (b) — Numerical Design [7 marks] **Given:** $D_L = 1800$ mm, $D_S = 800$ mm, $P = 1.0$ MPa, $S = 140$ MPa, $E = 1.0$, $\alpha = 35°$, $R_k = 250$ mm, $c = 1.5$ mm. **Step 1 — Cone required thickness at large end:** The general cone formula (since α > 30°, use cos α factor): $$\cos\alpha = \cos 35° = 0.8192$$ $$t_L = \frac{P D_L}{2 \cos\alpha \, (SE - 0.6P)} = \frac{1.0 \times 1800}{2 \times 0.8192 \times (140 \times 1.0 - 0.6 \times 1.0)}$$ $$t_L = \frac{1800}{2 \times 0.8192 \times 139.4} = \frac{1800}{228.39} = 7.88 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Required thickness at small end:** $$t_S = \frac{P D_S}{2 \cos\alpha \, (SE - 0.6P)} = \frac{1.0 \times 800}{228.39} = 3.50 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Cone governing thickness (large end):** $$t_{cone} = \max(t_L, t_S) + c = 7.88 + 1.5 = 9.38 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{round up to } 10 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Knuckle thickness:** $$t_{knuckle} = \frac{P \, R_k}{2 SE - 1.2 P} = \frac{1.0 \times 250}{2 \times 140 \times 1.0 - 1.2 \times 1.0} = \frac{250}{278.8} = 0.897 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5 — Apply minimum practical thickness and corrosion:** $$t_{knuckle, design} = 0.897 + 1.5 = 2.397 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{round up to } 3 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 — Verify knuckle radius satisfies ASME:** $$R_k \geq 0.06 D_L = 0.06 \times 1800 = 108 \text{ mm} \quad ✓$$ $$R_k \geq 3 t = 3 \times 10 = 30 \text{ mm} \quad ✓$$ Both satisfied. ✓ **Final design:** - Cone thickness = **10 mm** at large end (tapered down to 5 mm near small end) - Knuckle thickness = **3 mm** (governed by minimum practical + corrosion) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Conical Head Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 30° rule** — Students often compute $t$ without first checking whether α exceeds 30°. If α > 30°, you **must** use the cos α factor OR specify a toriconical head. Marks lost: **2**. > > 2. **Using D_L incorrectly** — Some students use the **outside diameter** instead of **inside diameter**. Always use **inside diameter D** in the formula. > > 3. **Forgetting corrosion allowance** — Always add **c** before rounding up. Marks lost: **1**. > > 4. **Not specifying units** — Every numerical step must carry units (MPa, mm). Marks lost: **1**. > > 5. **Skipping MAWP verification** — A complete 14-mark answer must verify that the chosen plate thickness can withstand the design pressure (reverse calculation). Marks lost: **2**. > > 6. **Confusing $0.6P$ vs $1.2P$** — In the cone formula written as $PD/(2SE - 1.2P)$, the $1.2$ comes from $2 \times 0.6$. In the formula $PD/(2\cos\alpha(SE-0.6P))$, the $0.6$ is inside. Don't mix them up. > > 7. **Forgetting slant length** — When the problem asks to "design the conical head," the slant length L must be reported as part of the final design. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conical Head — Things to Remember Before Every KTU Exam:** - ✅ **Definition:** A truncated cone (frustum) end closure with half-apex angle α. - ✅ **ASME Code Reference:** Section VIII Div. 1, **UG-32(e)**. - ✅ **30° Rule:** No reinforcement needed if α ≤ 30°. **Always check this first.** - ✅ **Core Formula:** $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$ for α ≤ 30° (use $D_L$ — the larger diameter). - ✅ **General Cone Formula:** $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 \cos\alpha (SE - 0.6 P)}$ for any α. - ✅ **Knuckle Formula (Toriconical):** $t_k = \dfrac{P R_k}{2 SE - 1.2 P}$. - ✅ **Min Knuckle Radius:** $R_k \geq \max(0.06 D_L, \, 3t)$. - ✅ **Slant Length:** $L = (D_L - D_S) / (2 \sin\alpha)$. - ✅ **Axial Height:** $h = (D_L - D_S) / (2 \tan\alpha)$. - ✅ **Final Ordered Thickness:** $t_{design} = t_{required} + c$ (round UP to standard plate). - ✅ **MAWP Reverse Check:** $MAWP = \dfrac{2 SE t}{D_L + 1.2 t}$ must exceed design pressure P. - ✅ **Governing Diameter:** Always the **larger end** $D_L$ because t ∝ D. - ✅ **Stress Type:** Hoop stress (circumferential) is dominant; $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{P D}{2 t \cos\alpha}$. - ✅ **Three Types:** Plain conical, toriconical, reinforced (pad) — pick based on α and design preference. - ✅ **Industrial Use:** Hopper bottoms, separators, reactor outlets, transition pieces in piping. - ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"Thirty degrees is the tipping point — beyond it, knuckle it!"*

topics.title: Elliptical Head
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elliptical Head

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Elliptical Head** (more precisely, a **2:1 Ellipsoidal Head**) is a toroidal/axisymmetric pressure vessel closure formed by cutting a head from a solid of revolution generated by revolving a **semi-ellipse** about the major axis of the ellipse, such that the ratio of the major axis to the minor axis is **2 : 1**. It is the most widely used head geometry in the process, petrochemical, and power industries because it provides an excellent compromise between structural efficiency and manufacturing economy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Proportion (2:1 Ellipsoidal Head):**
> - Inside Major Diameter $\,=\, 2a \,=\, D$ (i.e., the vessel inside diameter)
> - Inside Minor Depth $\,=\, 2b \,=\, D/2$ (i.e., the head is "D/4" deep on each side of the polar axis)
> - Therefore $a = D/2$ and $b = D/4$.

The radius of curvature at the **crown (apex)** of a 2:1 ellipsoidal head is $R_c = D$ (this is the *crown radius*). The radius of curvature at the **knuckle** (junction with the cylindrical shell) is $R_k = a^2/b = D$ for the pure mathematical ellipse; however, in manufactured heads a **straight flange (skirt)** of length $h \approx 1.5\,t$ to $3\,t$ and an optional **knuckle radius** $r \approx 0.17 D$ is provided to ensure smooth transition and welding clearance.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The "Egg" Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Half-Boiled Egg:**
> Take a chicken egg and slice it in half through its long axis. The outer curved profile of that half-egg is *exactly* the shape of a 2:1 ellipsoidal head. The pointed end is the **crown** (carries the maximum internal pressure load), and the flat open end is welded to the cylindrical shell. This shape is nature's optimal way to contain internal pressure (a sphere is theoretically best, but a half-egg is easier to fabricate on a press).

The deepest part of the head (the "pole" of the ellipse) is called the **crown** or **apex**, while the curved transition region joining the head to the cylindrical shell is called the **knuckle**. Because the knuckle region is a *discontinuity* between the curved head and the straight shell, it experiences **stress concentration** and is the most fatigue-prone location.

## 1.3 Code References & Standardization

| Code / Standard | Designation | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1** | UG-32 (d) | Most common — 2:1 ellipsoidal head thickness formula |
| **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 2** | Part 4.4 | Alternative rules (stress categorisation) |
| **IS 2825 (Indian Std.)** | Unfired Pressure Vessels | Mirrors ASME 2:1 ratio |
| **PD 5500 (British)** | Annex A | Category 1 vessels |
| **EN 13445 (European)** | Section 8 | Used in EU jurisdictions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The 2:1 ratio is *not* arbitrary. It was the original "standard proportion" developed by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers because it is the **deepest ellipsoidal head** that can be pressed from a single flat plate in a single die operation using the cold-forming process — making it the most economical head to manufacture. The crown radius $R_c = D$ is what gives it near-spherical strength at roughly the cost of a shallower head.

**Common Engineering Units Used in this Module:**
- $D$ (Inside Diameter) — **mm** or **m**
- $P$ (Internal Design Pressure) — **MPa** or **N/mm²** (= MPa)
- $t$ (Required Thickness) — **mm**
- $S$ (Allowable Stress) — **MPa** or **N/mm²**
- $E$ (Joint Efficiency) — **dimensionless** (0.70 to 1.00)
- $V$ (Volume) — **m³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Geometry (Why the 2:1 Ratio?)

The profile of an elliptical head is described by the canonical ellipse equation (in the meridional cross-section):

$$\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$$

where $x$ is measured from the polar axis (vertical centerline) and $y$ is the radial distance from the same axis. For the **2:1** standard head we have $a = D/2$ and $b = D/4$, giving the half-ellipse profile:

$$y(x) = \frac{b}{a}\sqrt{a^2 - x^2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(D/2)^2 - x^2}$$

**Curvature Analysis (Why the Head is Strong):**

The radius of curvature at any point on the ellipse is given by the formula:

$$R = \frac{\left[a^2 - x^2 + (b^4/a^2)\right]^{3/2}}{a \cdot b}$$

At the **crown** ($x = 0$):

$$R_c = \frac{a^2}{b} = \frac{(D/2)^2}{D/4} = D$$

This is why we use $D$ as the effective radius for membrane-stress calculations at the apex.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Head Design (Step-Wise)

A KTU examiner's question on elliptical head will typically follow this decision flow:

1. **Step 1 — Identify Design Inputs:** Internal design pressure $P$, inside diameter $D$, material allowable stress $S$, joint efficiency $E$, corrosion allowance $CA$, and design temperature.
2. **Step 2 — Verify Geometry:** Confirm the head is a *standard 2:1 ellipsoidal head* (so the ASME formula UG-32(d) applies); otherwise use the more general formula.
3. **Step 3 — Compute Crown Radius:** $R_c = D$ for a 2:1 head.
4. **Step 4 — Apply Thickness Formula:** $t_{req} = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$ (membrane theory with pressure correction).
5. **Step 5 — Add Corrosion Allowance:** $t_{actual} = t_{req} + CA + \text{mill allowance (if applicable)}$.
6. **Step 6 — Round Up:** Select next standard plate thickness per IS 2062 / SA-516 / SA-240.
7. **Step 7 — Check Knuckle Stress:** Ensure the discontinuity stress at the knuckle is within code limits (often satisfied by adding a knuckle radius $r = 0.17D$).
8. **Step 8 — Compute Volume / Weight:** For logistics, transport, and nozzle-loading estimates.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas assume SI units (mm and MPa = N/mm²).** The factor **0.2P** in the denominator is the *pressure-correction term* — it is **mandatory** in UG-32 and is *not* a typo.

### A. Design Thickness Formulas (ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1, UG-32)

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $t_{req} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2SE - 0.2P}$ | **Required thickness** of 2:1 ellipsoidal head |
| 2 | $t_{nom} = t_{req} + CA$ | **Nominal (ordered) thickness** with corrosion allowance |
| 3 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot S \cdot E}{D + 0.4t}$ | **Maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP)** |
| 4 | $R_c = D$ | **Crown radius** of 2:1 ellipsoidal head |
| 5 | $r = 0.17D$ | **Standard knuckle radius** (recommended) |
| 6 | $h_{head} = b + h_{skirt}$ | **Total head depth** = minor radius + straight flange |
| 7 | $h_{skirt} \approx 1.5\,t \text{ to } 3\,t$ | **Straight flange (skirt) length** for weld clearance |

### B. Volume, Capacity, and Weight Formulas

| # | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 8 | $V_{head} = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{24}$ | **Internal volume** of a 2:1 ellipsoidal head (ideal, no skirt) |
| 9 | $V_{actual} = V_{head} + \pi r^2 h_{skirt}$ | **Volume with straight skirt** |
| 10 | $W_{head} = \rho_m \cdot t_{nom} \cdot A_{blank}$ | **Weight of formed head** (uses blank area) |
| 11 | $A_{blank} \approx \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot (1 + 0.17)$ | **Approx. blank plate area** (adds 17% for thinning/trim) |
| 12 | $D_o = D + 2 t_{nom}$ | **Outside diameter** of head (for nozzle layout) |

### C. Stress Verification at Crown (Membrane Theory)

| # | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 13 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P R_c}{2 t} = \dfrac{P D}{2 t}$ | Membrane hoop stress at crown |
| 14 | $\sigma_{meridional} = \dfrac{P R_c}{2 t} = \dfrac{P D}{2 t}$ | Membrane meridional stress (equal at apex) |
| 15 | $\sigma_{allowable} = S \cdot E$ | Allowable stress with joint efficiency |
| 16 | $\sigma_{von} = \vert \sigma_{hoop} - \sigma_{meridional} \vert$ | Von Mises (at crown $\rightarrow 0$) |
| 17 | $SIF = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{t}{r}\right)\cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{D}{2r}\right)$ | Stress intensification at knuckle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Clarification — Crown vs. Knuckle:** The ASME thickness formula (Eq. 1) is derived from the **membrane stress at the crown**, *not* the peak stress at the knuckle. The knuckle stress is handled by **code-imposed geometry** (the use of $r = 0.17D$ and the appropriate $L/D$ ratio of the shell) rather than by an explicit stress calculation in Div. 1.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Storage Vessels:** Atmospheric & low-pressure storage tanks for LPG, ammonia, and crude oil.
- **Heat Exchangers:** Channel and bonnet heads of shell-and-tube exchangers.
- **Reactor Vessels:** End closures for batch reactors in pharma & specialty chemicals.
- **Boiler Drums:** End closures of fire-tube boilers.
- **Why not a hemisphere?** A true hemisphere is *theoretically* the strongest, but forming a $D/2$ deep hemisphere from a $D$-diameter flat plate requires **multiple pressing stages** and a much larger press capacity — typically **2× to 3× the cost** of a 2:1 ellipsoidal head.
- **Why not a flat plate?** Flat plates require thicknesses of $5\times$ to $10\times$ the shell thickness to resist bending — they are not economical for diameters above ~600 mm.
- **Why not a cone?** Cones are used for transitions between different diameters or for solids-handling hoppers, not for pressure containment.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head Thickness Formula

We derive the formula $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$ from **first principles of membrane theory of shells of revolution**, which is the foundation expected in KTU Module 1 viva and written exams.

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Equilibrium of a Shell of Revolution

Consider a thin-walled pressure vessel head shaped as a surface of revolution. The membrane (mid-surface) equilibrium equations in axisymmetric loading are:

$$\frac{N_\phi}{R_\phi} + \frac{N_\theta}{R_\theta} = P$$

where $N_\phi$ and $N_\theta$ are the meridional and circumferential (hoop) membrane forces per unit length, and $R_\phi$ and $R_\theta$ are the principal radii of curvature in the meridional and circumferential directions respectively.

### 3.1.2 Curvatures for the 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head

For the ellipse profile $y(x) = \dfrac{b}{a}\sqrt{a^2 - x^2}$ (with $a = D/2$, $b = D/4$), the principal radii of curvature at any point are:

$$R_\phi = \frac{\left[1 + (dy/dx)^2\right]^{3/2}}{\vert d^2y/dx^2 \vert} \quad , \quad R_\theta = \frac{x}{\sin\psi}$$

where $\psi$ is the angle the meridian makes with the polar axis. For the head, the **polar axis is the vertical axis of symmetry** (the axis of the cylindrical shell).

### 3.1.3 Specialisation at the Crown (Apex)

At the crown ($x = 0$), by symmetry:

- $dy/dx = 0$ at the apex.
- $d^2y/dx^2 = -\dfrac{b}{a^2}$ at the apex.
- Therefore $R_\phi = \dfrac{a^2}{b} = \dfrac{(D/2)^2}{D/4} = D$.
- $R_\theta = R_\phi = D$ at the apex (sphere-like local geometry).

### 3.1.4 Stress at the Crown

At the crown, $R_\phi = R_\theta = R_c = D$, and the equilibrium equation gives:

$$N_\phi + N_\theta = P R_c = P D$$

By symmetry of the apex, $N_\phi = N_\theta = N$, so:

$$2N = P D \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = \frac{P D}{2}$$

The membrane stress is $\sigma = N/t$ (force per unit length divided by thickness):

$$\sigma = \frac{P D}{2 t}$$

This is the **membrane hoop and meridional stress at the crown** — they are equal in magnitude and uniform in the principal directions at this point.

### 3.1.5 Applying the Allowable Stress Criterion

For safe design, the membrane stress must not exceed the allowable stress (with joint efficiency):

$$\sigma \le S \cdot E$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{P D}{2 t} \le S E$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t \ge \frac{P D}{2 S E}$$

### 3.1.6 Refinement — The "−0.2P" Pressure Correction

The above expression is the **first-order** design equation. However, for thick shells (where $t/D$ is non-negligible), the inner surface of the shell carries higher stress than the mid-surface. The **Tresca / membrane-with-correction** derivation (found in ASME Code Commentary) adds a pressure-dependent term to the denominator:

For thick spherical shells, the exact Lamé solution gives the inner-fibre stress as:

$$\sigma_i = \frac{P(a^3 + 2b^3)}{2 b^2 (a^3 - b^3)}$$

where $a$ = outer radius and $b$ = inner radius. For thin shells, Taylor-expanding gives the ASME pressure-correction form. For the 2:1 ellipsoidal head, ASME code (UG-32 d) writes:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

This is **derived by the code committee from the Lamé solution**, and the factor 0.2P is empirically calibrated to ensure inner-fibre stress never exceeds $SE$ for any practical $t/D$ ratio. This is the **canonical** formula you must memorise.

### 3.1.7 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step — KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Board Pattern):** A pressure vessel has an inside diameter of $D = 2000$ mm and operates at a design pressure of $P = 2.0$ MPa. The material is SA-516 Grade 70 with an allowable stress of $S = 138$ MPa at design temperature. The joint efficiency (Category A butt weld, fully radiographed) is $E = 1.0$. The corrosion allowance is $CA = 1.5$ mm. Design a 2:1 ellipsoidal head.

**Step 1 — Identify inputs.**

$$D = 2000 \text{ mm}, \quad P = 2.0 \text{ MPa}, \quad S = 138 \text{ MPa}, \quad E = 1.0, \quad CA = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Crown radius.**

$$R_c = D = 2000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Required thickness (ASME UG-32 d).**

$$t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

Substitute the values:

$$t_{req} = \frac{(2.0)(2000)}{2(138)(1.0) - 0.2(2.0)} = \frac{4000}{276 - 0.4} = \frac{4000}{275.6}$$

$$t_{req} = 14.514 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Add corrosion allowance.**

$$t_{nom} = t_{req} + CA = 14.514 + 1.5 = 16.014 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Round up to standard plate thickness (per IS 2062).**

$$t_{standard} = 18 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{next standard size above } 16.014 \text{ mm})$$

**Step 6 — Verify MAWP for the chosen thickness.**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 t_{standard} S E}{D + 0.4 \, t_{standard}} = \frac{2(18)(138)(1.0)}{2000 + 0.4(18)} = \frac{4968}{2007.2} = 2.476 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Check that MAWP $\ge$ design pressure.**

$$P_{max} = 2.476 \text{ MPa} > P = 2.0 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

The 18 mm thick head is **SAFE** with a margin of $(2.476 - 2.0)/2.0 \times 100\% = 23.8\%$ over-design.

**Step 8 — Compute head depth and volume.**

$$h_{head} = b + h_{skirt} = \frac{D}{4} + 2t = \frac{2000}{4} + 2(18) = 500 + 36 = 536 \text{ mm}$$

$$V_{head} = \frac{\pi D^3}{24} = \frac{\pi (2000)^3}{24} = \frac{\pi \times 8 \times 10^9}{24} = 1.047 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^3 = 1.047 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 9 — Compute weight of formed head (assuming carbon steel $\rho_m = 7850$ kg/m³).**

Blank area: $A_{blank} \approx \pi D^2 / 4 \times 1.17 = \pi (1)^2 \times 1.17 = 3.674 \text{ m}^2$ (for 1 m diameter equiv.)

$$W = \rho_m \cdot t_{standard} \cdot A_{blank} = 7850 \times 0.018 \times 3.674 \times \pi = 1631.6 \text{ kg}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Crown Radius from the Ellipse Equation

Starting from the ellipse $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$, the meridional radius of curvature at the apex is:

$$R_\phi = \frac{a^2}{b}$$

For a **2:1** head where $2a = D$ and $2b = D/2$:

$$R_\phi = \frac{(D/2)^2}{D/4} = \frac{D^2/4}{D/4} = D$$

This is why the crown radius is exactly equal to the inside diameter for a 2:1 ellipsoidal head.

## 3.3 Derivation of MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)

Starting from $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$, solving algebraically for $P$:

$$t(2SE - 0.2P) = PD \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2SE \cdot t - 0.2P \cdot t = PD$$

$$2SE \cdot t = PD + 0.2P \cdot t = P(D + 0.2t)$$

$$P = \frac{2 S E t}{D + 0.2t} \approx \frac{2 S E t}{D + 0.4t}$$

The factor 0.4 (instead of 0.2) is the ASME code convention for the MAWP formula (it accounts for the inner vs. mid-surface difference on the *thick* side). **Always use 0.4 in MAWP, 0.2 in thickness.**

To help the student compare across head types in the KTU exam, the following Mermaid block diagram highlights the **design formula** associated with each common closure shape (so that 14-mark questions comparing heads can be answered quickly).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Compare ellipsoidal and torispherical heads"*, immediately draw this comparison in your answer script. The **ellipsoidal head has a smoother profile** (no abrupt curvature change at the knuckle), which gives it a **lower stress concentration factor (SCF ≈ 2.0)** than a standard torispherical head (SCF ≈ 2.5 to 3.5). This is why 2:1 ellipsoidal heads are preferred for **cyclic / fatigue-loaded** service.

## 4.4 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head — Cross-Sectional Geometry (Schematic Description)

Since a true 2-D drawing is not possible in Mermaid, the geometry is described in text form below for sketching during the exam:

```
                  ___________
                 /           \        ← CROWN (apex, R_c = D)
                /             \        Maximum membrane stress
               /               \       at this location
              /                 \
             /                   \    ← KNUCKLE region
            /          0.17D       \   Optional radius r = 0.17D
            \                     /
             \                   /    ← STRAIGHT FLANGE (skirt)
              \                 /       Length 1.5t to 3t
               \               /        (weld preparation)
              __\_____________/__
            |   |             |   |    ← CYLINDRICAL SHELL
            |___|             |___|       Wall thickness = t
            D = Inside Diameter
```

The depth from the crown to the tangent line of the cylindrical shell (excluding skirt) is $b = D/4 = 500$ mm for a 2000 mm diameter vessel. With skirt, the total head depth is $D/4 + h_{skirt}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define an Elliptical Head. Why is the 2:1 ratio considered the standard proportion for pressure vessel heads?**

**Model Answer:**

An elliptical head is a pressure vessel closure formed by revolving a semi-ellipse about its major axis. In the standard 2:1 proportion, the major axis equals the inside diameter $D$ of the vessel, and the minor axis equals $D/2$, so the depth of the head is $D/4$. The 2:1 ratio is standard because:

(i) It is the **deepest ellipsoidal head** that can be **cold-formed in a single press operation** from a flat circular blank, making it the most economical to manufacture.

(ii) Its **crown radius $R_c = D$** gives it near-spherical strength with significantly less material than a hemisphere.

(iii) The smooth ellipsoidal profile has a **low stress concentration factor** (≈ 2.0) compared to torispherical heads (≈ 2.5 to 3.5), making it preferable for **fatigue and cyclic loading**.

(iv) The head depth of $D/4$ is shallow enough to be practical for transport and installation but deep enough to resist buckling and external pressure.

**Keywords for full marks:** *2:1 ratio, crown radius $R_c = D$, single-press forming, low SCF, ASME UG-32(d).*

---

### **Question 2** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the ASME Section VIII Div. 1 thickness formula for a 2:1 ellipsoidal head. List any four design parameters and their units.**

**Model Answer:**

The required thickness of a 2:1 ellipsoidal head as per ASME Section VIII Div. 1, UG-32(d) is given by:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

The four key design parameters and their SI units are:

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $P$ | Internal design pressure | **MPa** (N/mm²) |
| $D$ | Inside diameter of head | **mm** |
| $S$ | Allowable stress of material at design temperature | **MPa** (N/mm²) |
| $E$ | Joint efficiency (0.70 to 1.00) | **dimensionless** |

The formula assumes the head is integral with the cylindrical shell (or a butt-welded joint with $E = 1.0$ after full radiographic examination), and includes the 0.2P pressure-correction term required for the inner-fibre stress (Lamé solution calibration).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $0.2P$ term** in the denominator — *worth 2 marks lost per question*.
> 2. **Confusing $0.2P$ (in thickness) with $0.4P$ (in MAWP)** — *worth 1 mark*.
> 3. **Not adding the corrosion allowance** to the required thickness — *worth 1 mark*.
> 4. **Using $R_c = D/2$ (mistake for hemisphere) instead of $R_c = D$** — *worth 2 marks*.
> 5. **Forgetting the joint efficiency $E$** when Category B or C welds are present.
> 6. **Failing to round up** to the next standard plate thickness.
> 7. **Not writing the units** in the final answer — *strict 1-mark penalty per KTU norms*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the ASME Section VIII Div. 1 thickness formula $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 0.2P}$ for a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, starting from the membrane equilibrium of a surface of revolution. State clearly the geometric assumption about the crown radius. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pressure vessel has an inside diameter of $D = 1800$ mm and is closed at one end by a 2:1 ellipsoidal head. The vessel is designed for an internal pressure of $P = 1.6$ MPa at a design temperature of 250 °C. The material is SA-516 Grade 70 with allowable stress $S = 124$ MPa at 250 °C. The joint is a fully radiographed butt weld ($E = 1.0$). The corrosion allowance is $CA = 2$ mm. Determine:

- (i) The required head thickness.
- (ii) The nominal thickness after adding corrosion allowance.
- (iii) The standard thickness to be ordered.
- (iv) The Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP) for the chosen thickness.
- (v) The internal volume and approximate weight of the head (take density $\rho_m = 7850$ kg/m³, blank area factor 1.17).

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Equilibrium of a Shell of Revolution (2 marks):**

For an axisymmetric thin shell under internal pressure $P$, the membrane equilibrium equation in the meridional and circumferential directions is:

$$\frac{N_\phi}{R_\phi} + \frac{N_\theta}{R_\theta} = P$$

where $N_\phi$ = meridional force per unit length, $N_\theta$ = circumferential (hoop) force per unit length, $R_\phi$ = meridional radius of curvature, $R_\theta$ = circumferential radius of curvature.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the 2:1 Ellipsoid (2 marks):**

For a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, the major semi-axis $a = D/2$ and the minor semi-axis $b = D/4$. The crown radius of curvature at the apex ($x = 0$ of the ellipse profile) is:

$$R_c = \frac{a^2}{b} = \frac{(D/2)^2}{D/4} = D$$

At the apex, by symmetry, $R_\phi = R_\theta = R_c = D$.

**Step 3 — Stress at the Crown (2 marks):**

Substituting $R_\phi = R_\theta = D$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$\frac{N_\phi + N_\theta}{D} = P \quad\Rightarrow\quad N_\phi + N_\theta = P D$$

By symmetry at the apex, $N_\phi = N_\theta = N$, so $2N = PD$ and the membrane stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{N}{t} = \frac{P D}{2 t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Allowable Stress and Pressure Correction (1 mark):**

Setting $\sigma \le S E$ and applying the ASME pressure-correction term (from the Lamé solution) for inner-fibre stress gives:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$$

This completes the derivation.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $D = 1800$ mm, $P = 1.6$ MPa, $S = 124$ MPa, $E = 1.0$, $CA = 2$ mm.

**(i) Required thickness (1 mark):**

$$t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} = \frac{(1.6)(1800)}{2(124)(1.0) - 0.2(1.6)} = \frac{2880}{248 - 0.32} = \frac{2880}{247.68}$$

$$t_{req} = 11.629 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Nominal thickness (1 mark):**

$$t_{nom} = t_{req} + CA = 11.629 + 2.0 = 13.629 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Standard thickness (1 mark):**

Next standard size above 13.629 mm in IS 2062 plate schedule is $t_{std} = 16$ mm. **Order 16 mm plate.**

**(iv) MAWP for 16 mm head (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 \, t_{std} \, S \, E}{D + 0.4 \, t_{std}} = \frac{2(16)(124)(1.0)}{1800 + 0.4(16)} = \frac{3968}{1806.4}$$

$$P_{max} = 2.197 \text{ MPa} > 1.6 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Safe)}$$

**(v) Volume and Weight (2 marks):**

Volume of 2:1 ellipsoidal head (ideal, no skirt):

$$V = \frac{\pi D^3}{24} = \frac{\pi (1800)^3}{24} = \frac{\pi \times 5.832 \times 10^9}{24} = 7.634 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3 = 0.7634 \text{ m}^3$$

Blank area (with 17% trim factor):

$$A_{blank} = 1.17 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 1.17 \times \frac{\pi (1.8)^2}{4} = 1.17 \times 2.545 = 2.977 \text{ m}^2$$

Weight of formed head:

$$W = \rho_m \times t_{std} \times A_{blank} = 7850 \times 0.016 \times 2.977 = 373.8 \text{ kg}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare 2:1 ellipsoidal heads and standard torispherical heads on the basis of (i) profile geometry, (ii) crown radius, (iii) thickness formula, (iv) depth, and (v) stress concentration factor. State two specific applications where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel of $D = 1500$ mm is fitted with two 2:1 ellipsoidal heads. Design pressure is $P = 0.8$ MPa. The material is SA-240 Grade 304 stainless steel with allowable stress $S = 115$ MPa at the design temperature of 200 °C. The head-to-shell weld is a Category B butt joint with $E = 0.85$. Corrosion allowance is $CA = 0$ (stainless steel). Calculate:

- (i) Required thickness of each head.
- (ii) Standard plate thickness.
- (iii) MAWP for the standard plate chosen.
- (iv) Total internal volume of the two heads combined.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| # | Parameter | 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head | Torispherical Head |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Profile** | Smooth half-ellipse (single curve) | Crown (spherical) + knuckle (torus) + skirt |
| 2 | **Crown Radius $R_c$** | $D$ (from ellipse geometry) | $L = D$ (typical), but *not* derived from $D$ |
| 3 | **Knuckle Radius $r$** | Optional, typically 0.17 D | Mandatory 0.17 D (standard) |
| 4 | **Depth** | $D/4$ = 0.250 $D$ | ≈ 0.19 $D$ (shallower) |
| 5 | **Thickness Formula** | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$ | $t = \dfrac{0.885 P L}{S E - 0.1 P}$ |
| 6 | **Stress Concentration Factor** | ≈ 2.0 (low) | ≈ 2.5 to 3.5 (high) |
| 7 | **Manufacturing** | Single cold-press | Crown press + knuckle roll |
| 8 | **Cost** | Lower for $D \le 3000$ mm | Higher due to double forming |
| 9 | **Preferred Applications** | Cyclic / fatigue service; refineries, cryogenic tanks | Lower-pressure tanks, atmospheric storage |
| 10 | **Code Reference** | ASME UG-32 (d) | ASME UG-32 (e) |

> **Preferred applications:**
> - **Ellipsoidal:** Refinery reactors, heat-exchanger bonnets, cryogenic vessels (LNG), pharmaceutical reactors — *anywhere* the head undergoes thermal cycling or pressure cycling.
> - **Torispherical:** Atmospheric and low-pressure storage tanks (water, oil, fuel) where cost dominates and cycles are minimal.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $D = 1500$ mm, $P = 0.8$ MPa, $S = 115$ MPa, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 0$.

**(i) Required thickness (2 marks):**

$$t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} = \frac{(0.8)(1500)}{2(115)(0.85) - 0.2(0.8)} = \frac{1200}{195.5 - 0.16} = \frac{1200}{195.34}$$

$$t_{req} = 6.143 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Standard thickness (1 mark):**

Next standard size above 6.143 mm in SA-240 plate schedule is $t_{std} = 8$ mm. **Order 8 mm plate.**

**(iii) MAWP (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 t_{std} S E}{D + 0.4 t_{std}} = \frac{2(8)(115)(0.85)}{1500 + 0.4(8)} = \frac{1564}{1503.2} = 1.040 \text{ MPa}$$

$P_{max} = 1.040$ MPa $> 0.8$ MPa $\checkmark$ **Safe** with 30% margin.

**(iv) Total volume of two heads (2 marks):**

Volume of one head:

$$V_1 = \frac{\pi D^3}{24} = \frac{\pi (1500)^3}{24} = \frac{\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^9}{24} = 4.418 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3 = 0.4418 \text{ m}^3$$

Total volume of two heads:

$$V_{total} = 2 \times 0.4418 = 0.8836 \text{ m}^3 = 883.6 \text{ litres}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Elliptical Head: 10-Point Rapid Revision**

1. **2:1 ellipsoidal head** = standard ellipsoidal head with major axis = $D$ and minor axis = $D/2$. Depth = $D/4$.

2. **Crown radius $R_c = D$** (NOT $D/2$ — that's a hemisphere). Memorise: $R_c = a^2/b = D$.

3. **ASME UG-32(d) thickness formula:**

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} \quad \text{with the } 0.2P \text{ term MANDATORY.}$$

4. **MAWP formula** uses $0.4P$ in the denominator:

$$P_{max} = \frac{2 t S E}{D + 0.4 t}$$

5. **Always add corrosion allowance** $CA$ to the required thickness before rounding up to standard plate size.

6. **Round UP** to the next standard plate thickness — never round down (it would be unsafe).

7. **Joint efficiency $E$** depends on weld category: $E = 1.0$ for full-radiography Category A butt welds; $E = 0.85$ for spot-radiography; $E = 0.70$ for Category C (no radiography).

8. **Volume formula** (no skirt): $V = \pi D^3 / 24$. For weight, use $W = \rho_m \cdot t \cdot A_{blank}$ with $A_{blank} \approx 1.17 \times \pi D^2/4$.

9. **Why 2:1 and not other ratios?** Because it is the deepest ellipsoid that can be cold-formed in a *single press operation*, minimising manufacturing cost while maximising structural efficiency.

10. **Comparison hierarchy (strongest to weakest):** Sphere > 2:1 Ellipsoid > Torispherical > Cone > Flat plate. Ellipsoidal heads carry about 2× the pressure of a flat plate at the same thickness.

> **Final KTU Mantra for this topic:** *"2:1, Rc = D, t = PD/(2SE − 0.2P), never forget the 0.2, always add CA, always round up, always check MAWP."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation of Corresponding ASME Section 8 Codes for Membrane Stress Equations. Discontinuity Stress Analysis in Pressure Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of Corresponding ASME Section 8 Codes for Membrane Stress Equations. Discontinuity Stress Analysis in Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Section VIII** is the universally accepted code for the design, fabrication, inspection, and certification of unfired pressure vessels. It is administered by the **American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)** and is divided into three subdivisions:

- **Division 1 (Div-1):** Provides rules based on **prescriptive (rule-based) design** with higher safety factors. It covers vessels operating up to approximately **3000 psi (≈ 207 bar)**.
- **Division 2 (Div-2):** Provides **alternative rules** based on **analytical/design-by-analysis** methods with lower safety factors, applicable for higher pressures and temperatures.
- **Division 3 (Div-3):** Alternative rules for **high-pressure vessels** above 10,000 psi.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET862), **Section VIII Division 1 (mandatory)** and **Division 2 (design-by-analysis)** are the principal codes. Memorise the three-fold safety factor philosophy and the difference between **stress categories**.

### 1.2 Membrane Stress — Definition

**Membrane stress** is the component of stress that acts **uniformly through the thickness** of a shell, equibiaxial in nature (averaged across the wall). It is the stress that would exist in an **idealised thin shell** of negligible flexural rigidity. The classification per **ASME Section VIII, Div 2, Part 5 (Design by Analysis)** is the foundation of all modern vessel evaluation.

### 1.3 Discontinuity Stress — Definition

A **discontinuity** is a sudden change in the geometry, material, or loading of a shell (e.g., cylinder-to-cone junction, head-to-shell transition, nozzle insertion). Because the strain field is incompatible across such a junction, **secondary (self-equilibrating) bending stresses** arise near the junction. These are termed **discontinuity stresses** and are classified as **secondary stresses (Q)** in the ASME code.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the straw in a glass of water:**
> - Imagine a thin **paper straw** (cylindrical shell) dipped in water. The internal pressure puffs it up uniformly — the entire wall stretches — this is **membrane stress**.
> - Now take a **rigid bottle cap** (torispherical head) and screw it on the straw. The cap does not stretch the same way as the straw. At the joint, the cap "bends" the straw slightly inward or outward — this local bending is the **discontinuity stress**.
> - The **ASME code** is like the rule book a manufacturer consults to ensure neither the uniform puffing nor the local bending causes the straw (vessel) to fail.

Another analogy: **a balloon with a knot** — the membrane stress in the balloon is the tension in the rubber; the knot creates a localized concentration (discontinuity).

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Design pressure | P | MPa or psi |
| Inside radius | R | mm or in |
| Wall thickness | t | mm or in |
| Joint efficiency | E | 0.7 – 1.0 (Div-1) |
| Safety factor on S_y | n_y | **3.5** (Div-1 membrane) |
| Safety factor on S_u | n_u | **3.0** (Div-1 membrane) |
| Poisson's ratio (steel) | ν | **0.3** |
| Modulus of elasticity (carbon steel) | E | **2.0 × 10⁵ MPa** |
| Code stress limit (Div-1) | S_m | min(S_y/3.5, S_u/3) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Flow of ASME Code Selection

The designer follows this decision hierarchy:

1. **Determine service conditions** → Design pressure (P), Design temperature (T), corrosion allowance (c).
2. **Select material** → Use ASME **Section II Part A (Ferrous)** & **Part B (Non-Ferrous)** to obtain S_y and S_u.
3. **Compute allowable stress (S_m)** → Per **Appendix A of Div-1** or **Table 5-1 of Div-2**.
4. **Calculate required thickness** → Use membrane stress formulas in **UG-27 (cylinders)**, **UG-32 (heads)**.
5. **Check stress categories** → Per **Div-2, Part 5 (5.2.1 – 5.2.6)** — limits on P_m, P_L, P_b, Q, and F.
6. **Evaluate discontinuities** → Nozzle reinforcement (Div-1 UG-37, Div-2 Part 6), or full elastic analysis (FEA) for Div-2.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Code Provision

- **Higher safety factor in Div-1 (3.5)** → Compensates for approximations in the simple membrane equations (no bending, no discontinuity, no thermal gradient).
- **Lower safety factor in Div-2 (1.5 on 2/3 S_y or S_u/3)** → Permitted because the design-by-analysis route explicitly accounts for all stress categories including secondary stresses.
- **Joint efficiency E** → Reflects radiographic examination quality (100% for full radiography → E = 1.0; spot radiography → E = 0.85; no radiography → E = 0.70).
- **Stress categorisation** → Different stress types have different failure potential; therefore, different allowable limits are mandated.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU university exam numerical problems. All standard membrane stress equations are collated.

| Component | Membrane Stress Equation (Thin-Wall) | Applicable Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical shell — Hoop** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pR}{t} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | Div-1: UG-27(c)(1) |
| **Cylindrical shell — Longitudinal** | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{pR}{2t} = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | Div-1: UG-27(c)(1) |
| **Spherical shell** | $\sigma = \dfrac{pR}{2t} = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | Div-1: UG-27(d) |
| **Conical shell — Hoop** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t\cos\alpha}$ | Div-1: UG-32(g) |
| **Conical shell — Meridional** | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{pD}{4t\cos\alpha}$ | Div-1: UG-32(g) |
| **2:1 Ellipsoidal head (crown)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | Div-1: UG-32(d) |
| **2:1 Ellipsoidal head (knuckle)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{m}\right)$ | m = 2; simplifies to pD/(4t) |
| **Torispherical head (crown)** | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} + \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{2DR_i + 0.5L^2}}\right]$ | Div-1: UG-32(e) |
| **Required thickness (cylinder)** | $t = \dfrac{pR}{SE - 0.6p}$ | Div-1: UG-27(c)(1) |
| **Required thickness (sphere)** | $t = \dfrac{pR}{2SE - 0.2p}$ | Div-1: UG-27(d) |
| **Allowable stress Div-1 (carbon/low-alloy steel)** | $S_m = \min\left(\dfrac{S_y}{3.5}, \dfrac{S_u}{3}\right)$ | Div-1 Appendix A |
| **Allowable stress Div-2 (membrane)** | $S_m = \min\left(\dfrac{2}{3}S_y, \dfrac{S_u}{3}\right)$ | Div-2: 4.3.11 |
| **Allowable stress Div-2 (membrane + bending)** | $S_{m+b} = \min\left(S_y, \dfrac{1.5 S_u}{3}\right) = 1.5 S_m$ | Div-2: 5.5.6 |
| **Decay constant (cylinder)** | $\beta = \left[\dfrac{3(1-\nu^2)}{R^2t^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | Lame' / Bijlaard |
| **Edge moment (cylinder-head, internal pressure)** | $M_0 = -pR^2\left[\beta\, \text{(geometric term)}\right]$ | Discontinuity theory |

> **Notation:** p = internal pressure, R = inside radius, D = inside diameter, t = required wall thickness, S = allowable stress, E = joint efficiency, α = cone half-apex angle, L = crown radius of torisphere, R_i = knuckle radius.

## 2.4 Stress Categories — Allowable Limits (Div-2, Part 5)

| Stress Type | Symbol | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| General primary membrane | $P_m$ | $\le S_m$ |
| Local primary membrane | $P_L$ | $\le 1.5\, S_m$ |
| Primary bending | $P_b$ | $\le 1.5\, S_m$ |
| Primary membrane + bending | $P_m + P_b$ | $\le 1.5\, S_m$ |
| Secondary (self-equilibrating) | $Q$ | $\le 3\, S_m$ |
| Peak | $F$ | $\le S_a$ (fatigue) — $S_{a}$ from Fig. 5-110.1 |

**Why these limits?** Primary stresses do not redistribute with yielding — they cause gross distortion (catastrophic). Secondary stresses self-equilibrate and are tolerated up to 3 S_m (yield redistribution permitted).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder (ASME Membrane Equation)

### Step 1 — Free Body of a Cylinder Element

Consider a cylindrical shell of inside radius R, wall thickness t, subjected to internal pressure p. Slice a longitudinal section of unit length, subtending an angle 2θ at the centre.

Forces acting on the element:
- **Pressure on projected area** = $p \cdot 2R\theta \cdot 1$ (per unit length)
- **Hoop stress resultant** = $\sigma_h \cdot t \cdot 1$ acting on two cut faces of length ≈ 1 each
- **Hoop equilibrium:** The two hoop force components balance the projected pressure force.

### Step 2 — Hoop (Circumferential) Stress Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \sigma_h t (1) \sin\theta &= p (2R\theta)(1) \\
