\sigma_h t \cdot 2\theta &= p R \cdot 2\theta \quad \text{(for small } \theta, \sin\theta \approx \theta\text{)} \\
\sigma_h &= \frac{pR}{t} = \frac{pD}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The projected area on the diametrical plane is $2R\theta \times 1$, and the force component of $\sigma_h t$ in the vertical direction is $\sigma_h t \sin\theta$. Equating pressure force to membrane force yields the hoop stress.

### Step 3 — Longitudinal (Axial) Stress Derivation

Cut the cylinder transversely. The end cap force on projected area is $p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$. This is balanced by the longitudinal stress resultant on the cylindrical wall area $\sigma_L \cdot t \cdot \pi D$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L \cdot t \cdot \pi D &= p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
\sigma_L &= \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{pR}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Equilibrium of axial forces on a cross-section gives longitudinal stress, which is exactly **half the hoop stress**.

### Step 4 — Required Thickness (Div-1, UG-27)

The code provides a thickness equation that includes the **pressure term on the right-hand side** (a small correction for thick walls) and the **joint efficiency E**:

$$
t = \frac{pR}{SE - 0.6p}
$$

Derivation from $\sigma_h = pR/t$ with design margin:
- Design stress = SE (allowable stress times efficiency)
- $\sigma_h = pR/t \le SE \Rightarrow t \ge pR/SE$
- The $-0.6p$ term accounts for the **Hoop stress in a thick cylinder** (Lamé's equation first-order correction).

### Step 5 — Verification with Lamé's Equation (Thick Cylinder)

For a thick cylinder (t/R > 0.1), Lamé's equations give:
- Hoop stress at inside surface: $\sigma_i = p \dfrac{R_o^2 + R_i^2}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$
- Hoop stress at outside surface: $\sigma_o = p \dfrac{2R_i^2}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$

The **mean hoop stress** (which is what membrane stress represents) is:
$$
\sigma_{mean} = \frac{pR_i}{t}\left(1 - \frac{t}{2R_i}\right)
$$

For t/R_i ≤ 0.05, this reduces to the thin-wall formula.

## 3.2 Derivation: Discontinuity Stress at a Cylinder–Head Junction

### Step 1 — Kinematic Incompatibility

Consider a cylindrical shell of radius R and a 2:1 ellipsoidal head welded to it. Under internal pressure, the **free (unrestrained) radial growths** at the junction are:
- Cylinder: $\delta_{cyl} = \dfrac{pR^2}{Et}(1 - \nu/2)$
- Ellipsoidal head (at the junction where radius of curvature = R): $\delta_{head} = \dfrac{pR^2}{2Et}(1 - \nu)$

### Step 2 — Incompatibility

These two displacements are not equal. Their difference $\Delta$ must be absorbed by **edge forces (P_o)** and **edge moments (M_o)** at the junction.

$$
\Delta = \delta_{cyl} - \delta_{head} = \frac{pR^2}{2Et}(1 - \nu/2)
$$

### Step 3 — Solution Using Beam-on-Elastic-Foundation Analogy

The cylinder behaves like a semi-infinite beam (Winkler foundation with stiffness $k = \dfrac{Et}{R^2}$). The edge loads $M_0$ and $P_0$ decay with distance x from the junction.

The **decay constant** $\beta$ is:

$$
\beta = \left[\frac{3(1-\nu^2)}{R^2 t^2}\right]^{1/4}
$$

The displacement, rotation, moment, and shear along the cylinder are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(x) &= e^{-\beta x}\left(A \cos\beta x + B \sin\beta x\right) \\
\phi(x) &= \beta e^{-\beta x}\left[(B-A)\cos\beta x - (A+B)\sin\beta x\right] \\
M(x) &= -D \dfrac{d^2\delta}{dx^2} \\
V(x) &= -D \dfrac{d^3\delta}{dx^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

where D is the flexural rigidity of the shell.

### Step 4 — Edge Moment for Cylinder–2:1 Ellipsoidal Head

For an internal pressure p on a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, the edge moment at the cylinder-head junction is given by (per Zick & Watkins, also in Megyesy):

$$
M_0 = -pR^2 \left[\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}\right] \cdot (\text{geometric factor})
$$

For a **flat head** welded to a cylinder (stiff head), the moment is approximately:

$$
M_0 \approx -0.5\, pR^2
$$

The **maximum bending stress** at the junction on the cylinder wall is:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{6 M_0}{t^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Decay of Bending Stress

The bending stress decays exponentially:
$$
\sigma_b(x) = \sigma_{b,0} \, e^{-\beta x} \left(\cos\beta x + \sin\beta x\right)
$$

Within a distance of approximately $\pi/\beta$, the discontinuity stress is **essentially zero** — this is why junction effects are localized.

## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Junction Type | Pressure-induced M_0 (sign convention) | Decay Length ≈ $\pi/\beta$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cylinder – 2:1 Ellipsoidal head | M_0 < 0 (compressive at cylinder OD) | $\pi/\beta$ |
| Cylinder – flat head | M_0 ≈ -0.5 pR² (large bending) | Short (stiff) |
| Cylinder – torispherical head | Function of L/R ratio | Moderate |
| Cylinder – cone (small α) | M_0 ≈ -0.05 pR² | Long |
| Sphere – cylindrical nozzle | M_0 high; reinforcement needed | Very short |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ASME Code Selection

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision tree** that a designer follows when selecting between Div-1 and Div-2, and the analytical route thereafter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Pressure Vessel] --> B{Service Conditions Defined?}
    B -- No --> B1[Define P, T, Corrosion Allowance]
    B1 --> B
    B -- Yes --> C[Select Material from ASME Section II]
    C --> D{Determine Allowable Stress Sm}
    D -- Div 1 --> D1[Sm = min Sy/3.5, Su/3]
    D -- Div 2 --> D2[Sm = min 2Sy/3, Su/3]
    D1 --> E{Calculate Required Thickness t}
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Membrane Stress Equations]
    F --> G{Discontinuities Present?}
    G -- No --> H[Check Code Compliance]
    G -- Yes --> I[Compute Edge Loads M0 and P0]
    I --> J[Apply Beam-on-Elastic-Foundation Solution]
    J --> K[Decay Function e to power negative beta x]
    K --> H
    H --> L{Design by Analysis Required?}
    L -- No --> M[Use Div 1 Rule-Based]
    L -- Yes --> N[Use Div 2 Part 5 Stress Categorisation]
    N --> O[Check Pm, PL, Pb, Q, F Limits]
    O --> P[Final Design Approved]
```

## 4.2 Stress Categorisation Hierarchy (ASME Div-2, Part 5.2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Stress in Vessel Wall] --> B[Primary Stress P]
    A --> C[Secondary Stress Q]
    A --> D[Peak Stress F]
    B --> B1[General Membrane Pm - uniform - limit Sm]
    B --> B2[Local Membrane PL - within sqrt R t - limit 1.5 Sm]
    B --> B3[Bending Pb - due to pressure on non-thin region - limit 1.5 Sm]
    C --> C1[Self Equilibrating - includes discontinuity and thermal - limit 3 Sm]
    D --> D1[Localised - fatigue governed - limit Sa from Fig 5-110.1]
```

## 4.3 Cylinder–Head Junction — Force Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internal Pressure p] --> B[Cylindrical Shell Expansion delta_cyl]
    A --> C[Ellipsoidal Head Expansion delta_head]
    B --> D{Incompatibility Delta}
    C --> D
    D --> E[Edge Moment M0 and Shear P0 at Junction]
    E --> F[Bending Stress sigma_b at Junction]
    F --> G[Decay along length e to power negative beta x times cos and sin]
    G --> H[Negligible Beyond pi over beta]
```

## 4.4 Membrane Stress Distribution in a Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thin Cylindrical Shell Pressure p] --> B[Hoop Stress sigma_h = pR/t]
    A --> C[Longitudinal Stress sigma_L = pR/2t]
    B --> D[Maximum - governs design for cylinders under internal pressure]
    C --> E[Secondary design stress for closed-end vessels]
    D --> F[Code Reference ASME Div 1 UG-27 c 1]
    E --> F
```

> **Diagram note:** The Mermaid flowcharts above map the functional and analytical architecture of the ASME code application. The actual physical stress distribution in a cylinder is **uniform through the wall (membrane)** with **peak discontinuity stresses** localised at the end junctions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between primary membrane stress (P_m) and secondary stress (Q) as classified by ASME Section VIII, Division 2. Why is the allowable limit for Q higher than for P_m?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Primary Membrane Stress (P_m) | Secondary Stress (Q) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Internal/external pressure, mechanical loads | Self-equilibrating thermal or geometric discontinuity |
| Equilibrium with applied load | Yes (must be balanced by external forces) | No (self-equilibrating within the vessel) |
| Redistribution on yielding | No | Yes (local yielding relieves it) |
| Failure potential | Gross plastic deformation / collapse | Fatigue (ratcheting limit applies) |
| Allowable limit | $\le S_m$ | $\le 3 S_m$ |

**Reason for higher Q limit (1 mark):** Because secondary stresses are **self-equilibrating**, local plastic flow is permitted without leading to overall failure — only a limited amount of distortion occurs. Hence the code permits a 3-fold higher stress limit compared to P_m.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]

**Q: State the membrane stress equations (per ASME Section VIII, Div-1) for (i) a thin cylindrical shell and (ii) a thin spherical shell subjected to internal pressure. Compare their magnitudes and explain why cylindrical shells are designed by hoop stress.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) Cylinder:
$$
\sigma_h = \frac{pR}{t} \quad ; \quad \sigma_L = \frac{pR}{2t}
$$

(ii) Sphere:
$$
\sigma = \frac{pR}{2t}
$$

**Comparison:** The hoop stress in a cylinder is **twice** the longitudinal stress and **twice** the membrane stress in a sphere of the same R, t, and p. The hoop stress is the **maximum** and therefore governs the design. (1 mark each for equations + comparison + reasoning)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A 3 m internal diameter cylindrical pressure vessel of length 8 m is to operate at a design pressure of 2.5 MPa. The material is SA-516 Grade 70 with S_y = 260 MPa, S_u = 485 MPa, E = 200,000 MPa, ν = 0.3. Full radiographic examination is used (E = 1.0). Corrosion allowance c = 1.5 mm.**

**(a)** Calculate the **required wall thickness** of the cylinder per ASME Section VIII, Div-1. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP)** and the **longitudinal stress** for the actual thickness selected. Discuss the **discontinuity stress** expected at the cylinder-head junction. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to A(a): Required Thickness

**Step 1: Compute allowable stress S_m (Div-1, Appendix A).**

$$
S_m = \min\left(\frac{S_y}{3.5}, \frac{S_u}{3}\right) = \min\left(\frac{260}{3.5}, \frac{485}{3}\right) = \min(74.29, 161.67) = 74.29 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Div-1, UG-27(c)(1) formula for cylinder hoop stress.**

$$
t = \frac{pR}{SE - 0.6p}
$$

where R = D/2 = 3.0 / 2 = 1.5 m = 1500 mm, S = 74.29 MPa, E = 1.0, p = 2.5 MPa.

$$
t = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{74.29 \times 1.0 - 0.6 \times 2.5} = \frac{3750}{74.29 - 1.5} = \frac{3750}{72.79} = 51.52 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Add corrosion allowance.**

$$
t_{design} = t + c = 51.52 + 1.5 = 53.02 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Select next standard plate thickness.** Common plate increments are 2 mm or 4 mm. Select **t_actual = 56 mm** (or 54 mm if finer increments are allowed).

**Final required thickness (rounded):** $t_{required} = 53 \text{ mm}$; selected $t = 56 \text{ mm}$. ✓

---

#### Model Solution to A(b): MAWP, Longitudinal Stress, and Discontinuity Discussion

**Step 1: MAWP for the selected thickness (56 mm).**

Rearranging UG-27(c)(1):
$$
p_{max} = \frac{SEt}{R + 0.6t} = \frac{74.29 \times 1.0 \times 56}{1500 + 0.6 \times 56} = \frac{4160.24}{1533.6} = 2.713 \text{ MPa}
$$

(MAWP > design pressure 2.5 MPa → Safe. ✓)

**Step 2: Longitudinal stress for selected t.**

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{pR}{2t} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 56} = \frac{3750}{112} = 33.48 \text{ MPa}
$$

Compared to $S_m = 74.29$ MPa → $\sigma_L / S_m = 0.45$ (within limit).

**Step 3: Discontinuity stress discussion.**

At the cylinder-head junction (assumed 2:1 ellipsoidal head), the **incompatible radial growth** of the cylinder and head produces an **edge moment M_0** and **edge shear V_0** at the junction. The decay constant is:

$$
\beta = \left[\frac{3(1 - 0.3^2)}{1500^2 \times 56^2}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\frac{2.73}{1.0 \times 10^{13}}\right]^{1/4} = (2.73 \times 10^{-13})^{0.25} = 7.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad/mm}
$$

Decay length: $\pi/\beta \approx 4350 \text{ mm} = 4.35 \text{ m}$

**Implication:** The cylinder length is 8 m. Within ≈ 4.35 m from each head, discontinuity bending stresses are present; over the central region, only pure membrane stresses act. The **bending stress** at the junction is:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{6 M_0}{t^2} \approx \frac{6 \times 0.25 \times 2.5 \times 1500^2}{56^2} = \frac{6 \times 0.25 \times 2.5 \times 2.25 \times 10^6}{3136} \approx 2687 \text{ MPa (very high locally)}
$$

This is why the **secondary stress Q (discontinuity) limit is 3 S_m = 222.87 MPa**, not 74.29 MPa. (Note: real M_0 is much smaller; an approximate M_0 ≈ 0.5 pR² × K, where K is a geometric factor < 0.2 for ellipsoidal heads.) The discontinuity stress is **localised** and **self-equilibrating**, so it does not lead to global failure.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **stress categorisation** as per **ASME Section VIII, Div-2, Part 5**. Discuss the limits on P_m, P_L, P_b, Q, and F. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a **2:1 semi-ellipsoidal head** welded to a cylindrical shell of 1.2 m diameter, operating at 1.8 MPa, with S_m = 90 MPa, calculate:
   (i) The **crown membrane stress**.
   (ii) The **knuckle membrane stress**.
   (iii) The **required head thickness** per Div-1, UG-32(d). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to B(a): Stress Categorisation

(See Section 2.4 above for the table. The student should present the **5.2.1 to 5.2.6** classification and the **limits in 5.5.2 to 5.5.6** with examples for each. Key points to write:)

- **P_m** arises from **pressure or mechanical loads**, uniform across a section. Self-equilibrating in the section. **Limit: ≤ S_m**.
- **P_L** is membrane stress averaged over a **local region not exceeding $\sqrt{Rt}$** in any direction. **Limit: ≤ 1.5 S_m** (only when region is < $\sqrt{Rt}$).
- **P_b** is primary bending due to pressure on a **non-circular cross-section** (e.g., flat head). **Limit: ≤ 1.5 S_m**.
- **Q** is secondary stress: **discontinuity** (geometric mismatch) and **thermal gradient**. **Limit: ≤ 3 S_m**.
- **F** is peak stress: local **stress concentration** (notch, weld toe). **Limit: ≤ S_a** (fatigue curve).
- **P_m + P_b ≤ 1.5 S_m** (combined primary limit).
- **P_m + P_b + Q ≤ 3 S_m** (primary + secondary limit — the basis of "level D" service limits in Div-2).

---

#### Model Solution to B(b): Ellipsoidal Head Design

**Given:** D = 1.2 m = 1200 mm, p = 1.8 MPa, S_m = 90 MPa, E = 1.0, m = 2 (2:1 ellipsoidal).

**(i) Crown membrane stress:**

For a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, the maximum stress occurs at the **crown** (top of the head) and is:

$$
\sigma_{crown} = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{2t} = \frac{1080}{t} \text{ MPa (with t in mm)}
$$

(Wait — actually, for 2:1 ellipsoid, crown stress = pD/(2t); knuckle stress = pD/(4t) is wrong. Let me correct:)

For a 2:1 ellipsoidal head with major axis = D, minor axis = D/4 (semi-axes D/2 and D/4):
- **Crown stress** (at top): $\sigma_{crown} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$
- **Knuckle stress** (at junction with cylinder): $\sigma_{knuckle} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{m}\right) = \dfrac{pD}{2t}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$

(With m = major/minor axis ratio for 2:1 head.)

**(ii) Knuckle stress:**

$$
\sigma_{knuckle} = \frac{pD}{4t}
$$

(Substituting numerical values once t is known.)

**(iii) Required thickness (Div-1, UG-32(d)):**

The code formula for an ellipsoidal head is:

$$
t = \frac{pD}{2SE - 0.2p}
$$

$$
t = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{2 \times 90 \times 1.0 - 0.2 \times 1.8} = \frac{2160}{180 - 0.36} = \frac{2160}{179.64} = 12.025 \text{ mm}
$$

Add corrosion allowance c = 1.5 mm:
$$
t_{design} = 12.025 + 1.5 = 13.525 \text{ mm}
$$

**Select t = 14 mm.**

**Crown stress (with t = 14 mm):**
$$
\sigma_{crown} = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{2 \times 14} = \frac{2160}{28} = 77.14 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is less than S_m = 90 MPa ✓ (safe).

**Knuckle stress (with t = 14 mm):**
$$
\sigma_{knuckle} = \frac{1.8 \times 1200}{4 \times 14} = \frac{2160}{56} = 38.57 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The crown is the more critical region (stress ratio 77.14/90 = 0.857 vs. knuckle 38.57/90 = 0.428), and the head is safe per Div-1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing 2:1 ellipsoidal crown formula** with sphere formula. The crown of 2:1 ellipsoid has stress = pD/(2t), **NOT** pD/(4t). The latter is the knuckle (or sphere) formula. Many students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting corrosion allowance** when reporting "required" vs. "actual/design" thickness.
> 3. **Mixing Div-1 and Div-2 allowable stress limits** (3.5 vs. 1.5 / 2/3). Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Sign convention for edge moment M_0** — the sign depends on the type of head (flat, ellipsoidal, torispherical). Always state the assumption.
> 5. **In Part B(a) stress categorisation**, students often omit the **fatigue limit on F (peak stress)**, which is a 1-mark deduction. Always include the S_a reference.
> 6. **Neglecting the -0.6p term** in the thickness formula for cylinders; it appears small but examiners expect it for high-pressure calculations.
> 7. **Decay constant β** in discontinuity analysis is **independent of pressure**; it is a pure geometric/material property.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision bullets for last-minute KTU exam prep:**

### ✅ ASME Section VIII Architecture
- [ ] **Div-1** → Rule-based, **S_m = min(S_y/3.5, S_u/3)**, joint efficiency E explicitly used.
- [ ] **Div-2** → Design-by-analysis, **S_m = min(2 S_y/3, S_u/3)**, no joint efficiency (E = 1.0 implied).
- [ ] **Div-3** → High-pressure vessels (>10,000 psi).
- [ ] Allowable stress lookup → **ASME Section II, Part A (ferrous) or Part B (non-ferrous)**.

### ✅ Membrane Stress Equations (Thin-Wall, t/R ≤ 0.1)
- [ ] **Cylinder hoop:** $\sigma_h = pR/t = pD/(2t)$ ← **governs design**.
- [ ] **Cylinder longitudinal:** $\sigma_L = pR/(2t) = pD/(4t)$.
- [ ] **Sphere:** $\sigma = pR/(2t) = pD/(4t)$.
- [ ] **Cone hoop:** $\sigma_h = pD/(2t\cos\alpha)$.
- [ ] **Cone meridional:** $\sigma_m = pD/(4t\cos\alpha)$.
- [ ] **Ellipsoidal crown (2:1):** $\sigma = pD/(2t)$.
- [ ] **Ellipsoidal knuckle (2:1):** $\sigma = pD/(4t)$ ← half of crown.
- [ ] **Torispherical crown:** complex formula involving L, R_i (refer to UG-32(e)).

### ✅ Div-1 Thickness Formulas
- [ ] **Cylinder (UG-27):** $t = pR / (SE - 0.6p)$.
- [ ] **Sphere (UG-27(d)):** $t = pR / (2SE - 0.2p)$.
- [ ] **Ellipsoidal head (UG-32(d)):** $t = pD / (2SE - 0.2p)$.
- [ ] **Conical shell (UG-32(g)):** $t = pD / (2\cos\alpha)(SE - 0.6p)$.
- [ ] **Flat head (UG-34):** $t = D \sqrt{Kp/(SE)}$ — K is a tabulated factor (0.25 to 0.33).

### ✅ Stress Categories and Limits (Div-2, Part 5)
- [ ] **P_m ≤ S_m** (primary membrane).
- [ ] **P_L ≤ 1.5 S_m** (local membrane, area < $\sqrt{Rt}$).
- [ ] **P_b ≤ 1.5 S_m** (primary bending).
- [ ] **P_m + P_b ≤ 1.5 S_m**.
- [ ] **Q ≤ 3 S_m** (secondary — discontinuity + thermal).
- [ ] **P_m + P_b + Q ≤ 3 S_m**.
- [ ] **F ≤ S_a** (peak — fatigue governed, Fig. 5-110.1).

### ✅ Discontinuity Stress Essentials
- [ ] Discontinuity arises at **geometric mismatch** (cylinder-head, nozzle-shell, cone-cylinder).
- [ ] Solution uses **beam-on-elastic-foundation** (Winkler model).
- [ ] **Decay constant:** $\beta = [3(1-\nu^2)/(R^2 t^2)]^{1/4}$ for cylinders.
- [ ] **Decay length** ≈ $\pi/\beta$; beyond this, stress is negligible.
- [ ] **Edge moment M_0** and **edge shear V_0** are the two unknowns.
- [ ] For 2:1 ellipsoidal head on cylinder, M_0 < 0 (tends to bend cylinder outward at OD).
- [ ] For flat head, M_0 is **largest** → highest discontinuity stress.
- [ ] For torispherical head, M_0 depends on **L/R ratio**.

### ✅ Exam-Writing Tips for KTU
- [ ] **Always** state the code reference (e.g., "per ASME Sec VIII, Div-1, UG-27(c)(1)").
- [ ] **Always** include units in every step of the numerical solution.
- [ ] **Always** compute S_m and show the **min(...) selection** explicitly.
- [ ] **Always** add corrosion allowance to the calculated thickness.
- [ ] **Always** verify the design by computing the **actual stress** with the selected thickness.
- [ ] For Part B (14-mark) questions, structure the answer into: (i) Given data, (ii) Code reference, (iii) Formula, (iv) Substitution with units, (v) Result with comment, (vi) Conclusion/Safety verification.

### ✅ Common Numerical Values to Memorise
- [ ] **Safety factors:** 3.5 (S_y Div-1), 3.0 (S_u Div-1), 1.5 (S_y Div-2), 3.0 (S_u Div-2).
- [ ] **Poisson's ratio** for steel: 0.3.
- [ ] **Modulus of elasticity** for carbon steel: 2 × 10⁵ MPa.
- [ ] **Joint efficiencies:** 1.0 (100% radiography), 0.85 (spot), 0.70 (no radiography).
- [ ] **Standard corrosion allowance:** 1.5 mm (typical); 3 mm for severe service.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stresses in Thick Walled Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stresses in Thick Walled Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **thick-walled cylinder** is a cylindrical pressure vessel in which the wall thickness is comparable to (or greater than) one-twentieth of the internal diameter, i.e., the geometric ratio satisfies

$$\frac{t}{D_i} \;>\; \frac{1}{20}$$

Under this condition, the stress is **no longer uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness. The radial and hoop (circumferential/tangential) stresses become functions of the radial coordinate $r$, and their variation must be derived from the classical theory of elasticity — most commonly using **Lamé's Equations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** When $t/D_i \le 1/20$, the cylinder is classified as *thin-walled* and treated with the simple membrane-stress formula. When $t/D_i > 1/20$, the **thick-cylinder formulation (Lamé)** must be applied without exception.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Onion Shell"**
> Imagine slicing an onion radially. Each ring experiences a different squish — the innermost ring is crushed hard by the rings around it, while the outermost shell only carries its own weight. Similarly, inside a pressurized thick cylinder, the **inner fiber** is squeezed most by pressure and the hoop stress there is the largest. As you move outward, each successive fiber carries less load. Lamé's equations quantify exactly how this "load-sharing" decays with radius.

In contrast, a thin-walled cylinder is like a single sheet of paper stretched over a balloon — the load is shared roughly equally, so we treat it as constant.

## 1.3 Why Thin-Wall Theory Fails for Thick Walls

| Aspect | Thin Cylinder | Thick Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Stress through thickness | Assumed **uniform** | **Varies** with radius $r$ |
| Governing theory | Membrane equilibrium | Lamé / Elasticity |
| Applicable ratio | $t/D_i \le 1/20$ | $t/D_i > 1/20$ |
| Radial stress magnitude | Neglected | Considered fully |
| Examples | LPG gas cylinders, small tanks | Hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, high-pressure boilers, reactor shells |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Internal pressure:** $p_i$ — typically **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **External pressure:** $p_o$ — usually **0 MPa** for atmospheric exposure.
- **Inner radius:** $r_i$ — measured in **mm**.
- **Outer radius:** $r_o = r_i + t$ — measured in **mm**.
- **Allowable stress:** $\sigma_t$ or $S$ — derived from **ultimate tensile strength** divided by a **factor of safety**.
- **Poisson's ratio:** $\nu$ (typically **0.3** for steels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lamé's Governing Equations — Step-by-Step Logic

The radial stress $\sigma_r$ and hoop stress $\sigma_h$ at any radius $r$ in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$ are given by:

### 2.1.1 Lamé's Constants (A and B)

Consider an elemental ring at radius $r$. Force equilibrium + plane-strain elasticity yields two constants:

$$A = \frac{p_i \, r_i^{2} \;-\; p_o \, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} \;-\; r_i^{2}}$$

$$B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) \, r_i^{2} \, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} \;-\; r_i^{2}}$$

### 2.1.2 The Two Stress Components

$$\sigma_r(r) = A \;-\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_h(r) = A \;+\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These two equations drive every ASME Section VIII, IS 2825, and high-pressure vessel design calculation. The maximum hoop stress dictates the wall thickness required to prevent yielding at the bore.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation — Master These Before Solving Any Numerical.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Constant $A$ | $A = \dfrac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ | MPa | — |
| 2 | Constant $B$ | $B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ | MPa·mm² | — |
| 3 | Radial stress | $\sigma_r(r) = A - \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | MPa | at $r_i$: $\sigma_r = -p_i$ |
| 4 | Hoop stress | $\sigma_h(r) = A + \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | MPa | at $r_o$: $\sigma_r = -p_o$ |
| 5 | Maximum hoop stress (closed ends case) | $\sigma_{h,\max} = \dfrac{p_i (r_o^{2}+r_i^{2}) - 2 p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2}-r_i^{2}}$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 6 | Radial stress at bore | $\sigma_{r,\min} = -p_i$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 7 | Maximum shear stress (Lame) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2}\Big\vert_{\max}$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 8 | Effective stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_{\text{eff}} = \sigma_h - \sigma_r$ | MPa | at $r = r_i$ |
| 9 | Cylinder "thick" condition | $t/D_i > 1/20$ | — | — |
| 10 | Ratio simplification (only $p_o = 0$) | $K = r_o/r_i$ | — | design parameter |

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Physical Interpretation

1. **At inner surface $r = r_i$:** $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive, equals internal pressure).
2. **At outer surface $r = r_o$:** $\sigma_r = -p_o$ (if exposed to atmosphere, $p_o = 0$).
3. **Hoop stress is maximum at $r = r_i$**, regardless of whether $p_o = 0$ or $p_o > 0$.
4. **Hoop stress is minimum at $r = r_o$**.
5. **Radial stress goes from $-p_i$ (compressive) at bore to $-p_o$ (or $0$) at outer surface.**

## 2.4 Stress Distribution Across Wall (Visual Trend)

- The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ curve is **hyperbolic**, decaying outward.
- The radial stress $\sigma_r$ curve is **compressive throughout**, rising (becoming less negative) outward.
- The maximum stress differential (and therefore maximum shear) is always **at the inner bore**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Hydraulic presses and accumulators** — must withstand very high internal pressures (often > 100 MPa).
> - **Gun barrels and cannon tubes** — thick cylinder analysis is critical for autofrettage design.
> - **High-pressure reactor shells** in petrochemical plants — designed using ASME code which references Lamé's equations directly.
> - **Nuclear pressure vessels** — radial stress variation governs the thermal-stress superposition.

## 2.5 Common Derivations Required by KTU Examiners

1. Derivation of **Lamé's equations** from an elemental ring.
2. Derivation of expressions for **maximum hoop stress, minimum hoop stress, and maximum shear stress**.
3. Design for internal pressure given allowable stress: rearrange the $\sigma_h$ equation to solve for $r_o / r_i$.
4. Compound cylinder (shrink-fit / autofrettage) — add thermal/initial fit terms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lamé's Equations (Closed-End Cylinder)

### 3.1.1 Free Body — Elemental Ring

Consider an elemental ring of unit length (or unit axial length), located at radial coordinate $r$, of thickness $dr$, subtending angle $d\theta$.

Forces acting on this element:
- Radial stress $\sigma_r$ on the inner face of area $r \, d\theta \cdot 1$ acting outward.
- Radial stress $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)$ on the outer face of area $(r+dr)\, d\theta \cdot 1$ acting inward.
- Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ on the two side faces of area $dr \cdot 1$ acting tangentially inward, with pressure $p$ acting radially.

### 3.1.2 Force Equilibrium in the Radial Direction

Summing forces in the radial direction (outward positive):

$$\sigma_r \cdot r \, d\theta \;-\; (\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r+dr)\, d\theta \;+\; 2 \sigma_h \, dr \sin\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) = 0$$

For small angles, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$, and neglecting higher-order differentials $(dr \cdot d\sigma_r)$:

$$-d\sigma_r \cdot r \;-\; \sigma_r \, dr \;-\; d\sigma_r \, dr \;+\; \sigma_h \, dr = 0$$

$$-r \, d\sigma_r - \sigma_r \, dr + \sigma_h \, dr = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $dr$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} \;+\; \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_h}{r} = 0\;}$$

### 3.1.3 Integration — Total Radial Force on a Half-Cylinder

Consider a half-cylinder (cut through a diametrical plane). Total radial force on the curved surface at any radius $r$ must equal the force on the projected area:

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} \sigma_r \cdot r \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta \, d\theta \;=\; -p_i \, r_i \, L$$

Carrying out the integration (and similarly for $p_o$ at $r_o$):

$$2 \sigma_r \, r \, L \;=\; -2 p_i r_i L \quad \text{at } r_i$$
$$2 \sigma_r \, r \, L \;=\; -2 p_o r_o L \quad \text{at } r_o$$

Combining these via superposition yields:

$$\sigma_r \;=\; \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \;-\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2}\, r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\, r^{2}}$$

Let $A$ and $B$ denote the constants. We obtain the **first Lamé equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_r(r) \;=\; A \;-\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}\;}$$

where

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2}\, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

Substituting into the equilibrium equation and integrating:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_h(r) \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}\;}$$

This is the **second Lamé equation**.

## 3.2 Special Case: External Pressure = 0 ($p_o = 0$)

With $p_o = 0$:

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_r(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 - \frac{r_o^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]$$

$$\sigma_h(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 + \frac{r_o^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]$$

### 3.2.1 Maximum Hoop Stress (at $r = r_i$)

$$\sigma_{h,\max} = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 + 1\right] = p_i \, \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

### 3.2.2 Minimum Hoop Stress (at $r = r_o$)

$$\sigma_{h,\min} = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left[1 + 1\right] = \frac{2 p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

### 3.2.3 Maximum Shear Stress (at $r = r_i$)

At the bore, $\sigma_r = -p_i$, hence:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{h,\max} - \sigma_{r,\min}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[p_i \, \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + p_i\right]$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{\max} = \frac{p_i \, r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}\;}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For Pattern Reference)

**Problem:** A steel cylinder has $r_i = 50$ mm, $r_o = 100$ mm, and carries an internal pressure $p_i = 80$ MPa with $p_o = 0$. Find (i) hoop stress at $r = 50$ mm and $r = 75$ mm, (ii) radial stress at those radii, (iii) maximum shear stress.

**Step 1 — Compute $A$ and $B$:**

$$A = \frac{80 \times 50^{2}}{100^{2} - 50^{2}} = \frac{80 \times 2500}{7500} = 26.667 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{80 \times 50^{2} \times 100^{2}}{100^{2} - 50^{2}} = \frac{80 \times 2500 \times 10000}{7500} = 266{,}667 \text{ MPa·mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — At $r = 50$ mm:**

$$\sigma_r = 26.667 - \frac{266{,}667}{50^{2}} = 26.667 - 106.667 = -80.0 \text{ MPa} \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\sigma_h = 26.667 + \frac{266{,}667}{50^{2}} = 26.667 + 106.667 = 133.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — At $r = 75$ mm:**

$$\sigma_r = 26.667 - \frac{266{,}667}{75^{2}} = 26.667 - 47.41 = -20.74 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_h = 26.667 + \frac{266{,}667}{75^{2}} = 26.667 + 47.41 = 74.07 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear stress at $r = r_i$:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2} = \frac{133.33 - (-80)}{2} = 106.67 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Alert:** Notice the hoop stress at the bore (133.33 MPa) is significantly higher than the thin-wall estimate would give ($\sigma_h = pr/t = 80 \times 50 / 50 = 80$ MPa). This is exactly why Lamé's analysis is mandatory for thick cylinders.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lamé's Stress Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressure Vessel Geometry Input] --> B{Is t/Di greater than 1 by 20?}
    B -- No --> C[Apply Thin Wall Theory Membrane Stresses]
    B -- Yes --> D[Activate Lame Analysis Module]
    D --> E[Identify pi and po]
    D --> F[Identify ri and ro]
    E --> G[Compute Constant A]
    F --> G
    E --> H[Compute Constant B]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Compute Sigma r at any r]
    H --> I
    G --> J[Compute Sigma h at any r]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Evaluate at inner radius ri]
    J --> K
    I --> L[Evaluate at outer radius ro]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Maximum Hoop Stress Check]
    L --> N[Minimum Hoop Stress Check]
    K --> O[Maximum Shear Stress at Bore]
    M --> P[Compare with Allowable Stress]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P -- Safe --> Q[Design Accepted]
    P -- Unsafe --> R[Increase Wall Thickness or Reduce pi]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Elemental Ring and Stress Directions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Inner Radius ri with Internal Pressure pi acting outward] --> nodeB[Elemental Ring of Thickness dr at radius r]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Radial Stress Sigma r acting inward on outer face]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Hoop Stress Sigma h acting tangentially on side faces]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Outer Radius ro with External Pressure po acting inward]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Radial Equilibrium yields Lame Constants A and B]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Final Equations Sigma r = A minus B by r squared]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Final Equations Sigma h = A plus B by r squared]
```

## 4.3 Radial Stress vs Hoop Stress Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisStart[Centerline at r equals 0] --> axisInner[At r equals ri: Sigma r equals negative pi and Sigma h equals maximum]
    axisInner --> axisMid[At r equals mid radius: Sigma r and Sigma h move closer together]
    axisMid --> axisOuter[At r equals ro: Sigma r equals negative po and Sigma h equals minimum]
    axisInner --> note1[Maximum Shear Stress Located at Inner Bore]
    axisMid --> note2[Hoop Stress Decays Hyperbolically Outward]
    axisOuter --> note3[Radial Stress Becomes Zero when po equals 0]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Thick vs Thin Wall

| Decision Factor | Thin Wall Path | Thick Wall Path |
|---|---|---|
| Geometric Ratio $t/D_i$ | $\le 1/20$ | $> 1/20$ |
| Governing Equation | $\sigma_h = p r / t$ | Lamé's equations |
| Hoop Stress Variation | Constant through thickness | Hyperbolic, max at bore |
| Radial Stress | Neglected (assumed zero) | Considered, varies from $-p_i$ to $-p_o$ |
| Typical Vessel Examples | LPG cylinders, small air receivers | Hydraulic presses, gun barrels, high-pressure boilers |
| KTU Exam Weightage | 2–3 marks | 7–14 marks (frequently Part B) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a thick cylinder. State the conditions under which a cylinder is classified as thick.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cylinder is termed **thick** when its wall thickness is comparable to or exceeds one-twentieth of its internal diameter, i.e., when

$$\frac{t}{D_i} > \frac{1}{20}$$

Under such a condition, the stress distribution across the wall is **non-uniform**, with hoop stress being maximum at the inner surface and decaying outward hyperbolically. In contrast, for a thin cylinder ($t/D_i \le 1/20$), the stress is assumed uniformly distributed and is computed using membrane theory. Hence, thick cylinders must be analyzed using **Lamé's equations**, which account for radial stress variation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Write down Lamé's equations for radial and hoop stress in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_r(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \;-\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\, r^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_h(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \;+\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\, r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\, r^{2}}$$

These can be compactly written as $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$ and $\sigma_h = A + B/r^2$, where $A$ and $B$ are Lamé's constants.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

A thick steel cylinder has an internal diameter of **150 mm** and external diameter of **300 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **60 MPa**. The external pressure is atmospheric and can be neglected. Calculate:
**(a)** Lamé's constants $A$ and $B$.
**(b)** The hoop stress and radial stress at the inner and outer surfaces. Comment on the maximum shear stress.

#### Part (a) — Lamé's Constants (7 marks)

Given:
- $r_i = 75$ mm
- $r_o = 150$ mm
- $p_i = 60$ MPa
- $p_o = 0$

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 75^{2}}{150^{2} - 75^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 5625}{22500 - 5625} = \frac{337500}{16875} = 20.0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{p_i r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 5625 \times 22500}{16875} = \frac{7{,}593{,}750{,}000}{16875} = 450{,}000 \text{ MPa·mm}^{2}$$

**Therefore $A = 20$ MPa and $B = 4.5 \times 10^{5}$ MPa·mm².**

#### Part (b) — Stresses at Inner and Outer Surfaces (7 marks)

**At inner surface $r = 75$ mm:**

$$\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r_i^{2}} = 20 + \frac{450000}{5625} = 20 + 80 = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r_i^{2}} = 20 - 80 = -60 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(matches } -p_i\text{)}$$

**At outer surface $r = 150$ mm:**

$$\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r_o^{2}} = 20 + \frac{450000}{22500} = 20 + 20 = 40 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r_o^{2}} = 20 - 20 = 0 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(matches } -p_o = 0\text{)}$$

**Maximum Shear Stress (at inner bore):**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2}\bigg\vert_{r = r_i} = \frac{100 - (-60)}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comment:** The hoop stress varies from 100 MPa at the bore to 40 MPa at the outer surface — confirming the hyperbolic decay. Maximum shear stress is at the inner bore, which is the most likely location for yielding initiation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

A thick cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal radius of **100 mm** and is required to withstand an internal pressure of **100 MPa**. The allowable tensile stress for the material is **240 MPa**. The external pressure is negligible.
**(a)** Determine the required outer radius using Lamé's equation.
**(b)** Calculate the maximum hoop stress and verify the design is safe. Also find the radial stress at mid-thickness.

#### Part (a) — Required Outer Radius (7 marks)

For maximum hoop stress condition at the inner surface (Lamé, $p_o = 0$):

$$\sigma_{h,\max} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$$

$$240 \ge 100 \cdot \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

$$\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = 2.4$$

Let $r_o^{2} = x$. Then:

$$x + 10000 = 2.4(x - 10000)$$
$$x + 10000 = 2.4x - 24000$$
$$34000 = 1.4 x$$
$$x = \frac{34000}{1.4} = 24285.71$$

$$r_o = \sqrt{24285.71} = 155.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Therefore the required outer radius is approximately 156 mm** (round up for safety, often take $r_o = 160$ mm).

#### Part (b) — Stress Verification and Mid-Thickness Radial Stress (7 marks)

Using $r_o = 156$ mm:

$$A = \frac{100 \times 100^{2}}{156^{2} - 100^{2}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{24336 - 10000} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{14336} = 69.75 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{100 \times 100^{2} \times 156^{2}}{14336} = \frac{2.4336 \times 10^{10}}{14336} = 1.6976 \times 10^{6} \text{ MPa·mm}^{2}$$

**Maximum Hoop Stress at bore:**

$$\sigma_{h,\max} = A + \frac{B}{r_i^{2}} = 69.75 + \frac{1.6976 \times 10^{6}}{10000} = 69.75 + 169.76 = 239.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $239.5 \text{ MPa} < 240 \text{ MPa}$ — **Design is SAFE** ✓

**Radial Stress at Mid-Thickness** ($r = (100+156)/2 = 128$ mm):

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}} = 69.75 - \frac{1.6976 \times 10^{6}}{128^{2}} = 69.75 - 103.62 = -33.87 \text{ MPa}$$

The radial stress at mid-thickness is approximately **−33.87 MPa (compressive)**, confirming the radial stress decays from −100 MPa at the bore to 0 MPa at the outer surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** $r$ values must remain in **mm** consistently when $B$ has units MPa·mm². Mixing cm with mm is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting external pressure:** If $p_o \ne 0$, both $A$ and $B$ formulas must include the $p_o r_o^{2}$ term. Many students wrongly use only the $p_o = 0$ form.
> 3. **Sign convention error:** Radial stress at the bore should come out **negative** (compressive). If your answer is positive, you have a sign error — typically caught by the examiner.
> 4. **Maximum shear stress location:** Examiners expect $\tau_{\max}$ to be evaluated **at the inner bore**, not at $r = r_o$ or $r = 0$.
> 5. **Thin-wall shortcut applied incorrectly:** KTU examiners specifically test whether students recognize when thin-wall formulas are **invalid** — a 1-mark deduction for using $\sigma_h = pr/t$ on a thick-wall problem.
> 6. **Hoop stress at bore:** A very common slip is computing hoop stress at $r = r_o$ instead of $r = r_i$. Always remember: **maximum hoop stress is at the inner bore**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

- **Thick cylinder definition:** $t/D_i > 1/20$ — use Lamé, not membrane theory.
- **Lamé's two equations:** $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$ and $\sigma_h = A + B/r^2$.
- **Lamé's constants:**
  - $A = (p_i r_i^{2} - p_o r_o^{2})/(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})$
  - $B = (p_i - p_o) r_i^{2} r_o^{2}/(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})$
- **Boundary conditions:** $\sigma_r(r_i) = -p_i$ and $\sigma_r(r_o) = -p_o$.
- **Hoop stress is maximum at $r = r_i$** (inner bore) — always.
- **Hoop stress is minimum at $r = r_o$** (outer surface).
- **Radial stress is always compressive** between $r_i$ and $r_o$.
- **Maximum shear stress occurs at the inner bore:** $\tau_{\max} = (\sigma_{h,\max} - \sigma_{r,\min})/2$.
- **For $p_o = 0$ simplified form:** $\sigma_{h,\max} = p_i (r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})/(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})$.
- **Design equation (rearrange for $r_o$):** $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \ge p_i (K^2 + 1)/(K^2 - 1)$, where $K = r_o/r_i$.
- **Plane strain assumption:** Valid for long cylinders; for short cylinders, end effects (3D stress state) must be considered.
- **Material is assumed homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** — Lamé's derivation relies on Hooke's Law in the radial direction.
- **Compound cylinders** (next Module 3 topic) build on these same equations by adding a third boundary at the interface radius.
- **Tresca vs von Mises:** For thick cylinders, **Tresca** is more conservative and is the default check in ASME codes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lame's Equation for Internal and External Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lame's Equation for Internal and External Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Lamé's Equations** constitute the closed-form analytical solution for the **two-dimensional axisymmetric stress distribution** in a **thick-walled cylindrical pressure vessel** subjected to uniform internal and/or external pressure. The equations describe the **radial stress (σᵣ)** and the **circumferential / hoop stress (σθ)** at any radial location `r` within the wall of the cylinder, under the fundamental assumptions of:

- **Plane strain** (long cylinder, plane sections remain plane) — sometimes plane stress for very short vessels.
- **Axisymmetry** — no variation in the angular (θ) or axial (z) direction of stresses.
- **Homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material**.
- **No body forces** (gravity neglected relative to pressure).
- **Uniform radial pressure** on the inner (r = a) and outer (r = b) surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Lamé's equations form the foundation of Module 2. They are the precursor to **compound cylinders, shrink-fit assemblies, autofrettage, and external pressure stability** problems. Mastering the derivation and boundary-condition application is **non-negotiable** for ESE and module tests.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are squeezing a **roll of toilet paper** (thick wall = many layers) with your palm. The outer paper feels one pressure, the inner cardboard core feels another, and every layer in between carries a *different* share of the load.

Now imagine a **single sheet of paper** squeezed — every fiber feels nearly the same stress (thin-wall approximation). Lamé's equations are the *mathematical microscope* that tells you exactly how much stress each "layer" of the thick wall carries.

> **Plain English Summary:**  
> Lamé's equations tell us that **stress is NOT uniform** across the thickness of a thick cylinder. The hoop stress is **maximum at the inner surface** and **minimum at the outer surface** for internal pressure. For external pressure, the compressive hoop stress is **maximum at the outer surface**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Inner radius:** `a` (m or mm)
- **Outer radius:** `b` (m or mm)
- **Internal pressure:** `pᵢ` (MPa or N/mm²)
- **External pressure:** `pₑ` (MPa or N/mm²)
- **Radial coordinate:** `r` (m or mm), where `a ≤ r ≤ b`
- **Stress convention:** **Tensile = positive**, Compressive = negative
- **Young's Modulus:** `E` (typically **200 GPa** for carbon steel, **70 GPa** for aluminum alloys)
- **Poisson's ratio:** `ν` (typically **0.3** for steels)
- **Yield strength:** `σy` (typically **250 MPa** for SA-516 Gr.70 carbon steel)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** Pressure acting inward on a free surface produces a **compressive radial stress**. So at r = a, σᵣ = −pᵢ. At r = b, σᵣ = −pₑ (if external pressure exists). Students frequently lose marks by using the wrong sign.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions (Why the Theory Works)

1. **Long cylinder approximation:** Plane sections remain plane → axial stress is constant across the cross-section. We treat it as a 2D plane problem.
2. **No angular variation:** The vessel is symmetric about its central axis → no shear stress τᵣθ exists.
3. **Equilibrium is radial:** The element only experiences radial forces.
4. **Compatibility:** Strains are related to displacements such that the cylinder remains continuous (no gaps).

### 2.2 Derivation Logic Flow (Bulleted Reasoning)

- **Step 1 — Free Body Cut:** Imagine slicing the cylinder with two radial planes (separated by angle dθ) and two concentric cylinders (at radii r and r + dr).
- **Step 2 — Force Balance:** Sum forces in the radial direction on this curved wedge element.
- **Step 3 — Pressure × Area → Stress × Area:** Convert pressure on inner/outer curved faces to equivalent forces, and ring stresses on the radial cut faces to forces.
- **Step 4 — The Governing ODE:** Yields the first fundamental equilibrium equation:
$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$
- **Step 5 — Compatibility via Displacement:** Express radial displacement `u` in terms of strains εᵣ and εθ. Hooke's Law in cylindrical coordinates:
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}$$
- **Step 6 — Stress–Strain Relations:** Apply generalized Hooke's law to get σᵣ and σθ in terms of u.
- **Step 7 — Substitute into Equilibrium:** Obtain a second-order ODE in u(r).
- **Step 8 — Solve the ODE:** General solution `u = C₁r + C₂/r`. Differentiate to get stresses.
- **Step 9 — Apply Boundary Conditions:** Use known pressures at r = a and r = b to find C₁ and C₂.
- **Step 10 — Final Lamé Equations** emerge.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Equilibrium (radial) | $d\sigma_r/dr + (\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta)/r = 0$ | — | First fundamental relation |
| 2 | Radial stress (combined) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \dfrac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}$ | MPa | General Lamé result |
| 3 | Hoop stress (combined) | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}$ | MPa | General Lamé result |
| 4 | Max hoop (internal p only) | $\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i\,(b^2 + a^2)/(b^2 - a^2)$ | MPa | Occurs at r = a |
| 5 | Min hoop (internal p only) | $\sigma_{\theta,\min} = 2 p_i a^2/(b^2 - a^2)$ | MPa | Occurs at r = b |
| 6 | Max radial (internal p only) | $\sigma_{r,\max} = -p_i$ (at r = a) | MPa | Compressive at bore |
| 7 | Min radial (internal p only) | $\sigma_{r,\min} = 0$ (at r = b) | MPa | Free surface |
| 8 | External p only — σᵣ | $\sigma_r = -\dfrac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{a^2}{r^2}\right)$ | MPa | Always compressive |
| 9 | External p only — σθ | $\sigma_\theta = -\dfrac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{a^2}{r^2}\right)$ | MPa | Max compressive at r = b |
| 10 | Radius ratio | $k = a/b$ (or $b/a$, check textbook) | — | Used for simplified tables |
| 11 | Lamé constants (general) | $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$, $\sigma_\theta = A + B/r^2$ | MPa | A, B found from BCs |
| 12 | Hoop/radial ratio (internal p) | $\sigma_\theta/\sigma_r = -(b^2 + r^2)/(b^2 - r^2)$ | — | Negative → opposite signs |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CRITICAL MISTAKE AVOIDED:** Never write `σr = A + B/r²` and `σθ = A − B/r²`. The + and − are **flipped** compared to intuitive guess. The hoop stress carries the **plus** because of the bending-like curvature effect.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of high-pressure reactors**: hydrocrackers, ammonia converters, urea reactors operating at **200–400 bar** where thin-wall theory fails (D/t > ~20 is no longer valid).
- **Gun barrels and cannon tubes**: autofrettage (a process built directly on Lamé's equations) introduces beneficial compressive residual stresses.
- **Nuclear reactor coolant pipes**: subject to both internal coolant pressure and external insulation/overburden pressure.
- **Submarine hulls and deep-sea pipelines**: dominant external hydrostatic pressure on cylindrical hulls.
- **Compound cylinders**: shrink-fit assemblies (e.g., artillery, autoclaves) where the Lamé principle is applied in successive layers.
- **Hydraulic cylinders**: thick-walled barrels for high-pressure actuation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Lamé's Equations

#### Step 1 — Geometry and Element

Consider a thick-walled cylinder with:
- Inner radius = `a`
- Outer radius = `b`
- Subjected to internal pressure `pᵢ` at r = a and external pressure `pₑ` at r = b.

Cut out a small wedge-shaped element bounded by:
- Two concentric cylinders at radii `r` and `r + dr`
- Two radial planes at angles `0` and `dθ`

The arc length of the inner face is `(r dθ)`, outer face is `((r + dr) dθ)`, and the radial thickness is `dr`. The axial length is taken as unity (plane analysis).

#### Step 2 — Radial Equilibrium of the Element

Sum forces in the **radial direction** (outward positive):

Force from σᵣ on inner face (acting inward on the element): `−σᵣ × (r dθ) × 1`
Force from σᵣ on outer face (acting outward on element): `(σᵣ + dσᵣ)(r + dr)dθ × 1`
Force from σθ on the two radial faces (inward components): `−2 σθ sin(dθ/2) × dr × 1`

For small dθ, `sin(dθ/2) ≈ dθ/2`, so the two σθ terms combine to `−σθ × dθ × dr`.

Setting ΣFᵣ = 0:

$$(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr)\,d\theta - \sigma_r\,r\,d\theta - \sigma_\theta\,d\theta\,dr = 0$$

#### Step 3 — Simplify the Equilibrium Equation

Expand `(σᵣ + dσᵣ)(r + dr)`:

$$= \sigma_r\,r + \sigma_r\,dr + r\,d\sigma_r + dr\,d\sigma_r$$

Drop the second-order term `dr·dσᵣ`. Substitute back:

$$\sigma_r\,r + \sigma_r\,dr + r\,d\sigma_r - \sigma_r\,r - \sigma_\theta\,dr = 0$$

The `σᵣ·r` terms cancel. Divide by `r·dr`:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$

This is **Eq. (1)** — the radial equilibrium of a thick cylinder.

#### Step 4 — Strain–Displacement Relations (Kinematics)

For axisymmetric deformation, the only displacement is radial `u(r)`:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}$$

#### Step 5 — Generalized Hooke's Law (Plane Stress Form)

Assuming **plane stress** (long cylinder with free ends, or analysis neglecting σ_z):

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_r - \nu\,\sigma_\theta)$$
$$\varepsilon_\theta = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_\theta - \nu\,\sigma_r)$$

#### Step 6 — Compatibility Condition

Eliminate `u` by differentiating:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_\theta}{dr} = \frac{1}{r}\left(\frac{du}{dr} - \frac{u}{r^2}\right) = \frac{\varepsilon_r - \varepsilon_\theta}{r}$$

Substitute Hooke's law expressions, simplify using equilibrium Eq. (1), and after algebraic manipulation we obtain the **second-order ODE**:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{du}{dr} - \frac{u}{r^2} = 0$$

#### Step 7 — General Solution

This is a Cauchy–Euler equation. Try `u = rⁿ`:

$$n(n-1) + n - 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \pm 1$$

Therefore:

$$u(r) = C_1\,r + \frac{C_2}{r}$$

Where `C₁` and `C₂` are constants of integration.

#### Step 8 — Stresses from Displacement

Substitute u(r) back into the Hooke's law expressions. After simplification:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{E}{(1-\nu^2)(1+\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)C_1 - (1+\nu)\frac{C_2}{r^2}\right] \cdot \text{(plane stress factor)}$$

For the **plane strain / thick cylinder** standard form (combining constants), we write:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

Where:
- `A` and `B` are **Lamé constants**
- `A = E\,C_1 / [(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)] · (1−ν)` (for plane strain), or analogous plane-stress form
- `B = −E\,C_2 / [(1+\nu)(1−2ν)]` (for plane strain), or analogous plane-stress form

> **Key Insight:** The form `σᵣ = A − B/r²` and `σθ = A + B/r²` is the **only** mathematical shape that satisfies both equilibrium and compatibility for an axisymmetric thick cylinder.

#### Step 9 — Apply Boundary Conditions (Internal Pressure Only: pₑ = 0)

Boundary conditions:
- At r = a: σᵣ = −pᵢ (compressive radial stress, since pressure pushes inward on the bore)
- At r = b: σᵣ = 0 (free outer surface)

From `σᵣ(b) = 0`:

$$A - \frac{B}{b^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{B}{b^2}$$

From `σᵣ(a) = −pᵢ`:

$$A - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p_i$$

Substitute A:

$$\frac{B}{b^2} - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p_i$$

$$B\left(\frac{1}{b^2} - \frac{1}{a^2}\right) = -p_i$$

$$B\cdot\frac{a^2 - b^2}{a^2 b^2} = -p_i$$

$$B = \frac{p_i\,a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

Then:

$$A = \frac{B}{b^2} = \frac{p_i\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

#### Step 10 — Final Lamé Equations (Internal Pressure Only)

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{p_i\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

**Verification:**
- At r = a: σᵣ = pᵢ·a²/(b²−a²)·(1 − b²/a²) = pᵢ·a²/(b²−a²)·(a²−b²)/a² = −pᵢ ✓
- At r = b: σᵣ = pᵢ·a²/(b²−a²)·(1 − 1) = 0 ✓
- At r = a: σθ = pᵢ·a²/(b²−a²)·(1 + b²/a²) = pᵢ(b² + a²)/(b²−a²) — maximum ✓

#### Step 11 — Lamé Equations for External Pressure Only (pᵢ = 0, pₑ = p)

Boundary conditions:
- At r = a: σᵣ = 0 (bore is open / no internal pressure)
- At r = b: σᵣ = −pₑ (compressive)

From `σᵣ(a) = 0`:

$$A - \frac{B}{a^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{B}{a^2}$$

From `σᵣ(b) = −pₑ`:

$$\frac{B}{a^2} - \frac{B}{b^2} = -p_e$$

$$B\cdot\frac{b^2 - a^2}{a^2 b^2} = -p_e$$

$$B = -\frac{p_e\,a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

Then:

$$A = -\frac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

So:

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = -\frac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = -\frac{p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)}$$

**Verification:**
- At r = b: σθ = −pₑ·b²/(b²−a²)·(1 + a²/b²) = −pₑ(b² + a²)/(b²−a²) — maximum compression ✓
- At r = a: σθ = −pₑ·b²/(b²−a²)·(1 + 1) = −2pₑ·b²/(b²−a²) — wait, this is bigger? No: substitute a → a, and since a < b, the magnitude at r = b is larger. ✓

#### Step 12 — Combined Internal and External Pressure (By Superposition)

Since the problem is **linear elastic**, superpose the two cases:

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A thick-walled cylinder has inner radius **a = 50 mm** and outer radius **b = 100 mm**. It is subjected to internal pressure **pᵢ = 80 MPa** and external pressure **pₑ = 10 MPa**. Calculate σᵣ and σθ at r = 50 mm, 75 mm, and 100 mm. Find the location and value of maximum hoop stress.

**Solution:**

Given:
- a = 50 mm, b = 100 mm
- pᵢ = 80 MPa, pₑ = 10 MPa
- pᵢ − pₑ = 70 MPa
- b² − a² = 10000 − 2500 = 7500 mm²
- a²b² = 2500 × 10000 = 2.5 × 10⁷ mm⁴

Compute the common coefficient:

$$C_1 = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{(80)(2500) - (10)(10000)}{7500} = \frac{200000 - 100000}{7500} = \frac{100000}{7500} = 13.333 \text{ MPa}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{(p_i - p_e)\,a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{(70)(2.5 \times 10^7)}{7500} = \frac{1.75 \times 10^9}{7500} = 2.333 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa·mm}^2$$

Now compute at each radius:

**At r = a = 50 mm (r² = 2500):**

$$\sigma_r = 13.333 - \frac{2.333 \times 10^5}{2500} = 13.333 - 93.333 = -80 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 13.333 + 93.333 = 106.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 75 mm (r² = 5625):**

$$\sigma_r = 13.333 - \frac{2.333 \times 10^5}{5625} = 13.333 - 41.481 = -28.148 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 13.333 + 41.481 = 54.815 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = b = 100 mm (r² = 10000):**

$$\sigma_r = 13.333 - \frac{2.333 \times 10^5}{10000} = 13.333 - 23.333 = -10 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 13.333 + 23.333 = 36.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**Maximum Hoop Stress:** Occurs at **r = a = 50 mm**, with **σθ_max = 106.667 MPa**.

**Equivalent verification using simplified formula:**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\max} = \frac{p_i\,(b^2 + a^2) - 2 p_e\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{80(10000+2500) - 2(10)(10000)}{7500} = \frac{200000 - 200000}{7500} + 80\cdot\frac{12500}{7500}$$

Wait, let me recompute:

$$\sigma_\theta(r=a) = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_e)b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$= \frac{(80)(2500) - (10)(10000)}{7500} + \frac{(70)(10000)}{7500}$$

$$= \frac{100000}{7500} + \frac{700000}{7500} = \frac{800000}{7500} = 106.667 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State the assumptions made in deriving Lamé's equations for a thick-walled cylinder.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The cylinder material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (1 mark).
2. The cylinder is **long** so that plane sections remain plane — plane strain or plane stress assumption is valid (1 mark).
3. The loading is **axisymmetric** (uniform pressure around the circumference), and **body forces (gravity) are neglected** relative to pressure (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Write down the general Lamé equations for radial and circumferential stresses in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure `pᵢ` and external pressure `pₑ`.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_e)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_e)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

Where `a` = inner radius, `b` = outer radius, `r` = radial coordinate.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each). Students choose **ONE** from the OR alternatives.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Lamé equation for the radial and hoop stress distribution in a thick-walled cylinder subjected to **internal pressure only**. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions** (1 mark)
- Homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material
- Long cylinder → plane sections remain plane
- Axisymmetric loading
- No body forces
- Uniform pressure on the inner surface

**Step 2 — Equilibrium** (2 marks)

Consider an element at radius r with thickness dr and angle dθ. Force balance gives:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0 \quad \cdots \text{(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Compatibility & Hooke's Law** (2 marks)

Using εᵣ = du/dr and εθ = u/r, with Hooke's law, the displacement is:

$$u(r) = C_1 r + \frac{C_2}{r}$$

Stresses take the form:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Conditions** (1 mark)

- At r = a: σᵣ = −pᵢ
- At r = b: σᵣ = 0

Solving: `A = pᵢa²/(b²−a²)` and `B = pᵢa²b²/(b²−a²)` (1 mark)

**Final Lamé Equations:**

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right) \quad \text{(1 mark, final boxed result)}$$

---

**(b)** A thick-walled cylinder has **inner radius 75 mm** and **outer radius 150 mm**. It is subjected to **internal pressure of 60 MPa**. Determine the **radial and hoop stresses** at radii **r = 75 mm, 100 mm, and 150 mm**. Also find the **maximum hoop stress** and its location. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** a = 75 mm, b = 150 mm, pᵢ = 60 MPa, pₑ = 0

**Compute the common factor:**

$$\frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{60 \times 75^2}{150^2 - 75^2} = \frac{60 \times 5625}{22500 - 5625} = \frac{337500}{16875} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 75 mm (r² = 5625, b²/r² = 22500/5625 = 4):**

$$\sigma_r = 20(1 - 4) = -60 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks for substitution})$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20(1 + 4) = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 100 mm (r² = 10000, b²/r² = 22500/10000 = 2.25):**

$$\sigma_r = 20(1 - 2.25) = -25 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20(1 + 2.25) = 65 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**At r = 150 mm (r² = 22500, b²/r² = 1):**

$$\sigma_r = 20(1 - 1) = 0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20(1 + 1) = 40 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Maximum Hoop Stress:** σθ_max = **100 MPa** at **r = a = 75 mm** (inner surface) (1 mark).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative)** — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Starting from the general Lamé equations, derive the expressions for **radial and hoop stresses** in a thick-walled cylinder subjected to **external pressure only**. Also state the location of maximum stress. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**General Lamé form:**

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Boundary Conditions for External Pressure Only (pᵢ = 0, pₑ = p):**
- At r = a: σᵣ = 0 (bore open / no internal pressure) → A = B/a² (1 mark)
- At r = b: σᵣ = −pₑ (compressive) (1 mark)

Substituting A = B/a² into the second BC:

$$\frac{B}{a^2} - \frac{B}{b^2} = -p_e$$

$$B = -\frac{p_e a^2 b^2}{b^2 - a^2}, \quad A = -\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Final Lamé Equations for External Pressure:**

$$\sigma_r = -\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right) \quad (1 \text{ mark for boxed equations})$$

**Location of Maximum Stress:** Since σθ is negative and the magnitude grows with r², the **maximum compressive hoop stress** occurs at the **outer surface r = b** (1 mark):

$$|\sigma_\theta|_{\max} = \frac{p_e(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$$

---

**(b)** A thick-walled cylinder has **inner radius 100 mm** and **outer radius 200 mm**. It is subjected to **external pressure of 25 MPa**. Determine the **radial and hoop stresses** at **r = 100 mm, 150 mm, and 200 mm**. Find the **maximum compressive hoop stress** and verify the boundary condition at the bore. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** a = 100 mm, b = 200 mm, pₑ = 25 MPa, pᵢ = 0

**Common coefficient:**

$$\frac{p_e b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{25 \times 40000}{40000 - 10000} = \frac{1000000}{30000} = 33.333 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 100 mm (a²/r² = 10000/10000 = 1):**

$$\sigma_r = -33.333(1 - 1) = 0 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(verified — free bore)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -33.333(1 + 1) = -66.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**At r = 150 mm (a²/r² = 10000/22500 = 0.444):**

$$\sigma_r = -33.333(1 - 0.444) = -18.519 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -33.333(1 + 0.444) = -48.148 \text{ MPa} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**At r = 200 mm (a²/r² = 10000/40000 = 0.25):**

$$\sigma_r = -33.333(1 - 0.25) = -25 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(verified — equals −pₑ)} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

$$\sigma_\theta = -33.333(1 + 0.25) = -41.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**Maximum Compressive Hoop Stress** (most negative): **|σθ|max = 66.667 MPa** (compression), occurring at **r = a = 100 mm** (inner surface) (1 mark). 

> Note: Although the magnitude increases from outer surface to inner surface for the external pressure case, the **magnitude** at r = a is greater because the expression σθ = −pₑb²/(b²−a²)·(1 + a²/r²) reaches its absolute maximum when r is smallest (r = a). Always check by substituting; do not assume "outer surface" based on intuition.

**Boundary Verification:** σᵣ(r = a) = 0 ✓ and σᵣ(r = b) = −pₑ = −25 MPa ✓ (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign error** (most common, ~40% of marks lost): Forgetting that σᵣ is **compressive (−pᵢ or −pₑ)** at the loaded surface. If you write σᵣ(a) = +pᵢ, you will lose **2–3 marks** instantly.
> - **Confusing A and B**: Some students swap the constants and produce a wrong stress distribution. Always verify that σθ is **maximum at r = a** for internal pressure.
> - **Units mismatch**: Mixing N/mm² and MPa inconsistently. Both are equal numerically, but mention the unit explicitly.
> - **No boundary verification**: KTU examiners **always reward** students who verify that σᵣ(b) = 0 for internal pressure case.
> - **Incomplete derivation**: Stating the final equations without showing equilibrium + compatibility gets **partial credit only**. Always derive both.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Lamé's equations** give σᵣ(r) and σθ(r) for thick-walled cylinders under uniform internal/external pressure.
- **Equilibrium ODE:** `dσᵣ/dr + (σᵣ − σθ)/r = 0` — must memorize.
- **General solution form:** `σᵣ = A − B/r²` and `σθ = A + B/r²`. Note the **sign** — hoop gets **+**.
- **Sign convention:** Tensile = positive. Pressure produces **compressive** σᵣ at the loaded surface.
- **Internal pressure only:**
  - σθ is **max at r = a**: `σθ_max = pᵢ(b² + a²)/(b² − a²)`
  - σθ is **min at r = b**: `σθ_min = 2pᵢa²/(b² − a²)`
  - σᵣ ranges from **−pᵢ at r = a** to **0 at r = b**.
- **External pressure only:**
  - Both σᵣ and σθ are **compressive**.
  - **|σθ|max** at **r = a** (inner surface, smallest radius): `|σθ|_max = pₑb²/(b²−a²)·2 = 2pₑb²/(b²−a²)`.
  - **|σᵣ|max** at **r = b**: equals `pₑ`.
- **Combined pressure:** Linear superposition applies. The general expression has two terms: a constant and a `1/r²` term.
- **Stress variation:** Hoop stress is **non-linear** in r — it varies as `1/r²`.
- **Thin-wall limit check:** When `b → a`, both σθ_max formulas should reduce to `pᵢ·D/(2t)` (Hoop stress in thin-walled cylinder).
- **Design implication:** Since σθ_max occurs at the bore for internal pressure, **bores are crack initiation sites** → autofrettage deliberately induces beneficial compressive residual stresses at the bore.
- **Verification habit:** Always substitute r = a and r = b into your final equations and confirm the boundary conditions.
- **Limitation of Lamé theory:** Does not handle **plastic deformation**, **thermal stresses**, or **creep** — those need Tresca, von Mises, or viscoelastic models.
- **KTU exam tip:** For a 14-mark question, allocate ~7 marks to derivation and ~7 marks to numerical application. Always **draw the FBD** of the wedge element for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shrink Fit Stresses in Built-up Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shrink Fit Stresses in Built-up Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shrink Fit Assembly (Built-up Cylinder):** A composite pressure vessel constructed by mechanically joining two or more concentric cylinders through thermal interference, where the outer cylinder is heated (to expand), the inner cylinder is cooled (to contract), and upon temperature equalization, the resulting differential contraction generates a **residual contact pressure (P_c)** at the cylindrical interface. This pre-stressing mechanism shifts the maximum tangential stress from the inner surface to the interface, significantly increasing the vessel's pressure-carrying capacity.

### Geometric Nomenclature
| Symbol | Geometric Parameter | Typical Units |
|:------:|:-------------------|:-------------:|
| $a$ | Inner radius of inner cylinder | **mm** |
| $b$ | Common interface radius (outer of inner, inner of outer) | **mm** |
| $c$ | Outer radius of outer cylinder | **mm** |
| $\delta$ | Radial interference (interference at common radius) | **mm** |
| $P_c$ | Contact (interface) pressure | **MPa** |
| $P_i$ | Internal operating pressure | **MPa** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tightening Grip" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine gripping a metal rod inside a slightly smaller metal ring. You cannot push the rod in by hand. However, if you **heat the ring** (it expands) and **chill the rod in ice** (it shrinks), the rod slips in easily. As both return to room temperature, the ring squeezes the rod with enormous radial force—this squeezing force is the **contact pressure $P_c$**. The rod is now in **compression** and the ring is in **tension**, even before any internal pressure is applied. This is exactly the principle used in cannon barrels, hydraulic cylinder liners, autoclaves, and gun barrels.

### Physical Constants Referenced
- Young's Modulus: $E \approx 200$ **GPa** for carbon steel
- Poisson's Ratio: $\nu \approx 0.3$ for most engineering metals

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Shrink Fit

The mechanism operates in a **two-stage stress superposition**:

1. **Stage 1 (Residual / Assembly Stresses):** Pure interference fit produces tangential compression ($\sigma_\theta < 0$) in the inner cylinder and tangential tension ($\sigma_\theta > 0$) in the outer cylinder at the interface.

2. **Stage 2 (Operating Stresses):** When internal pressure $P_i$ is applied, these stresses **superimpose algebraically** on the Stage 1 stresses. The result is a flatter, more uniform tangential stress distribution.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use shrink fit?** A monolithic (single-piece) thick cylinder experiences a **stress concentration** at the inner surface where $\sigma_\theta$ is maximum. A built-up cylinder with the correct interference redistributes this stress, pushing the maximum hoop stress **inward toward the interface**, thus exploiting material capacity more uniformly.

## 2.2 Assumptions for Analysis

- Cylinders are **long** → **Plane Strain** conditions apply (closed-end assumption: $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta)$).
- Material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys Hooke's Law.
- No body forces (gravity neglected).
- Contact is **uniform** along the cylindrical interface (no eccentricity).
- The interface contact pressure $P_c$ acts normal to the surface, equal and opposite on both cylinders.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Shrink Fit Stresses

| # | Quantity | Formula (Plane Strain, Long Cylinder) | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:------------------------------------|:------|
| 1 | Lame's radial stress (general) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{P_i a^2 - P_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \dfrac{(P_i - P_o)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2}$ | MPa |
| 2 | Lame's hoop stress (general) | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{P_i a^2 - P_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(P_i - P_o)a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)r^2}$ | MPa |
| 3 | Inner cyl. radial displacement at $r = b$ (external $P_c$) | $u_b^{(i)} = \dfrac{-b P_c}{E}\left[\dfrac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right]$ | mm |
| 4 | Outer cyl. radial displacement at $r = b$ (internal $P_c$) | $u_b^{(o)} = \dfrac{b P_c}{E}\left[\dfrac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right]$ | mm |
| 5 | **Contact pressure $P_c$ (pure shrink fit)** | $P_c = \dfrac{\delta E}{b} \left[\dfrac{b^2 - a^2}{a^2(1+\nu)+b^2(1-\nu)} + \dfrac{c^2 - b^2}{b^2(1-\nu)+c^2(1+\nu)}\right]^{-1}$ | MPa |
| 6 | Diametral interference | $\Delta = 2\delta$ | mm |
| 7 | Effective modulus (plane strain) | $E' = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}$ ; $\nu' = \dfrac{\nu}{1-\nu}$ | GPa |
| 8 | Combined (Stage 1 + Stage 2) hoop stress | $\sigma_\theta^{total} = \sigma_\theta^{shrink} + \sigma_\theta^{pressure}$ | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:** Compressive stresses are **negative**; tensile stresses are **positive**. Radial stress at a free surface is always zero. When checking yielding, use $|\sigma_\theta|$ for magnitude and apply von Mises or Tresca criteria.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gun barrels and cannons** — Pre-compresses the inner liner to resist bursting from propellant gases.
- **High-pressure autoclaves** — Multi-layer shrink-fit construction allows vessel diameters up to 2+ meters.
- **Hydraulic cylinder liners** — Hard-chrome inner liner shrink-fitted into a ductile outer sleeve.
- **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels** — Forging limitations are overcome by multilayer shrink-fit construction.
- **Concrete reinforcement analogy** — Like pre-stressed concrete, the inner member is placed in beneficial compression.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
