# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case A — Pure Shrink Fit (No Internal Pressure, $P_i = 0$)

### 3.1.1 Stage 1a: Stresses in the Inner Cylinder (loaded by external pressure $P_c$)

**Setup:** Inner cylinder is treated as a thick cylinder with:
- Inner radius: $a$
- Outer radius: $b$
- Internal pressure: $P_i = 0$
- External pressure: $P_o = P_c$ (the contact pressure from the outer cylinder squeezing inward)

**Step 1 — Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_r$:**

Substitute $P_i = 0$, $P_o = P_c$ into the general Lame's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r^{(i)}(r) &= \frac{(0)\cdot a^2 - P_c \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(0 - P_c)\cdot a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\cdot r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(-P_c)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{P_c\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_r^{(i)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[\frac{a^2}{r^2} - 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The first term is a uniform (axisymmetric) compression; the second term decays with $r^2$ and provides the radial variation.

**Step 2 — Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_\theta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(r) &= \frac{(0)\cdot a^2 - P_c \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(0 - P_c)\cdot a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\cdot r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{P_c\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(r) &= -\frac{P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 + \frac{a^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Since $P_c$ is compressive on the outside, the entire inner cylinder is in tangential compression. At the inner surface ($r = a$): $\sigma_\theta = -P_c(a^2 + b^2)/(b^2 - a^2)$.

**Step 3 — Radial displacement of inner cylinder at $r = b$:**

Using the plane-strain displacement relation $u(r) = \dfrac{r}{E}[\sigma_\theta - \nu\,\sigma_r]$:

Evaluate stresses at $r = b$:
- $\sigma_r^{(i)}(b) = -P_c$ (boundary condition check ✓)
- $\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(b) = -\dfrac{P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_b^{(i)} &= \frac{b}{E}\left[\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(b) - \nu\,\sigma_r^{(i)}(b)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b}{E}\left[-\frac{P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2} - \nu(-P_c)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b}{E}\left[-\frac{P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2} + \nu P_c\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{a^2 + b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \nu\right] \\[6pt]
u_b^{(i)} &= \frac{-b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The negative sign indicates the inner cylinder's outer surface moves **inward** (radially) under the squeezing action of the outer cylinder.

---

### 3.1.2 Stage 1b: Stresses in the Outer Cylinder (loaded by internal pressure $P_c$)

**Setup:** Outer cylinder is treated as a thick cylinder with:
- Inner radius: $b$
- Outer radius: $c$
- Internal pressure: $P_i = P_c$ (the contact pressure pushing outward on the inner surface of the outer cylinder)
- External pressure: $P_o = 0$ (free outer surface)

**Step 4 — Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_r$:**

Substitute $P_i = P_c$, $P_o = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c \cdot b^2 - 0}{c^2 - b^2} - \frac{(P_c - 0)\cdot b^2 c^2}{(c^2 - b^2)\cdot r^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2} - \frac{P_c\, b^2 c^2}{(c^2 - b^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_r^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2}\left[1 - \frac{c^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Lame's general formula for $\sigma_\theta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2} + \frac{P_c\, b^2 c^2}{(c^2 - b^2)\, r^2} \\[6pt]
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(r) &= \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2}\left[1 + \frac{c^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** The outer cylinder is in **tangential tension** at the interface, with maximum tensile hoop stress at $r = b$ equal to $P_c(b^2 + c^2)/(c^2 - b^2)$.

**Step 6 — Radial displacement of outer cylinder at $r = b$:**

Evaluate stresses at $r = b$:
- $\sigma_r^{(o)}(b) = -P_c$ (matches the inner cylinder — Newton's 3rd law ✓)
- $\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) = \dfrac{P_c(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_b^{(o)} &= \frac{b}{E}\left[\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) - \nu\,\sigma_r^{(o)}(b)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b}{E}\left[\frac{P_c(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2} - \nu(-P_c)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{b^2 + c^2}{c^2 - b^2} + \nu\right] \\[6pt]
u_b^{(o)} &= \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual logic:** Positive value indicates the outer cylinder's inner surface moves **outward** (radially) due to internal pressurization from the contact force.

---

### 3.1.3 Compatibility Equation — Solving for $P_c$

**Step 7 — Geometric compatibility at the interface:**

The total radial interference $\delta$ must equal the difference in radial displacements of the two mating surfaces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= u_b^{(o)} - \left(-u_b^{(i)}\right) = u_b^{(o)} + \left\vert u_b^{(i)} \right\vert \\[6pt]
\delta &= \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right] + \frac{b P_c}{E}\left[\frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $\dfrac{b P_c}{E}$:**

$$
\delta = \frac{b P_c}{E}\left\{\frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2} + \frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}\right\}
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $P_c$:**

$$
\boxed{P_c = \frac{\delta\, E}{b}\left[\frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}\right]^{-1}}
$$

**Textual logic:** This is the **residual contact pressure** produced purely by the mechanical shrink fit. It is independent of any internal operating pressure.

---

## 3.2 Case B — Combined Shrink Fit and Internal Pressure $P_i$

### Superposition Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle of Superposition:** Total stress = (Stresses due to shrink fit alone) + (Stresses due to internal $P_i$ on a single equivalent cylinder).

**Step 10 — Stage 2 stress in the inner cylinder due to $P_i$ alone (treated as a thick cylinder with $a$ to $b$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r^{P_i}(r) &= \frac{P_i\, a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right] \\[6pt]
\sigma_\theta^{P_i}(r) &= \frac{P_i\, a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 — Stage 2 stress in the outer cylinder due to $P_c$ (at interface, $P_i = 0$ external):**

This is identical to the shrink-fit-only result derived in Step 4–5. However, here $P_c$ is **not** solely due to interference but is determined by the simultaneous action of $P_i$.

**Step 12 — Revised compatibility (radial displacements must match at $r = b$):**

Inner cylinder now sees internal $P_i$ and external $P_c$ simultaneously:

$$
u_b^{(i,\,total)} = \frac{b}{E}\left[\sigma_\theta^{(i,total)}(b) - \nu\,\sigma_r^{(i,total)}(b)\right]
$$

where:
- $\sigma_r^{(i,total)}(b) = -P_c$
- $\sigma_\theta^{(i,total)}(b) = \dfrac{P_i\, a^2 - P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(P_i - P_c)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)b^2} = \dfrac{P_i\, a^2 - P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{(P_i - P_c)\, a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$

Simplifying:
$$
\sigma_\theta^{(i,total)}(b) = \frac{P_i\, a^2(1 + b^2/b^2) - P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{2 P_i\, a^2 - P_c(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

**Step 13 — Final form of compatibility equation for combined loading:**

$$
\boxed{\delta = \frac{b}{E}\left[P_c\left(\frac{a^2(1+\nu)+b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2-a^2}+\frac{b^2(1-\nu)+c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2-b^2}\right) - P_i\left(\frac{2 a^2(1-\nu)+b^2(1+\nu) - 2a^2}{b^2-a^2}\right)\right]}
$$

Solving this for $P_c$ yields the **operating contact pressure**, which is **lower** than the assembly contact pressure (since the internal pressure pushes outward, partially relieving the interface compression).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Loading Stage | Inner Cylinder Loading | Outer Cylinder Loading | Interface Pressure |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Pre-pressurization (Assembly)** | External $P_c$ only | Internal $P_c$ only | $P_c = P_c^{assembly}$ (maximum) |
| **Operating (Internal $P_i$ applied)** | Internal $P_i$ + External $P_c$ | Internal $P_c$ only | $P_c = P_c^{operating} < P_c^{assembly}$ |
| **Net Effect on $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = a$** | Increases (tensile) | — | Reduced from monolithic peak |
| **Net Effect on $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = b$** | Decreases (compressive residual reduced) | Increases (tensile residual reduced) | Redistributes stress |

## 4.1 Geometry of a Two-Layer Shrink-Fit Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        R2["Outer radius b (interface)"]
        R1 --- R2

    subgraph OC["OUTER CYLINDER"]

        R4["Outer radius c (free surface, sigma_r = 0)"]
        R3 --- R4
    end

    IC --> OC

    F1["Residual Contact Pressure P_c acts radially inward on inner cylinder at r=b"] -.->|squeezes| R2
    F2["Residual Contact Pressure P_c acts radially outward on outer cylinder at r=b"] -.->|stretches| R3

    L1["Stage 1: Shrink fit creates compressive sigma_theta in inner cylinder"]
    L2["Stage 1: Shrink fit creates tensile sigma_theta in outer cylinder"]
    L3["Stage 2: Internal pressure P_i adds tensile sigma_theta to inner cylinder"]
    L4["Stage 2: Total stress is superposition of Stage 1 and Stage 2"]

    L1 --> IC
    L2 --> OC
    L3 --> IC
    L4 --> R1
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of Shrink Fit Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STEP 1: Machine inner cylinder to outer diameter d_b + delta"] --> B["STEP 2: Machine outer cylinder to inner diameter d_b"]
    B --> C["STEP 3: Heat outer cylinder uniformly above assembly temperature"]
    C --> D["STEP 4: Cool inner cylinder below ambient (optional)"]
    D --> E["STEP 5: Slide inner cylinder into outer cylinder with care for alignment"]
    E --> F["STEP 6: Allow temperature to equalize at room temperature"]
    F --> G["STEP 7: Outer cylinder contracts and inner cylinder expands, creating interference fit"]
    G --> H["STEP 8: Measure contact pressure P_c via strain gauges or compute from compatibility equation"]

    style A fill:#E8F4F8
    style H fill:#FFE5B4
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A1["sigma_r equals 0 (free surface)"]
        A2["sigma_theta equals MINUS P_c times a squared plus b squared divided by b squared minus a squared (highly compressive)"]
        A3["sigma_z equals nu times sigma_theta (axial, due to plane strain)"]
    end

    subgraph B["Stress State at Interface r equals b"]
        B1["sigma_r equals MINUS P_c (continuous across interface, equal and opposite)"]
        B2["sigma_theta jumps from compressive in inner to tensile in outer"]
        B3["Compatibility: u_b of inner equals MINUS u_b of outer numerically equal to delta"]
    end

    subgraph C["Stress State at Outer Surface r equals c"]
        C1["sigma_r equals 0 (free surface)"]
        C2["sigma_theta equals P_c times b squared plus c squared divided by c squared minus b squared (highly tensile)"]
        C3["This is the critical surface for failure analysis"]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "shrink fit" as applied to built-up pressure vessels. State two engineering advantages of using a shrink-fit construction over a monolithic cylinder.**

**Model Answer:**

A shrink fit is a method of assembling two or more concentric cylinders by creating a deliberate **radial interference** ($\delta$) at the common interface. This is achieved by heating the outer cylinder (causing thermal expansion), sliding the inner cylinder into position, and allowing temperature equalization. Upon cooling, the outer cylinder contracts onto the inner cylinder, generating a **residual contact pressure $P_c$** at the interface.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Stress redistribution:** The maximum tangential (hoop) stress in a monolithic cylinder occurs at the inner surface. A shrink fit introduces pre-compression in the inner cylinder, which **partially cancels** the tensile stress produced by internal pressure, thereby increasing the burst pressure capacity.
2. **Manufacturing feasibility:** Very large-diameter thick-walled vessels (e.g., for hydrocrackers, nuclear reactors) cannot be forged as a single piece. Shrink-fit construction allows layered assembly, overcoming forging size limitations.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Write down Lame's equations for radial and tangential (hoop) stresses in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$. Define all terms.**

**Model Answer:**

For a thick-walled cylinder with inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$:

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{P_i\, a^2 - P_o\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(P_i - P_o)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{P_i\, a^2 - P_o\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(P_i - P_o)\, a^2 b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}
$$

**Where:**
- $\sigma_r$ = radial stress at radius $r$
- $\sigma_\theta$ = tangential (hoop) stress at radius $r$
- $P_i$ = internal pressure, $P_o$ = external pressure
- $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius
- $r$ = radial coordinate where stress is evaluated

**Boundary conditions:** At $r = a$: $\sigma_r = -P_i$ (if $P_i$ is the applied internal pressure, by sign convention); at $r = b$: $\sigma_r = -P_o$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** A two-layer shrink-fit cylinder has the following dimensions: inner radius $a = 100$ **mm**, interface radius $b = 200$ **mm**, outer radius $c = 300$ **mm**. The radial interference is $\delta = 0.10$ **mm**. Calculate the **contact pressure $P_c$** generated by the shrink fit. Assume $E = 200$ **GPa**, $\nu = 0.3$. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** For the cylinder in part (a), determine the **maximum tangential stress in the inner cylinder** and the **maximum tangential stress in the outer cylinder** due to the shrink fit alone. Comment on which cylinder is more critical. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Given:**
- $a = 100$ **mm**, $b = 200$ **mm**, $c = 300$ **mm**
- $\delta = 0.10$ **mm**
- $E = 200 \times 10^3$ **MPa** = $200\,000$ **N/mm²** (since 1 GPa = 1000 N/mm² = 1000 MPa)
- $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1 — Compute the two bracket terms:**

Inner cylinder term:
$$
T_1 = \frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{(100)^2(1.3) + (200)^2(0.7)}{(200)^2 - (100)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10\,000 \times 1.3 + 40\,000 \times 0.7}{40\,000 - 10\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{13\,000 + 28\,000}{30\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{41\,000}{30\,000} = 1.3667
\end{aligned}
$$

Outer cylinder term:
$$
T_2 = \frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= \frac{(200)^2(0.7) + (300)^2(1.3)}{(300)^2 - (200)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{40\,000 \times 0.7 + 90\,000 \times 1.3}{90\,000 - 40\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{28\,000 + 117\,000}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{145\,000}{50\,000} = 2.9000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the contact pressure formula:**

$$
P_c = \frac{\delta\, E}{b\, (T_1 + T_2)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{0.10 \times 200\,000}{200 \times (1.3667 + 2.9000)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20\,000}{200 \times 4.2667} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20\,000}{853.33} \\[4pt]
P_c &= 23.4375 \text{ MPa} \approx 23.44 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $\boxed{P_c \approx 23.44 \text{ MPa}}$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Maximum hoop stress in the inner cylinder (occurs at $r = a$):**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(a) = -\frac{P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left[1 + \frac{a^2}{a^2}\right] = -\frac{2 P_c\, b^2}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(i)}(a) &= -\frac{2 \times 23.44 \times (200)^2}{(200)^2 - (100)^2} \\[4pt]
&= -\frac{2 \times 23.44 \times 40\,000}{30\,000} \\[4pt]
&= -\frac{1\,875\,200}{30\,000} \\[4pt]
&= -62.51 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum hoop stress in the outer cylinder (occurs at $r = b$):**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) = \frac{P_c\, b^2}{c^2 - b^2}\left[1 + \frac{c^2}{b^2}\right] = \frac{P_c(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{(o)}(b) &= \frac{23.44 \times ((200)^2 + (300)^2)}{(300)^2 - (200)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{23.44 \times (40\,000 + 90\,000)}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{23.44 \times 130\,000}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3\,047\,200}{50\,000} \\[4pt]
&= 60.94 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (b):**
- Inner cylinder: $|\sigma_\theta^{(i)}|_{max} = 62.51$ **MPa** (compressive) at $r = a$
- Outer cylinder: $|\sigma_\theta^{(o)}|_{max} = 60.94$ **MPa** (tensile) at $r = b$

**Comment:** Although the magnitudes are comparable, the **outer cylinder is more critical** because:
1. Its stress is **tensile** (the inner cylinder's is compressive, which is safer).
2. The **interface stress** of 60.94 MPa governs design, since failure (yielding) typically initiates at the highest tensile location.
3. Under operating pressure, the tensile stress in the outer cylinder will further **increase**, while the compressive stress in the inner cylinder will be partially relieved.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** A built-up cylinder consists of an inner liner (inner radius $a = 50$ **mm**, outer radius $b = 150$ **mm**) shrink-fitted into an outer shell (inner radius $b = 150$ **mm**, outer radius $c = 250$ **mm**). The radial interference is $\delta = 0.05$ **mm**. The assembly is subjected to an internal pressure of $P_i = 50$ **MPa**. Compute the **operating contact pressure** $P_c$ at the interface. Use $E = 200$ **GPa**, $\nu = 0.3$. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** If the same cylinder were made as a **monolithic** (single-piece) cylinder with the same dimensions ($a = 50$ **mm**, $b = 250$ **mm**), calculate the **maximum hoop stress** at the inner surface for the same internal pressure. Compare and comment on the **efficiency of the built-up construction**. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Given:**
- $a = 50$ **mm**, $b = 150$ **mm**, $c = 250$ **mm**
- $\delta = 0.05$ **mm**, $P_i = 50$ **MPa**
- $E = 200\,000$ **MPa**, $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1 — Compute coefficients $T_1$ and $T_2$ (same as before):**

$$
T_1 = \frac{a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{2500(1.3) + 22500(0.7)}{22500 - 2500} = \frac{3250 + 15750}{20000} = \frac{19000}{20000} = 0.95
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)}{c^2 - b^2} = \frac{22500(0.7) + 62500(1.3)}{62500 - 22500} = \frac{15750 + 81250}{40000} = \frac{97000}{40000} = 2.425
$$

**Step 2 — Write the combined compatibility equation:**

$$
\delta = \frac{b P_c}{E}(T_1 + T_2) - \frac{b P_i}{E}\left[\frac{2a^2}{b^2-a^2}\right]
$$

(The second term accounts for the radial expansion of the inner cylinder's outer surface due to $P_i$, which partially relieves the interference.)

**Step 3 — Compute the second term coefficient:**

$$
K = \frac{2a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{2 \times 2500}{22500 - 2500} = \frac{5000}{20000} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{b}{E}\left[P_c(T_1 + T_2) - P_i\, K\right] \\[4pt]
0.05 &= \frac{150}{200\,000}\left[P_c(0.95 + 2.425) - 50 \times 0.25\right] \\[4pt]
0.05 &= 7.5 \times 10^{-4}\left[3.375 P_c - 12.5\right] \\[4pt]
\frac{0.05}{7.5 \times 10^{-4}} &= 3.375 P_c - 12.5 \\[4pt]
66.667 &= 3.375 P_c - 12.5 \\[4pt]
3.375 P_c &= 79.167 \\[4pt]
P_c &= 23.46 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $\boxed{P_c^{operating} \approx 23.46 \text{ MPa}}$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Monolithic cylinder:** $a = 50$ **mm**, $b = 250$ **mm**, $P_i = 50$ **MPa**, $P_o = 0$.

**Maximum hoop stress occurs at $r = a$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{max} = \frac{P_i(a^2 + b^2)}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta^{max} &= \frac{50 \times (2500 + 62500)}{62500 - 2500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50 \times 65000}{60000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3\,250\,000}{60\,000} \\[4pt]
&= 54.17 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison and Comment:**

| Configuration | $\sigma_\theta$ at Inner Surface | Burst Pressure Capacity |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| **Monolithic** | +54.17 MPa (pure tensile) | Limited by inner surface |
| **Built-up with $P_c \approx 23.46$ MPa** | Net = $54.17 - $ (compressive component from $P_c$) | Significantly higher |

For the built-up cylinder, the **net inner surface hoop stress** is the superposition:
- Tensile from $P_i$: $+54.17$ **MPa**
- Compressive from $P_c$: $-P_c \times 2b^2/(b^2-a^2) = -23.46 \times 4.5 = -105.57$ **MPa**

**Net $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = a$ for built-up cylinder:**
$$
\sigma_\theta^{net}(a) = 54.17 - 105.57 = -51.40 \text{ MPa (still compressive)}
$$

**Comment:** The built-up cylinder keeps the **inner surface in compression** even under full operating pressure. This dramatically delays yielding and raises the **burst pressure** by approximately **60–80%** over the monolithic design. This is the key engineering benefit of shrink-fit construction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks in shrink-fit problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in displacement equations:** Forgetting that the inner cylinder's outer surface moves **inward** (negative radial displacement), so the total interference is the **algebraic sum** of the two displacements. Marks lost: 2–3 per part.
>
> 2. **Mixing up inner and outer cylinder terms:** Students sometimes apply the wrong sign for $(1+\nu)$ and $(1-\nu)$ in $T_1$ vs. $T_2$. **Memory aid:** The term with the **interface radius $b$** appears in **both** $T_1$ and $T_2$. In $T_1$, $b$ carries $(1-\nu)$; in $T_2$, $b$ carries $(1-\nu)$. Verify by checking dimensions.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** Mixing **mm** with **GPa** is a guaranteed zero. Always use **N/mm² = MPa** consistently, or convert everything to SI (Pa, m).
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if you do not state "plane strain," "long cylinder," "no body forces," etc.
>
> 5. **Confusing diametral vs. radial interference:** If a problem gives "diametral interference $\Delta$", the radial interference is $\delta = \Delta/2$. Marks lost: 2.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Poisson's effect:** Setting $\nu = 0$ gives a 5–10% error in $P_c$, which is unacceptable for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shrink Fit in Built-up Cylinders**

- **Definition:** Shrink fit = two cylinders joined by thermal interference generating residual contact pressure $P_c$ at the common interface.
- **Geometry:** $a$ (inner) $< b$ (interface) $< c$ (outer). Three radii define the system.
- **Lame's Equations:** Foundation of thick-cylinder analysis. Use with correct sign convention.
- **Sign Convention:** $\sigma_r$ and $\sigma_\theta$ tensile = positive; compressive = negative.
- **Contact pressure $P_c$ (pure shrink fit):** Function of $\delta$, $E$, $\nu$, and all three radii $a, b, c$.
- **Compatibility equation:** $\delta = u_b^{(o)} + \vert u_b^{(i)} \vert$ (the sum of outward expansion of outer cylinder's inner surface and inward contraction of inner cylinder's outer surface).
- **Inner cylinder stress state (shrink fit only):** Tangential **compression** throughout; maximum compressive $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = a$.
- **Outer cylinder stress state (shrink fit only):** Tangential **tension** throughout; maximum tensile $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = b$.
- **Operating contact pressure < Assembly contact pressure** because $P_i$ partially relieves interface compression.
- **Superposition principle:** Total stress = Shrink fit stress + Pressure-induced stress.
- **Design benefit:** Built-up cylinders exhibit **higher burst pressure** (typically 50–80% increase) and **more uniform stress distribution** than monolithic cylinders of equivalent size.
- **Critical surfaces for design check:**
  - Inner cylinder: $r = a$ (during operation, maximum tensile stress).
  - Outer cylinder: $r = b$ (interface, maximum tensile residual stress).
- **Plane strain assumption** is standard for long cylinders; replace $E \to E/(1-\nu^2)$ and $\nu \to \nu/(1-\nu)$ if using plane-stress formulas.
- **Standard units:** $E$ in **MPa** (= N/mm²) when radii are in mm; $\delta$ in mm; $P_c$ in MPa.
- **Memory aid for $T_1$ and $T_2$:** The term with the **squared radius that is NOT the interface** carries the $(1+\nu)$ factor; the term with the **interface radius** carries $(1-\nu)$. (Inner cylinder: $a^2(1+\nu) + b^2(1-\nu)$; Outer cylinder: $b^2(1-\nu) + c^2(1+\nu)$.)
- **Key advantage to remember for viva:** Pre-stressing shifts the maximum hoop stress **away from the bore**, enabling higher operating pressures than monolithic design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto Frettage of Thick Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Auto Frettage of Thick Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto-Frettage** is a pre-stressing technique applied to thick-walled cylindrical pressure vessels (or gun barrels) in which a controlled internal hydraulic pressure — higher than the initial yield pressure but lower than the bursting pressure — is applied so that the **inner fibers undergo plastic deformation** while the **outer fibers remain elastic**. When this autofrettage pressure is released, the elastic outer region attempts to recover elastically, but is restrained by the plastically deformed inner core. This induces a **residual compressive hoop stress** at the bore and a **residual tensile hoop stress** at the outer surface.

The cylinder is thereby pre-conditioned in a manner analogous to a **shrink-fit (built-up) cylinder**, but achieved through a single, monolithic, homogeneous forging — eliminating joints, interfaces, and the corresponding fatigue-prone discontinuities.

> [!NOTE]
> The term *frettage* derives from the French *fretter* (to hoop or bind). *Auto* implies self-generated prestress, as opposed to external mechanical interference.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Imagine a thick rubber tube that you stretch forcefully from the inside (by inflating it well beyond its safe limit) and then release. The inner wall remains slightly compressed, while the outer wall is held in slight tension — like a wrestler who has already exhausted part of his muscle elasticity *before* the actual match begins. When working (operational) pressure is later applied, the cylinder must first "undo" this built-in compression at the bore before any new tensile hoop stress can develop. This **delays the onset of yielding and fatigue cracking** at the inner surface, where stress concentrations and crack initiation are most dangerous.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

| Aspect | Impact of Auto-Frettage |
|---|---|
| Allowable working pressure | **Increases by 30 – 50 %** compared to an untreated thick cylinder of the same dimensions |
| Fatigue life | Bore hoop stresses become compressive → **suppresses crack propagation** |
| Wall thickness | Reduced for a given design pressure → **saves material and weight** |
| Burst pressure | Approaches twice the initial yield pressure |
| Manufacturing | Single forging (no shrink-fit, no welding) → **simpler QA, no interface** |

**Important standard / datum values to memorize:**

- Radius ratio: $K = b/a$, where $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius
- Tresca yield criterion: $\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r = \sigma_Y$
- Von Mises yield criterion (for closed-end cylinder): $\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r = \dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}$
- Yield pressure of a thick cylinder (full elastic limit): $p_Y = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}\ln(K^2)$ (von Mises, closed-end)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The autofrettage cycle consists of four distinct stages:

1. **Loading Stage (Elastic → Elastic-Plastic)**
   - Internal pressure $p_i$ is increased from 0.
   - At $p_i = p_Y$, the innermost fiber ($r = a$) just yields.
   - For $p_i > p_Y$, a plastic annulus grows outward from $r = a$ to a boundary $r = c$.
   - Region $a \le r \le c$: plastic (fully yielded, follows flow rule).
   - Region $c \le r \le b$: elastic (Lamé stress state).

2. **Hold Stage**
   - Pressure held at autofrettage value $p_f$ long enough for stable plastic flow.

3. **Unloading Stage**
   - $p_i$ reduced from $p_f$ to 0. Unloading is purely elastic (Bauschinger effect ignored in classical theory).

4. **Residual Stress State**
   - The cylinder is left with permanent residual stresses — the **autofrettage benefit**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason |
|---|---|
| Why apply pressure higher than yield? | To plastically deform the inner region, so that upon release the elastic recovery generates beneficial residual stresses. |
| Why not yield the entire wall? | If $c \to b$, residual stresses vanish — no benefit. Full plastic yielding defeats the purpose. |
| Why unload elastically? | Plastic flow is irreversible; elastic unloading produces the **mismatch** that becomes residual stress. |
| Why residual compression at bore? | Outer elastic zone "pulls inward" against the plastically elongated inner core → bore gets compressed. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below assume a **closed-end thick cylinder**, plane-strain-like axial stress $\sigma_z = (\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta)/2$, and use the **von Mises yield criterion**. Substitute $\sigma_Y$ for $\dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}$ everywhere for the **Tresca criterion**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Region / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yield constant (von Mises) | $Y_{VM} = \dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Used in plastic zone |
| 2 | Yield constant (Tresca) | $Y_T = \sigma_Y$ | Used in plastic zone |
| 3 | Plastic zone radial stress | $\sigma_r = Y \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{a}\right) - p_f$ | $a \le r \le c$ |
| 4 | Plastic zone hoop stress | $\sigma_\theta = Y\!\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{a}\right)\right] - p_f$ | $a \le r \le c$ |
| 5 | Elastic zone (during loading) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{Y\,c^2}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{b^2} - \dfrac{1}{r^2}\right)$ | $c \le r \le b$ |
| 6 | Elastic zone (during loading) | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{Y\,c^2}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{b^2} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\right)$ | $c \le r \le b$ |
| 7 | Autofrettage pressure | $p_f = Y \ln\!\left(\dfrac{c}{a}\right) + \dfrac{Y}{2}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{c^2}{b^2}\right)$ | Required for plastic radius $c$ |
| 8 | **Full autofrettage** ($c = b$) | $p_f = Y \ln(K)$ | Maximum pre-stress |
| 9 | Residual hoop stress (plastic zone) | $\sigma_\theta^{res} = \sigma_\theta^{loaded} - \sigma_\theta^{el,unload}$ | $a \le r \le c$ |
| 10 | Residual hoop stress (elastic zone) | $\sigma_\theta^{res} = \sigma_\theta^{Lam\acute{e},loaded} - \sigma_\theta^{el,unload}$ | $c \le r \le b$ |
| 11 | **Optimum autofrettage** | Inner fiber re-yields under operating $p_{op}$: $\sigma_\theta^{res}(a) + \sigma_\theta^{op}(a) = \sigma_Y$ | Used to size $c$ |
| 12 | Burst pressure (Hill-type) | $p_b \approx \dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}\ln(K)$ | Twice yield pressure |
| 13 | Lamé (elastic, internal pressure $p$) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{p\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{b^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Reference solution |
| 14 | Lamé (elastic, internal pressure $p$) | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{p\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{b^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Reference solution |

> [!NOTE]
> Symbol conventions used everywhere in this sheet: $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius, $c$ = elastic-plastic boundary radius during autofrettage, $K = b/a$, $p_f$ = autofrettage (pre-stress) pressure, $Y$ = yield constant (Tresca or von Mises), $\sigma_Y$ = uniaxial tensile yield stress.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Auto-frettage is standard in:
- **Gun barrels and howitzers** (the original application, dating to late 19th century).
- **High-pressure hydraulic cylinders** (e.g., water-jet cutting, isostatic pressing).
- **Reactor pressure vessels** for deep-sea submersibles and high-pressure chemical reactors.
- **Accumulator cylinders** in hydraulic systems operating at 350 – 700 bar.
- **Coal gasification / hydrocracking reactor barrels** where fatigue is a critical limit state.

The technique transforms a plain ASME-grade forged cylinder into a **fatigue-resistant pre-stressed component** without any change in material chemistry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Setup and Assumptions

- Cylindrical pressure vessel with **closed ends**.
- Internal radius $a$, external radius $b$.
- Axisymmetric loading; long cylinder assumption (plane strain in axial direction).
- Material is elastic-perfectly plastic (no strain hardening in classical theory).
- Loading is quasi-static; inertia and creep are neglected.
- Yield criterion: **Von Mises** (also showing Tresca results).

### 3.2 Equilibrium Equation (Always Valid)

From radial equilibrium of an axisymmetric thick cylinder:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0
$$

### 3.3 Stresses in the Plastic Zone ($a \le r \le c$) — During Autofrettage Loading

**Step 1 — Apply the von Mises yield criterion for a closed-end cylinder:**

$$
\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r = \frac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}} \equiv Y_{VM}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation:**

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = \frac{\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r}{r} = \frac{Y_{VM}}{r}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate with respect to $r$:**

$$
\int d\sigma_r = \int \frac{Y_{VM}}{r}\,dr \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_r = Y_{VM}\ln(r) + C_1
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the inner boundary condition $\sigma_r(a) = -p_f$:**

$$
-p_f = Y_{VM}\ln(a) + C_1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_1 = -p_f - Y_{VM}\ln(a)
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by combining the logarithms:**

$$
\sigma_r^{pl} = Y_{VM}\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{a}\right) - p_f
$$

**Step 6 — Obtain the hoop stress from the yield criterion:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{pl} = \sigma_r^{pl} + Y_{VM} = Y_{VM}\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)\right] - p_f
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both stresses are **independent of any elastic constants** ($E$, $\nu$) — they depend only on yield stress and the applied autofrettage pressure. This is the hallmark of fully plastic flow under internal pressure.

### 3.4 Stresses in the Elastic Zone ($c \le r \le b$) — During Autofrettage Loading

**Step 7 — Apply Lamé's elastic solution:**

$$
\sigma_r^{el} = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \qquad \sigma_\theta^{el} = A + \frac{B}{r^2}
$$

**Step 8 — Outer boundary condition $\sigma_r^{el}(b) = 0$:**

$$
0 = A - \frac{B}{b^2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad A = \frac{B}{b^2}
$$

**Step 9 — Apply the yield criterion at the elastic-plastic interface $r = c$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{el}(c) - \sigma_r^{el}(c) = Y_{VM}
$$

$$
\frac{2B}{c^2} = Y_{VM} \quad\Rightarrow\quad B = \frac{Y_{VM}\,c^2}{2}
$$

**Step 10 — Combine to get the elastic-zone stresses during loading:**

$$
\sigma_r^{el} = \frac{Y_{VM}\,c^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{b^2} - \frac{1}{r^2}\right)
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta^{el} = \frac{Y_{VM}\,c^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{b^2} + \frac{1}{r^2}\right)
$$

### 3.5 Autofrettage Pressure $p_f$ Required for Plastic Radius $c$

**Step 11 — Match radial stress at $r = c$ from both regions:**

$$
\sigma_r^{pl}(c) = \sigma_r^{el}(c)
$$

$$
Y_{VM}\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) - p_f = \frac{Y_{VM}\,c^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{b^2} - \frac{1}{c^2}\right) = \frac{Y_{VM}}{2}\left(\frac{c^2}{b^2} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 12 — Solve for $p_f$:**

$$
p_f = Y_{VM}\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) + \frac{Y_{VM}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{c^2}{b^2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,p_f = \frac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{c^2}{b^2}\right)\right]\,}
$$

**Step 13 — Special case: Full autofrettage (plastic zone reaches outer fiber, $c = b$):**

$$
p_f^{\text{full}} = \frac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}\ln(K)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Students frequently confuse *full* autofrettage ($c = b$) with *yield-at-bore* ($c = a$). The former means plastic deformation has penetrated to the outer surface; the latter is the initial yield threshold.

### 3.6 Residual Stresses After Unloading

**Step 14 — Recognize that unloading is purely elastic.** Reverse elastic stresses generated by releasing $p_f$ are those of a fully elastic thick cylinder under internal pressure $-p_f$ (i.e., $-p_f$ acting outward):

$$
\sigma_r^{\text{rev}}(r) = -\frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{rev}}(r) = -\frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

**Step 15 — Residual stress = (stresses during loading) + (reverse elastic stresses):**

For the **plastic zone** ($a \le r \le c$):

$$
\sigma_r^{\text{res}}(r) = Y_{VM}\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{a}\right) - p_f \;-\; \frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(r) = Y_{VM}\!\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)\right] - p_f \;-\; \frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

For the **elastic zone** ($c \le r \le b$):

$$
\sigma_r^{\text{res}}(r) = \frac{Y_{VM}\,c^2}{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{b^2} - \frac{1}{r^2}\right) \;-\; \frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(r) = \frac{Y_{VM}\,c^2}{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{b^2} + \frac{1}{r^2}\right) \;-\; \frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

### 3.7 Equilibrium Check of Residual Stress Field

A useful verification — at the bore $r = a$:

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(a) = Y_{VM} - p_f - \frac{2\,p_f\,a^2\,b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,a^2}\cdot\frac{1}{2} \;=\; Y_{VM} - p_f\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{b^2 - a^2}\right)
$$

A typical numerical outcome: $\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(a) < 0$, confirming **compressive residual hoop stress at the bore** — the entire goal of autofrettage.

### 3.8 Optimum Autofrettage Depth

**Step 16 — Design philosophy:** Choose $c$ such that when the operating pressure $p_{op}$ is applied, the inner fiber re-yields exactly:

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(a) + \sigma_\theta^{op}(a) = \sigma_Y
$$

**Step 17 — Substitute Lamé elastic stress for the operating state:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{op}(a) = \frac{2\,p_{op}\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2}
$$

**Step 18 — Solve iteratively (or via chart) for $c$:** This is the standard *optimum autofrettage* design used in gun-barrel engineering; designers usually express it graphically as $c/b$ versus $K$.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 numerical problems, you will most often be asked to: (i) compute $p_f$ for a given $c$, (ii) compute $c$ for a given $p_f$, or (iii) compute residual stresses at one specific radius.

### 3.9 Comparison Table — Tresca vs Von Mises Results

| Quantity | Tresca | Von Mises |
|---|---|---|
| Yield constant $Y$ | $\sigma_Y$ | $\dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155\,\sigma_Y$ |
| Full autofrettage pressure $p_f$ | $\sigma_Y\ln(K)$ | $\dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}\ln(K)$ |
| Yield pressure of elastic cylinder | $\sigma_Y\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{K^2}\right)\Big/2$ (Tresca, open-end) | $\dfrac{\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}\ln(K^2)$ (VM, closed-end) |
| Plastic-zone hoop stress | $\sigma_Y\!\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{a}\right)\right] - p_f$ | $\dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}\!\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r}{a}\right)\right] - p_f$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, **state the criterion explicitly** before substituting $Y$. Failing to do so is a guaranteed ½-mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — The Auto-Frettage Cycle

The following block diagram maps the four physical stages of the autofrettage process, the stress-state transitions at the bore, and the resulting residual stress profile across the wall.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Virgin Cylinder: a inner radius, b outer radius] --> A1[Stage 1: Apply Internal Pressure pi]
    A1 --> A2{pi compared with Yield Pressure pY}
    A2 -->|pi less than pY| A3[Elastic Lamé State across full wall]
    A3 --> A4[Hoop stress max at bore sigma theta equals pi times 2b squared by b squared minus a squared]
    A2 -->|pi greater than pY| A5[Plastic annulus grows from r=a to r=c]
    A5 --> A6[Plastic zone a to c: equilibrium plus von Mises yield]
    A6 --> A7[Elastic zone c to b: Lamé equations]
    A7 --> A8[Stage 2: Hold at Autofrettage Pressure pf]
    A8 --> A9[Stage 3: Release pf to Zero purely elastic unloading]
    A9 --> A10[Reverse elastic Lamé stresses superposed on loaded state]
    A10 --> A11[Stage 4: Residual Stress Field]
    A11 --> A12[Bore r equals a: residual hoop stress is compressive]
    A11 --> A13[Outer r equals b: residual hoop stress is tensile]
    A11 --> A14[Re-apply operating pressure pop]
    A14 --> A15[Bore re-yields only if design reaches optimum autofrettage depth]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Stress-Field Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Input: a, b, sigma Y, criterion] --> L2[Compute Yield Pressure pY]
    L2 --> L3[Select Autofrettage Depth c]
    L3 --> L4[Compute Autofrettage Pressure pf from derived formula]
    L4 --> L5[Compute Stresses in Plastic Zone a to c]
    L4 --> L6[Compute Stresses in Elastic Zone c to b]
    L5 --> L7[Compute Reverse Elastic Lamé Stresses from pf release]
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> L8[Superpose to obtain Residual Stresses]
    L8 --> L9[Verify Bore Compression and Outer Tension]
    L9 --> L10{Optimum check: re-yield under pop?}
    L10 -->|Yes| L11[Design Acceptable]
    L10 -->|No| L12[Adjust c and iterate]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of the Residual Hoop-Stress Profile

Although the Mermaid renderer used in this document does not natively produce 2-D stress-vs-radius plots, the following ASCII schematic conveys the qualitative shape of $\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(r)$ after autofrettage. (Vertical axis: residual hoop stress; horizontal axis: radial position.)

```
  σ_θ^res
      |
      |                                       ++++++++ (tension at outer fiber)
      |                                   ++++
      |                                +++
      |                              ++
      |                            +
      |                          +
      |                        +
      |                      +
      |                    +
      |                  +
      |                +
      |             ++
      |          ++++
      |______________________________ ZERO _____________
      |   ///////                  r
      | ////                       
      |// (compression at bore)        Radius →
   ---+--------+-----+-----+-----------------> r
     r = a     r = c                 r = b
     (Bore)  (Elasto-plastic        (Outer
              boundary)              surface)
```

**Key features of the curve:**

- At $r = a$: $\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}$ is **compressive** (negative).
- At $r = c$: $\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}$ passes through zero (transition).
- At $r = b$: $\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}$ is **tensile** (positive).
- Net radial force on any cross-section sums to zero (equilibrium).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define auto-frettage. Why is it employed in thick-walled cylinders?**

**Model Answer:**
Auto-frettage is a pre-stressing process in which a thick-walled cylinder is subjected to an internal pressure high enough to cause plastic yielding in the inner fibers while the outer fibers remain elastic. Upon release of this pressure, the elastic recovery of the outer region induces **residual compressive hoop stress at the bore** and **residual tensile hoop stress at the outer surface**.

It is employed to:
1. Increase the **fatigue life** of the cylinder by suppressing tensile hoop stress at the bore (the most fatigue-critical location).
2. Permit a **higher operating pressure** for the same wall thickness, or **reduce wall thickness** for the same operating pressure.
3. Achieve benefits of a built-up (shrink-fit) cylinder using a **single monolithic forging**, thereby eliminating welded/joined interfaces.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks reasons)*

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between full autofrettage and partial (optimum) autofrettage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Full Autofrettage | Partial / Optimum Autofrettage |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic zone extent | Extends to the outer surface ($c = b$) | Plastic zone confined to $a < c < b$ |
| Required pressure | $p_f = Y\ln(K)$ | Lower, given by $p_f = Y\ln(c/a) + (Y/2)(1 - c^2/b^2)$ |
| Residual stress benefit | Maximum compressive bore stress, but outer fibers are at zero stress | Tuned so that bore just re-yields under operating pressure |
| Risk | Overstraining outer fibers; possible micro-damage | Safer; standard in engineering practice |
| Practical use | Mostly theoretical reference | Used in real gun barrels and high-pressure vessels |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison with valid formulae)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`
**(a)** A closed-end thick-walled cylinder has an inner radius $a = 50$ mm and outer radius $b = 150$ mm. It is made of steel with uniaxial yield stress $\sigma_Y = 600$ MPa. Using the **von Mises** yield criterion, determine the **autofrettage pressure** $p_f$ required to develop a plastic zone extending to a radius $c = 100$ mm. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the same cylinder, compute the **residual hoop stress** at the bore ($r = a$) and at the elastic-plastic boundary ($r = c$) after releasing the autofrettage pressure. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Given:** $a = 50$ mm, $b = 150$ mm, $c = 100$ mm, $\sigma_Y = 600$ MPa, von Mises.

**Step 1 — Compute the von Mises yield constant:**

$$
Y_{VM} = \frac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2 \times 600}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1200}{1.7321} = 692.82 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the autofrettage pressure formula:**

$$
p_f = Y_{VM}\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) + \frac{Y_{VM}}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{c^2}{b^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm term:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{50}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second term:**

$$
\frac{c^2}{b^2} = \frac{100^2}{150^2} = \frac{10000}{22500} = 0.4444
$$

$$
\frac{Y_{VM}}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{c^2}{b^2}\right) = \frac{692.82}{2}(1 - 0.4444) = 346.41 \times 0.5556 = 192.45 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum to obtain $p_f$:**

$$
p_f = (692.82 \times 0.6931) + 192.45 = 480.21 + 192.45 = 672.66 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,p_f \approx 672.7 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 6 — Compute the reverse elastic Lamé constant for unloading:**

$$
\frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{672.66 \times 50^2}{150^2 - 50^2} = \frac{672.66 \times 2500}{22500 - 2500} = \frac{1681650}{20000} = 84.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Hoop stress in plastic zone during loading (at $r = a$):**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{pl}(a) = Y_{VM}\!\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\frac{a}{a}\right)\right] - p_f = Y_{VM} - p_f = 692.82 - 672.66 = 20.16 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 8 — Reverse elastic hoop stress at $r = a$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{rev}}(a) = -\frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right) = -84.08\!\left(1 + \frac{22500}{2500}\right) = -84.08 \times 10 = -840.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 9 — Residual hoop stress at the bore:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(a) = 20.16 - 840.8 = -820.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The residual compressive stress at the bore ($\approx -820$ MPa) is large in magnitude. In reality, this would be capped by the material's compression yield limit, since the classical theory neglects Bauschinger and strain-hardening effects.

**Step 10 — Hoop stress during loading at $r = c$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{pl}(c) = Y_{VM}\!\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right)\right] - p_f = 692.82(1 + 0.6931) - 672.66 = 692.82 \times 1.6931 - 672.66
$$

$$
= 1172.98 - 672.66 = 500.32 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 11 — Reverse elastic hoop stress at $r = c$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{rev}}(c) = -84.08\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{c^2}\right) = -84.08\!\left(1 + \frac{22500}{10000}\right) = -84.08 \times 3.25 = -273.26 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 12 — Residual hoop stress at $r = c$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(c) = 500.32 - 273.26 = +227.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 13 — Summary of results:**

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(a = 50\text{ mm}) \approx -820.6 \text{ MPa (compression)};\quad \sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(c = 100\text{ mm}) \approx +227.1 \text{ MPa (tension)}\,}
$$

The sign reversal confirms the **fundamental autofrettage benefit**: bore compression, outer tension.

---

#### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`
**(a)** With the aid of neat sketches, explain the **concept of auto-frettage**. Derive the expression for the **autofrettage pressure** required to produce a plastic zone of radius $c$ in a closed-end thick cylinder of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$, using the **Tresca yield criterion**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A steel cylinder has $a = 40$ mm, $b = 120$ mm, $\sigma_Y = 500$ MPa. Using the **Tresca criterion**, calculate:
   (i) The **full autofrettage pressure**.
   (ii) The **residual hoop stress at the bore** after full autofrettage and pressure release. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Concept (sketch description — refer Section 4):** The cylinder is loaded hydraulically to a pressure $p_f$ that exceeds the yield pressure $p_Y$. Plastic deformation initiates at the bore ($r = a$) and progresses radially outward to $r = c$. The outer annulus ($c \le r \le b$) remains elastic. Upon release, the elastic region attempts to recover but is restrained by the plastically enlarged bore, locking in a **compressive hoop stress at the inner surface**.

**Derivation using Tresca:**

**Step 1 — Yield criterion (Tresca):**

$$
\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r = \sigma_Y \equiv Y_T
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into equilibrium:**

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = \frac{\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r}{r} = \frac{Y_T}{r}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate with BC $\sigma_r(a) = -p_f$:**

$$
\sigma_r = Y_T\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{a}\right) - p_f, \qquad \sigma_\theta = Y_T\!\left[1 + \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)\right] - p_f
$$

**Step 4 — In elastic region ($c \le r \le b$), Lamé's solution applies:**

$$
\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2},\quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}
$$

With BCs $\sigma_r(b) = 0$ and $\sigma_\theta(c) - \sigma_r(c) = \sigma_Y$:

$$
A = \frac{Y_T\,c^2}{2b^2},\quad B = \frac{Y_T\,c^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Match radial stress at $r = c$:**

$$
Y_T\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) - p_f = \frac{Y_T}{2}\!\left(\frac{c^2}{b^2} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $p_f$:**

$$
\boxed{\,p_f = \sigma_Y\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{a}\right) + \frac{\sigma_Y}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{c^2}{b^2}\right)\,}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $a = 40$ mm, $b = 120$ mm, $\sigma_Y = 500$ MPa, Tresca criterion.

**(i) Full autofrettage pressure** ($c = b$):

$$
p_f^{\text{full}} = \sigma_Y\ln(K) = \sigma_Y\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = 500 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{120}{40}\right) = 500 \times \ln(3)
$$

$$
p_f^{\text{full}} = 500 \times 1.0986 = 549.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,p_f^{\text{full}} \approx 549.3 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

**(ii) Residual hoop stress at the bore:**

**Step A — Compute Lamé's reverse elastic coefficient:**

$$
\frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{549.3 \times 40^2}{120^2 - 40^2} = \frac{549.3 \times 1600}{14400 - 1600} = \frac{878880}{12800} = 68.66 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step B — Hoop stress in plastic zone at $r = a$ during loading:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{pl}(a) = \sigma_Y\!\left[1 + \ln(1)\right] - p_f = \sigma_Y - p_f = 500 - 549.3 = -49.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step C — Reverse elastic hoop stress at $r = a$:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{rev}}(a) = -\frac{p_f\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\!\left(1 + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right) = -68.66 \times \left(1 + \frac{14400}{1600}\right) = -68.66 \times 10 = -686.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step D — Residual hoop stress at the bore:**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(a) = -49.3 - 686.6 = -735.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_\theta^{\text{res}}(a = 40 \text{ mm}) \approx -735.9 \text{ MPa (compressive)}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** A residual compressive hoop stress of approximately $-736$ MPa exists at the bore, which is a large pre-stress (it exceeds the elastic limit in many steels, indicating that the classical theory gives the **upper bound**; in practice, strain hardening and the Bauschinger effect cap this value to roughly $-\sigma_Y$).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in autofrettage problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the second term** $\tfrac{Y}{2}(1 - c^2/b^2)$ in the autofrettage pressure formula — many students write only $p_f = Y\ln(c/a)$, which is correct **only** for full autofrettage ($c = b$). Deducted: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Tresca and von Mises constants** — substituting $\sigma_Y$ where $\frac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}}$ is required (or vice-versa). Always state the criterion explicitly at the top of your answer. Deducted: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing plastic-zone and elastic-zone Lamé formulas** — the Lamé solution applies only in the elastic region. In the plastic region, stresses follow $Y\ln(r/a) - p_f$. Deducted: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the equilibrium check at the elastic-plastic boundary** — at $r = c$, both radial and hoop stresses must be continuous. Forgetting this continuity equation leads to inconsistent answers. Deducted: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Treating residual stresses as the loaded-state stresses** — the residual state is computed **after** complete release of $p_f$. Including the operating pressure makes the problem a "re-pressurized autofrettaged cylinder" problem, which is different. Read the question carefully. Deducted: **3 marks**.
> 6. **Sign convention errors** — internal pressure is compressive on the inner wall ($\sigma_r(a) = -p_i$). Failing to use the negative sign gives wrong signs in every subsequent equation. Deducted: **1 mark per step affected**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Auto-Frettage of Thick Cylinders:**

- **Definition:** Autofrettage = self-induced pre-stress via over-pressure yielding of inner fibers.
- **Goal:** Produce residual compressive hoop stress at the bore to delay yielding and crack initiation.
- **Mechanism:** Plastic deformation at bore → elastic outer recovery → locked-in compressive stress at inner wall.
- **Key radii:** $a$ (inner), $b$ (outer), $c$ (elastic-plastic boundary during autofrettage).
- **Yield constant (von Mises, closed-end):** $Y = \dfrac{2\sigma_Y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155\sigma_Y$.
- **Yield constant (Tresca):** $Y = \sigma_Y$.
- **Autofrettage pressure for plastic depth $c$:**
  $p_f = Y\ln(c/a) + \dfrac{Y}{2}(1 - c^2/b^2)$.
- **Full autofrettage pressure ($c = b$):** $p_f = Y\ln(K)$.
- **Plastic-zone stresses (during loading):**
  $\sigma_r = Y\ln(r/a) - p_f$, $\sigma_\theta = Y[1 + \ln(r/a)] - p_f$.
- **Elastic-zone stresses (during loading):**
  $\sigma_r = (Yc^2/2)(1/b^2 - 1/r^2)$, $\sigma_\theta = (Yc^2/2)(1/b^2 + 1/r^2)$.
- **Residual stresses** = loaded stresses − reverse Lamé elastic stresses (with $-p_f$).
- **Residual bore hoop stress:** always **negative (compressive)** — primary design benefit.
- **Residual outer hoop stress:** always **positive (tensile)** — auxiliary effect.
- **Optimum autofrettage:** $c$ chosen so that bore re-yields at the design operating pressure.
- **Real-world uses:** Gun barrels, high-pressure hydraulic cylinders, isostatic pressing vessels, coal-gasification reactors.
- **Comparison vs. built-up (shrink-fit) cylinders:** Auto-frettage uses **one monolithic forging** — no interface → no fretting fatigue, simpler manufacture, no assembly tolerances.
- **Comparison vs. wire-wound cylinders:** Auto-frettage is simpler; wire-winding allows even higher pressures but is more expensive.
- **Failure mode shift:** Burst pressure $\approx 2 \times$ yield pressure; fatigue is the controlling limit state, not gross yielding.
- **KTU exam trap:** Always **state the yield criterion** at the top of the solution; always **include the second term** in $p_f$ for partial autofrettage; always **superpose** unloaded elastic Lamé stresses to get residual stresses.

**Final memory hook:** *Inner bore wants to push outward after plastic flow, but elastic outer ring holds it back → bore compression. This single sentence captures the entire physics of autofrettage.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Reinforced Opening (Familiarisation of Corresponding ASME Section 8 Codes for Reinforcement Analysis)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Theory of Reinforced Opening (Familiarisation of Corresponding ASME Section 8 Codes for Reinforcement Analysis) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (ASME Section VIII, Div. 1 — UG-37) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reinforced Opening (ASME UG-37):** > An opening cut through the shell or formed head of a pressure vessel is termed a **reinforced opening** when the material removed to form the hole is compensated by *additional load-bearing area* so placed around the opening that the **net load-carrying cross-section** in the pressure-loaded direction is at least equal to the **load-carrying cross-section** that would have existed had no opening been made. In other words, the designer must prove that: $$A_{\text{available}} \;\geq\; A_{\text{required}}$$ where the **area of reinforcement required** $A$ is computed from the diameter of the hole, the required shell thickness, and a correction factor $F$, while $A_{\text{available}}$ is the algebraic sum of surplus areas in the **shell/head, nozzle wall, reinforcing pad, welds**, and any **integral forging**. The corresponding ASME paragraph that governs this check is **UG-37 (Reinforcement of Openings in Vessels Under Internal Pressure)**, supplemented by: - **UG-36** — Openings and reinforcements (general) - **UG-38** — Reinforcement required for openings in flat heads - **UG-39** — Reinforcement of openings in formed heads - **UG-40** — Reinforcement of openings — limits of reinforcement zone - **UG-41** — Reinforcement of multiple openings - **UG-42** — Reinforcement of openings — special methods ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Wooden Barrel & the Plug Imagine a wooden barrel made of vertical **staves** (the shell). Each stave carries a vertical compressive load equal to the hydrostatic pressure inside the barrel. Now drill a hole through one stave — you have just removed a slice of load-bearing wood. The remaining stave is now over-stressed and could split. > **Fix #1** — Use a thicker piece of wood at that location. → *Excess shell thickness or a thicker nozzle wall* > > **Fix #2** — Bolt a thick steel saddle on the outside of the barrel, covering the hole. → *Reinforcing pad (repad)* > > **Fix #3** — Carve a thicker boss into the wood before drilling. → *Integral reinforcement (forged nozzle)* > > **Fix #4** — Glue a ring around the hole. → *Weld metal (limited credit)* In pressure vessel language, the **load path is circumferential (hoop) stress** in a cylindrical shell. Cutting a hole severs hoop fibers; the reinforcement restores the missing cross-section by providing equivalent material in the same **load-parallel direction** within a defined **reinforcement zone**. ## 1.3 Why Reinforcement Is Mandatory | Failure Mode if Not Reinforced | Physical Consequence | |-------------------------------|----------------------| | Hoop-stress concentration at hole edge | Local yielding, fatigue cracking | | Gross area deficiency around opening | Rupture along the line of the opening | | Stress magnification under cyclic loading | Crack initiation at nozzle/shell junction | | Bending due to unsupported ligaments | Buckling of flat regions near the hole | > [!NOTE] > **Core Principle:** *Material removed for an opening must be replaced, in kind or in compensating geometry, within a narrowly defined zone surrounding the opening.* The total **cross-sectional area parallel to the vessel wall surface** in this zone, after subtracting what is needed to carry pressure, must equal or exceed the area that would have existed without the hole. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Nomenclature (ASME Convention) - **$d$** — finished (i.e., corroded) diameter of the opening, **in mm or in**. - **$D$** — inside diameter of the vessel shell, **in mm or in**. - **$t$** — nominal thickness of shell/head, **in mm or in**. - **$t_r$** — required thickness of shell/head (from UG-27, etc.), **in mm or in**. - **$t_n$** — nominal wall thickness of nozzle, **in mm or in**. - **$t_{nr}$** — required nozzle wall thickness (from pipe code or UG-27), **in mm or in**. - **$t_e$** — corrosion allowance (added in addition to $t_r$), **typically 1.5–3 mm**. - **$t_p$** — thickness of reinforcing pad (if any), **in mm or in**. - **$F$** — correction factor for the type of vessel (1.0 for shells, heads, spheres). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conceptual Flow of the UG-37 Reinforcement Check The reinforcement calculation proceeds in a strict, ordered sequence: 1. **Establish geometry** — Inside diameters, thicknesses, finished hole diameter $d$. 2. **Compute required thicknesses** — $t_r$ (shell), $t_{nr}$ (nozzle) from the appropriate formulas in UG-27. 3. **Compute area of reinforcement required** $A$ from $d$, $t_r$, and $F$. 4. **Identify the limits of the reinforcement zone** — both along the vessel wall and normal to it. 5. **Compute each contributing area** available: $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5$. 6. **Sum the available areas** $A_{\text{avail}} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + A_4 + A_5$. 7. **Compare** $A_{\text{avail}}$ against $A$. If $A_{\text{avail}} \geq A$, the opening is **adequately reinforced**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The five "areas of reinforcement" $A_1$ through $A_5$ are the *only* load-carrying contributions recognised by UG-37. Any material falling outside the reinforcement zone or in the wrong direction **cannot** be credited. ## 2.2 The Five Contributing Areas — What Each Represents | Area | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Geometry | |------|--------|------------------|----------| | Excess shell/head area | $A_1$ | Material in the vessel wall *over and above* $t_r$ | In vessel wall, within zone | | Excess nozzle-wall area | $A_2$ | Material in the nozzle pipe wall *over and above* $t_{nr}$ | In nozzle wall, within zone, *outside* the opening | | Reinforcing-pad area | $A_3$ | Area of the welded pad (or integral forging extension) | In the pad, within zone | | Weld-metal area | $A_4$ | Weld metal in the joint, beyond the minimum required weld | In weld cross-section, within zone | | Integral forging area | $A_5$ | Material of an integrally forged boss extending into the reinforcement zone | In forged boss, within zone | > [!NOTE] > $A_4$ is the *least* efficient contributor and is the only one for which the code limits the credit to the **minimum weld size required by UG-41** plus any excess beyond that minimum. Some interpretations cap $A_4 \leq A_{4,\text{limit}}$. ## 2.3 Limits of the Reinforcement Zone (UG-40) The reinforcement zone is the region around the opening within which any load-carrying material may be counted. It has **two extents** that must each be satisfied: ### 2.3.1 Parallel to the Vessel Wall Surface The zone extends, on each side of the centreline of the opening, a distance equal to the **greater of**: $$L_p = \max \left(\, d,\; R_n + t_n \,\right)$$ or, for **replacement** openings, $L_p = \max(d,\; R_n + t_n + \text{insertion depth})$. Here $R_n$ is the inside radius of the nozzle. > In practice, for a typical branch on a pipe of moderate wall, $L_p \approx d$ governs. ### 2.3.2 Normal to the Vessel Wall Surface The zone extends **inward** from the inside surface of the vessel to a total depth that depends on which material is available: - For $A_1$ (shell): only the shell thickness **above** the required thickness $t_r$ is credited (i.e., $t - t_r$). - For $A_2$ (nozzle): only material lying **above** the required nozzle thickness $t_{nr}$ is credited. - The total normal extent measured **outward from the outside of the vessel wall** is bounded, but the inward direction is bounded by the inside of the nozzle. > **Practical rule:** credit only those material portions that lie *within* the parallel limit and *above* the required thickness on each side of the opening. ## 2.4 The "F" Factor (Correction Factor) | Vessel Geometry | Value of $F$ | |----------------|--------------| | Cylindrical shell (longitudinal joint) | **1.00** | | Cylindrical shell (circumferential joint) | **1.00** | | Spherical shell | **1.00** | | Conical shell | **1.00** | | Formed heads (torispherical, ellipsoidal, hemispherical) — at centroid | **1.00** | | Flat head — full face | **special per UG-39** | For most practical problems in Module 2, **F = 1.0** and the formula reduces to: $$A = d \cdot t_r$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description | |---|----------|-------------| | 1 | $A = d \cdot t_r \cdot F$ | **Area of reinforcement required** | | 2 | $A_{\text{avail}} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + A_4 + A_5$ | Total reinforcement area available | | 3 | $A_{\text{avail}} \geq A$ | Reinforcement adequacy check | | 4 | $A_1 = (t - t_r)\,d - 2\,t_n\,(t - t_r)$ | Excess shell area within zone (typical form) | | 5 | $A_2 = 2\,(t_n - t_{nr})\,L_p$ | Excess nozzle wall area | | 6 | $A_3 = L_p \cdot t_p$ | Reinforcing pad area | | 7 | $A_4 \leq \text{min. weld size required}$ | Weld-metal credit (capped) | | 8 | $L_p = \max(d,\; R_n + t_n)$ | Parallel limit of reinforcement zone | | 9 | $t_r = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P}$ | Required shell thickness (UG-27, cylindrical) | | 10 | $t_{nr} = \dfrac{P \cdot r_n}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P}$ | Required nozzle wall thickness (thin-shell pipe) | | 11 | $r_n = d/2 - t_n$ | Inside radius of nozzle pipe | > [!NOTE] > **Pipe:** $P$ = design internal pressure, $R$ = inside radius of shell, $S$ = allowable stress at design temperature, $E$ = joint efficiency. ## 2.6 Why the Reinforcement Zone Is Limited The zone limits exist for two engineering reasons: 1. **Stress distribution** — Beyond the zone, the stress field has redistributed to its undisturbed, far-field value. Material placed there cannot effectively share the load. 2. **Bending and shear** — Material too far from the hole cannot transfer shear efficiently to the load-bearing fibres; hence it is conservatively ignored. ## 2.7 Multiple Openings and "Pitch" When two openings are close enough that their reinforcement zones **overlap**, the available area in the overlap must be apportioned — UG-41 treats this case explicitly. The rule of thumb is: > **Pitch $p$ ≥ sum of radii of the two reinforcement zones** ⇒ independent design. > **Pitch $p$ < sum of radii** ⇒ combine as a single composite opening (or enlarge reinforcement). The "limit line" of the reinforcement zone is typically elliptical, but for hand-calculations the **parallel-to-shell projection** of the zone is treated as a rectangle of width $2L_p$ centred on the opening. ## 2.8 Utility in Real Engineering - **Oil & Gas** — every branch connection on a separator, heat exchanger, or reactor is a reinforced opening. - **Power** — boiler drums, headers, and feed-pipe penetrations. - **Chemical** — reactor nozzles, manways, instrument tappings. - **Pharmaceutical / Cryogenic** — every LNG vessel nozzle and clean-in-place (CIP) port. > Every Code-stamped vessel drawing you will see carries a **"Nozzle Schedule"** whose last column reads *"Reinforcement: A_req / A_avail — OK"*. That single number traces back to UG-37. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Area of Reinforcement Required ($A = d \cdot t_r \cdot F$) ### 3.1.1 Conceptual Basis Consider an infinite plate under uniaxial tension $\sigma$ with a circular hole of diameter $d$. The load that would have been carried by the missing cross-section is: $$F_{\text{missing}} = \sigma \cdot t \cdot d$$ The reinforcement philosophy of UG-37 uses a slightly more conservative notion: it does **not** use the actual membrane stress; it uses the stress associated with the **required** thickness $t_r$. This keeps the rule independent of the joint efficiency and allowable-stress choices, focusing purely on **geometry**. ### 3.1.2 Derivation Step 1: The required thickness of the shell under internal pressure (cylindrical, circumferential stress) is obtained from UG-27(c)(1): $$t_r = \dfrac{P R}{S E - 0.6 P}$$ Step 2: For a width $d$ of shell that has been cut out, the load that the code "would like" the shell to carry is: $$F_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_{\text{allow, hoop}} \cdot t_r \cdot d$$ Step 3: The required reinforcement area is the area equivalent to that load at the **shell's allowable stress level**, so: $$A = \dfrac{F_{\text{ideal}}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}} = t_r \cdot d$$ Step 4: For geometries with non-uniform stress distribution (formed heads at the centre, flat heads, etc.), the correction factor $F$ is introduced: $$A = d \cdot t_r \cdot F$$ > **Conclusion:** $A$ is the *equivalent cross-sectional area*, in the plane of the vessel wall, that must exist within the reinforcement zone to replace the missing material. ## 3.2 Derivation of Excess-Shell Area $A_1$ ### 3.2.1 Geometric Setup Let the parallel limit of the reinforcement zone be $L_p$ on **each side** of the centreline, giving a **total parallel length** $= 2 L_p$. Within this strip of the shell of nominal thickness $t$, the area is: $$A_{\text{total strip}} = 2 L_p \cdot t$$ ### 3.2.2 Subtracting the Openings and the Nozzle Walls The shell material that is *not* available because it has been drilled out (the hole itself, plus the two nozzle-wall projections through the shell thickness) is: $$A_{\text{hole}} = d \cdot t$$ $$A_{\text{nozzle-proj}} = 2 t_n \cdot t \quad \text{(two slots where nozzle wall intersects the shell)}$$ ### 3.2.3 Subtracting the Required Portion Within the reinforcement zone, the shell is required to provide $d \cdot t_r$ of load-bearing area. Therefore the *excess* available is: $$A_1 = 2 L_p \cdot t - d \cdot t - 2 t_n \cdot (t - t_r) - \text{(required portion)}$$ For the common simplification $L_p \approx d$, and crediting only material **above** the required thickness: $$A_1 = (t - t_r) \cdot d - 2 t_n (t - t_r)$$ > **Physical reading:** $A_1$ is the rectangular strip of "extra shell" minus the portions consumed by the hole and by the nozzle-wall intersection. ## 3.3 Derivation of Excess-Nozzle Area $A_2$ The nozzle-wall area projecting **outward** from the vessel wall, within the parallel limit $L_p$ and **outside** the opening diameter, has a length $2L_p$ and a thickness $(t_n - t_{nr})$ — only the *excess* above the required nozzle thickness is credited: $$A_2 = 2 \cdot (t_n - t_{nr}) \cdot L_p$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Pad Area $A_3$ The reinforcing pad, when used, sits on the outside of the vessel and covers the opening within the parallel limit $L_p$ (extending $L_p$ on each side). Its contributing area is the rectangle: $$A_3 = L_p \cdot t_p \quad \text{(single-side pad; double for two-sided pad)}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark) ### 3.5.1 Problem Statement > A **branch nozzle** of nominal size **DN 200 (outside diameter 219.1 mm)** is welded to a **cylindrical shell** of inside diameter $D = 1500$ mm, nominal shell thickness $t = 18$ mm. The nozzle pipe has a nominal wall thickness $t_n = 12.5$ mm, and the design internal pressure is $P = 3.0$ MPa at $300 °C$. Allowable stress $S = 137$ MPa, joint efficiency $E = 1.0$, corrosion allowance $= 1.5$ mm. Verify the reinforcement using UG-37. ### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Finished hole diameter** (subtract corrosion allowance from $d$ once on each side of the wall): $$d = 219.1 + 2(1.5) = 222.1 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Required shell thickness** (UG-27, circumferential): $$t_r = \dfrac{P R}{S E - 0.6 P}$$ with $R = D/2 = 750$ mm: $$t_r = \dfrac{(3.0)(750)}{(137)(1.0) - 0.6(3.0)} = \dfrac{2250}{137 - 1.8} = \dfrac{2250}{135.2} = 16.64 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Required nozzle thickness** (treat as thin pipe): $$r_n = d/2 - t_n = 111.05 - 12.5 = 98.55 \text{ mm}$$ $$t_{nr} = \dfrac{P r_n}{S E - 0.6 P} = \dfrac{(3.0)(98.55)}{135.2} = \dfrac{295.65}{135.2} = 2.187 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Area of reinforcement required:** $$A = d \cdot t_r \cdot F = (222.1)(16.64)(1.0) = 3696 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 5 — Parallel limit of reinforcement zone:** Inside radius of nozzle: $$R_n = r_n = 98.55 \text{ mm}$$ $$L_p = \max(d,\; R_n + t_n) = \max(222.1,\; 98.55 + 12.5) = \max(222.1,\; 111.05) = 222.1 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 — Excess shell area $A_1$:** $$A_1 = (t - t_r)\,d - 2 t_n (t - t_r)$$ $$A_1 = (18 - 16.64)(222.1) - 2(12.5)(18 - 16.64)$$ $$A_1 = (1.36)(222.1) - (25.0)(1.36)$$ $$A_1 = 302.06 - 34.00 = 268.06 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 7 — Excess nozzle area $A_2$:** $$A_2 = 2 (t_n - t_{nr}) L_p = 2(12.5 - 2.187)(222.1)$$ $$A_2 = 2(10.313)(222.1) = 4581 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 8 — Pad area $A_3$:** none used in this example. $A_3 = 0$. **Step 9 — Weld area $A_4$:** credit the minimum weld size required; for typical fillet, $A_4 \approx 0$ in hand calculation (use code value if required). **Step 10 — Compare:** $$A_{\text{avail}} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + A_4 = 268.06 + 4581 + 0 + 0 = 4849 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_{\text{avail}} = 4849 \text{ mm}^2 \;\geq\; A = 3696 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$ > **Conclusion:** The opening is **adequately reinforced** with a substantial margin of $\approx 31\%$. The large margin indicates the branch pipe (12.5 mm) was sized primarily for pipe-stress and pressure containment, not for shell reinforcement — a typical outcome. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls in Calculation | Common Student Error | Correct Treatment | Penalty in Board Exam | |---------------------|-------------------|----------------------| | Using nominal $d$ instead of finished $d$ | Add **2 × corrosion allowance** to OD | 2–3 marks | | Using nominal $t$ instead of $t_r$ | Compute $t_r$ from UG-27 | 3–4 marks | | Forgetting the $2t_n(t-t_r)$ term in $A_1$ | Subtract nozzle-wall intersection area | 2 marks | | Crediting pad area outside the parallel limit $L_p$ | $A_3$ capped at $L_p \cdot t_p$ | 2 marks | | Using $F = 1$ for a flat head | For flat heads, UG-39 governs | 4–5 marks | | Ignoring multiple-opening overlap | Apply UG-41 rules | 3–4 marks | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reinforcement Zone Geometry The reinforcement zone, viewed from **outside the vessel wall**, is shown as the rectangular envelope of width $2 L_p$ around the opening. Material within this envelope and **above** the required thickness contributes to $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5$. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Shell / Head Wall (nominal thickness t)"]:::shell B["Reinforcement Zone Envelope
Parallel limit Lp on each side
Normal limit bounded by required thicknesses"]:::zone C["Finished Hole
Diameter d"]:::hole D["Nozzle Pipe Wall
Thickness tn"]:::nozzle E["Reinforcing Pad
Thickness tp
A3 = Lp x tp"]:::pad F["Weld Metal
A4 capped by min weld"]:::weld G["Load Path: Hoop Stress
Material above tr and tnr counted"]:::load A --> B B --> C B --> D B --> E B --> F B --> G classDef shell fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef zone fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef hole fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef nozzle fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef pad fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef weld fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef load fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — UG-37 Reinforcement Check ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1: Define geometry
D, d, t, tn, tp"]:::step S2["Step 2: Compute tr from UG-27
tr = P R / SE - 0.6P"]:::step S3["Step 3: Compute tnr for nozzle"]:::step S4["Step 4: Determine parallel limit Lp
Lp = max d, Rn + tn"]:::step S5["Step 5: Compute required area A
A = d tr F"]:::step S6["Step 6: Compute available areas
A1, A2, A3, A4, A5"]:::step S7["Step 7: Sum Aavail
Aavail greater or equal A?"]:::check S8["Step 8A: OK - Reinforced"]:::ok S9["Step 8B: Increase pad or tn
Reiterate"]:::revise S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 S7 -- YES --> S8 S7 -- NO --> S9 S9 --> S6 classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef check fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef ok fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef revise fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture — Components Contributing to Reinforcement ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VESSEL["VESSEL SHELL / HEAD"] SHELL["Shell Thickness t
Required tr
A1 from Excess Above tr"] end subgraph NOZZLE["BRANCH NOZZLE"] NPIPE["Pipe Wall tn
Required tnr
A2 from Excess Above tnr"] end subgraph PAD["REINFORCING PAD"] RPAD["Pad tp
Lies Outside Shell
A3 within Parallel Limit"] end subgraph WELD["WELD JOINT"] WJP["Fillet Weld
Min size governed by UG-41
A4 limited to weld metal"] end subgraph FORGE["INTEGRAL FORGING"] IBOSS["Forged Boss
A5 from Boss within Zone"] end SHELL --> CHECK["Total Reinforcement
A1 + A2 + A3 + A4 + A5"] NPIPE --> CHECK RPAD --> CHECK WJP --> CHECK IBOSS --> CHECK CHECK --> CRITERIA["Must Satisfy
Aavail greater or equal A = d tr F"] classDef vesselNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef nozzleNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef padNode fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef weldNode fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef forgeNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef checkNode fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef criteriaNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Layout — Multi-Opening Reinforcement Zones ```mermaid flowchart LR O1["Opening 1
Diameter d1
Zone width 2 Lp1"]:::op1 O2["Opening 2
Diameter d2
Zone width 2 Lp2"]:::op2 OV["Overlap Region
Material shared
Apportion per UG-41"]:::ov P["Pitch p
centre to centre"]:::pitch O1 --- OV --- O2 O1 --- P --- O2 classDef op1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef op2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef ov fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef pitch fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Marks are lost primarily for **two reasons**: (a) using *nominal* $d$ instead of *finished* $d$ (i.e., forgetting $2 \times$ corrosion allowance), and (b) forgetting to compute $t_r$ from UG-27 before plugging into $A$. The Code requires $t_r$, not $t$. > - Do **not** credit material that lies **outside** the parallel limit $L_p$ or **below** the required thickness.
