Many students over-credit $A_3$ by including pad material beyond $L_p$. > - For multiple openings, if you ignore the overlap rule (UG-41), you will under- or over-design by 20–30%. Always state whether the openings are independent or interacting. > - Always write the **final verdict** explicitly: "Hence $A_{\text{avail}} = \ldots \geq A = \ldots$ → **Reinforcement is adequate.**" A conclusion without a numeric statement loses the last mark. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the term "area of reinforcement required" as used in ASME Section VIII, Division 1, UG-37. State the formula and identify each term.** **Model Answer:** The **area of reinforcement required** $A$ is the load-carrying cross-sectional area, parallel to the vessel wall surface, that must exist within the reinforcement zone around an opening to compensate for the material removed in cutting the hole. $$A = d \cdot t_r \cdot F$$ where - $d$ = finished (corrosion-allowance-included) diameter of the opening, - $t_r$ = required thickness of the shell or head from UG-27, - $F$ = correction factor (= 1.0 for cylindrical, spherical, conical shells, and formed heads at the centreline). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List the five areas of reinforcement recognised by UG-37 and state, in one line, the physical meaning of each.** **Model Answer:** | Area | Meaning | |------|---------| | $A_1$ | Excess area in the shell/head wall above the required thickness $t_r$. | | $A_2$ | Excess area in the nozzle wall above the required nozzle thickness $t_{nr}$. | | $A_3$ | Area of the reinforcing pad lying within the reinforcement zone. | | $A_4$ | Area of the weld metal above the minimum size required by UG-41. | | $A_5$ | Area of an integral forging (boss) extending into the reinforcement zone. | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` A branch nozzle of **outside diameter 168.3 mm (DN 150)** with a nominal wall thickness $t_n = 11.0$ mm is welded to a cylindrical shell of **inside diameter 1200 mm** and nominal thickness $t = 16$ mm. Design pressure $P = 2.5$ MPa at $250 °C$. Allowable stress $S = 138$ MPa, joint efficiency $E = 1.0$, corrosion allowance $= 2.0$ mm. The designer proposes a **reinforcing pad of 12 mm thickness** welded on the outside. - **(a)** Compute the area of reinforcement required and the parallel limit of the reinforcement zone. - **(b)** Compute $A_1$, $A_2$, $A_3$ and determine whether the proposed reinforcement is adequate. #### Model Solution **(a)** Finished hole diameter: $$d = 168.3 + 2(2.0) = 172.3 \text{ mm}$$ Required shell thickness: $$t_r = \dfrac{P R}{S E - 0.6 P} = \dfrac{(2.5)(600)}{(138)(1.0) - 0.6(2.5)} = \dfrac{1500}{138 - 1.5} = \dfrac{1500}{136.5} = 10.99 \text{ mm}$$ Area of reinforcement required (F = 1): $$A = d \cdot t_r \cdot F = (172.3)(10.99)(1.0) = 1893.6 \text{ mm}^2$$ Inside radius of nozzle: $$R_n = \frac{172.3}{2} - 11.0 = 86.15 - 11.0 = 75.15 \text{ mm}$$ Parallel limit: $$L_p = \max(d,\; R_n + t_n) = \max(172.3,\; 75.15 + 11.0) = \max(172.3,\; 86.15) = 172.3 \text{ mm}$$ **(b)** Excess shell area $A_1$: $$A_1 = (t - t_r) d - 2 t_n (t - t_r)$$ $$A_1 = (16 - 10.99)(172.3) - 2(11.0)(16 - 10.99)$$ $$A_1 = (5.01)(172.3) - (22.0)(5.01)$$ $$A_1 = 863.22 - 110.22 = 753.0 \text{ mm}^2$$ Excess nozzle area $A_2$: Required nozzle thickness: $$t_{nr} = \dfrac{P R_n}{S E - 0.6 P} = \dfrac{(2.5)(75.15)}{136.5} = \dfrac{187.875}{136.5} = 1.376 \text{ mm}$$ $$A_2 = 2 (t_n - t_{nr}) L_p = 2(11.0 - 1.376)(172.3)$$ $$A_2 = 2(9.624)(172.3) = 3316.7 \text{ mm}^2$$ Reinforcing-pad area $A_3$: $$A_3 = L_p \cdot t_p = (172.3)(12) = 2067.6 \text{ mm}^2$$ Total available area (ignoring $A_4$): $$A_{\text{avail}} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 = 753.0 + 3316.7 + 2067.6 = 6137.3 \text{ mm}^2$$ Verdict: $$A_{\text{avail}} = 6137.3 \text{ mm}^2 \;\geq\; A = 1893.6 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$ **Reinforcement is adequate** with a margin of $(6137.3/1893.6 - 1)\times 100\% \approx 224\%$. > The very large margin indicates the reinforcing pad is **over-designed**. A thinner pad (say 6 mm) would still satisfy the check; the student should comment on this in a design-engineering sense. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` A **radial nozzle** of **OD 114.3 mm (DN 100)** with **$t_n = 8.0$ mm** penetrates a cylindrical shell of **$D = 900$ mm**, **$t = 12$ mm**. Design pressure $P = 4.0$ MPa at $200 °C$. Allowable stress $S = 144$ MPa, $E = 1.0$, corrosion allowance $= 1.5$ mm. - **(a)** Compute the required reinforcement area and the limits of the reinforcement zone. *(7 marks)* - **(b)** With **no reinforcing pad**, determine whether the shell and nozzle alone provide adequate reinforcement. If not, compute the **minimum pad thickness** required. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a)** Finished hole diameter: $$d = 114.3 + 2(1.5) = 117.3 \text{ mm}$$ Required shell thickness: $$t_r = \dfrac{P R}{S E - 0.6 P} = \dfrac{(4.0)(450)}{(144)(1.0) - 0.6(4.0)} = \dfrac{1800}{144 - 2.4} = \dfrac{1800}{141.6} = 12.71 \text{ mm}$$ Area required: $$A = d \cdot t_r = (117.3)(12.71) = 1491 \text{ mm}^2$$ Inside radius of nozzle: $$R_n = \frac{117.3}{2} - 8.0 = 58.65 - 8.0 = 50.65 \text{ mm}$$ Parallel limit: $$L_p = \max(d,\; R_n + t_n) = \max(117.3,\; 50.65 + 8.0) = 117.3 \text{ mm}$$ **(b)** Required nozzle thickness: $$t_{nr} = \dfrac{P R_n}{S E - 0.6 P} = \dfrac{(4.0)(50.65)}{141.6} = \dfrac{202.6}{141.6} = 1.431 \text{ mm}$$ Excess shell area $A_1$: $$A_1 = (t - t_r) d - 2 t_n (t - t_r)$$ $$A_1 = (12 - 12.71)(117.3) - 2(8.0)(12 - 12.71)$$ $$A_1 = (-0.71)(117.3) - (16.0)(-0.71)$$ $$A_1 = -83.28 + 11.36 = -71.92 \text{ mm}^2$$ > Note that $t < t_r$ (the shell is *under-thick*), so the shell alone provides **negative** excess. Credit zero — $A_1 = 0$ is conservative. Excess nozzle area $A_2$: $$A_2 = 2 (t_n - t_{nr}) L_p = 2(8.0 - 1.431)(117.3)$$ $$A_2 = 2(6.569)(117.3) = 1541.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ Total available without pad: $$A_{\text{avail}} = A_1 + A_2 = 0 + 1541.5 = 1541.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ Compare: $$A_{\text{avail}} = 1541.5 \text{ mm}^2 \;\geq\; A = 1491 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ (just adequate, margin } 3.4\%)$$ > The opening is *just* adequate without a pad. Since margins below ~10% are not considered robust in practice, a pad is recommended. **Minimum pad thickness** for an additional design margin of 30%: Required $A_{\text{avail, new}} = 1.30 \times 1491 = 1938 \text{ mm}^2$ Required $A_3$: $$A_3 \geq A_{\text{avail, new}} - A_2 = 1938 - 1541.5 = 396.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ Required pad thickness: $$t_p = \dfrac{A_3}{L_p} = \dfrac{396.5}{117.3} = 3.38 \text{ mm} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{specify } t_p = 6 \text{ mm (next standard)}$$ > **Conclusion:** A 6 mm reinforcing pad is recommended for a 30% margin. The opening is technically adequate without a pad but lacks design robustness. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **Reinforced opening** = opening whose removed material is compensated by added load-bearing area within a defined zone (UG-37). - [ ] **Governing inequality:** $A_{\text{avail}} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + A_4 + A_5 \;\geq\; A = d \cdot t_r \cdot F$. - [ ] **$F$**: 1.0 for shells, spheres, formed heads at the centre; consult UG-39 for flat heads. - [ ] **Finished diameter $d$**: always OD of opening **plus** $2 \times$ corrosion allowance. - [ ] **Required thicknesses**: $t_r$ from UG-27 (cylindrical: $P R/(SE - 0.6P)$); $t_{nr}$ from pipe code or UG-27 for the nozzle. - [ ] **Parallel limit $L_p = \max(d,\; R_n + t_n)$** — governs width of reinforcement zone along vessel wall. - [ ] **Normal limit** — bounded by the inside of vessel and the outside of pad/nozzle, but **only material above required thickness** is credited. - [ ] **$A_1$**: only **excess** shell $(t - t_r)$ contributes, less the slot where the nozzle wall passes through. - [ ] **$A_2$**: only **excess** nozzle $(t_n - t_{nr})$ contributes; **outside** the opening. - [ ] **$A_3$**: pad area capped at $L_p \cdot t_p$ (single-side) or $2 L_p \cdot t_p$ (double-side). - [ ] **$A_4$** (weld): credit is conservative; capped by minimum required weld. - [ ] **$A_5$**: integral forging credit; treated like $A_1$ in geometry. - [ ] **Multiple openings**: apply UG-41 if zones overlap; otherwise design independently. - [ ] **Limits on $A_4$**: never credit excess weld beyond minimum specified by UG-41. - [ ] **Pad material**: must be documented on drawing; not all fabricators allow pads. - [ ] **Verification step**: always finish with an explicit **numerical comparison** of $A_{\text{avail}}$ and $A$. - [ ] **Margin of safety**: typical design margin 10–30% above required $A$. - [ ] **Multiple openings on the same line** may be combined into a single equivalent opening under UG-41. - [ ] **Inspection**: reinforced openings must be examined by NDT per UW-11 series; reinforcement affects weld geometry. > [!TIP] > **One-line memory hook for the exam:** > *"Find $A$, find $A_1$ to $A_5$, sum and compare — **A avail ≥ A**."*

topics.title: Design of Tall Vessel under Wind and Seismic Load. Design of Leg, Lug and Saddle Supports. Buckling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET862 - PRESSURE VESSEL AND PIPING DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Tall Vessel under Wind and Seismic Load. Design of Leg, Lug and Saddle Supports. Buckling

# MODULE 3: Design of Tall Vessels, Supports, and Buckling Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Tall Vessel"?
A pressure vessel is classified as a **Tall Vessel** when its height (or tangent length) is large enough that environmental loads — particularly **wind** and **earthquake (seismic)** — produce bending moments and shears comparable to (or greater than) the stresses caused by internal pressure. The classical industry rule is:

$$\frac{H}{D_i} \geq 10 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{L}{D_i} \geq 5$$

where $H$ is the total height, $L$ is the tangent length, and $D_i$ is the inside diameter. By this rule, a distillation column, a catalytic reactor stack, or a regenerator is virtually always a "tall vessel."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET862 / Module 3):**
> A tall vessel is a vertical pressure vessel whose structural design is governed by the combined action of internal pressure, **weight**, **wind**, and **seismic** loads, requiring explicit calculation of **overturning moment**, **base shear**, and **maximum compressive longitudinal stress** at the windward (and leeward) meridian. Supports — legs, lugs, or saddles — are designed to transmit these loads safely to the foundation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Palm Tree and the Skyscraper"
Imagine a **palm tree** standing in a storm. Three forces act on it: the wind pushing it sideways (lateral), gravity pulling it down, and the soil pushing it back at the roots (reaction). A tall pressure vessel behaves in **exactly** the same way:
- **Wind / Earthquake** = the storm (lateral load).
- **Weight of vessel + contents** = gravity (axial compression).
- **Anchor bolts / base ring** = the soil (reaction at supports).

The "bending stress" in the trunk of the tree corresponds to the **bending stress in the shell** of the vessel. If the tree is too slender, it **buckles** — that is the connection between tall-vessel design and **buckling analysis**.

### 1.3 Types of Supports Studied in This Module
| Support Type | Typical Use | Load Transfer |
|---|---|---|
| **Leg Support** (cylindrical/conical) | Small-to-medium tall vessels, $D \leq 1.5$ m | Axial compression → concrete pedestal |
| **Lug Support** (plate lug + gusset) | Horizontal vessels on columns; vertical vessels braced to steel | Shear & bending → structural steel |
| **Saddle Support** | Horizontal vessels only (not for vertical) | Two-line contact → vessel shell |

### 1.4 Buckling — A First Look
Buckling is **sudden, catastrophic lateral failure** of a structural member under compression, *not* by crushing of material but by **loss of geometric stability**. For pressure vessels, three types are critical:

1. **Column buckling** of vessel shell (treated as a cantilever).
2. **Shell buckling under external pressure** (vacuum).
3. **Local buckling** of stiffeners, conical transitions, and tori-spherical heads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PEMET862 explicitly links wind/seismic loads to **combined longitudinal stress** in the shell, the design of **leg / lug / saddle** supports, and the **buckling** checks mandated by ASME Section VIII Div. 1 (UG-28, UG-54) and IS 2825.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wind Load on a Tall Vessel (IS 875 Part 3, 2015)

A tall vessel is treated as a **cylindrical structure** with one or more attachments (nozzles, platforms, ladders). The wind pressure is *not* uniform — it increases with height (the "wind gradient"). IS 875 Part 3 (2015) prescribes:

**Step 1 — Basic Wind Speed $V_b$**
A zone-specific value (33–50 m/s for Kerala) read from the IS 875 map.

**Step 2 — Design Wind Speed $V_z$ at height $z$**

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$$

where
- $k_1$ = risk coefficient (1.0 for normal buildings; 1.08 for important structures).
- $k_2$ = terrain, height & structure size factor (1.0–1.36).
- $k_3$ = topography factor (1.0 for flat).
- $k_4$ = importance factor for cyclonic region.

**Step 3 — Design Wind Pressure**

$$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^{\,2} \quad \left[\text{N/m}^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Wind Force on a Section**

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$$

where $C_f$ is the **force coefficient** (≈ 0.7 for a smooth cylinder with $V_z D \leq 6 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$; up to 1.2 for $V_z D \geq 45 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ — IS 875 Table 10), and $A_e$ is the effective frontal area.

**Step 5 — Overturning Moment**
The vessel is divided into $n$ horizontal strips; the moment about the base is:

$$M_{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cdot h_i$$

where $F_i$ is the wind force on strip $i$ and $h_i$ is the centroid height of that strip above the base.

### 2.2 Seismic Load (IS 1893 Part 4, 2015 / Equivalent Static Method)

For industrial structures (which includes tall vessels), the **equivalent static method** is acceptable for $H \leq 40$ m. The **design horizontal seismic coefficient** is:

$$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $Z$ | Zone factor (Kerala = III) | **0.16** |
| $I$ | Importance factor | 1.0 / 1.5 |
| $R$ | Response reduction factor (ductile vessel) | **3.0 – 4.0** |
| $S_a/g$ | Spectral acceleration coefficient (from IS 1893 spectra) | 1.0 – 2.5 |

The total **base shear**:

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W_e$$

where $W_e$ is the **seismic weight** = dead weight + 25 % of full live load (for vessels: weight of empty vessel + 100 % of operating weight of contents + 25 % of insulation).

The **base moment**:

$$M_B = V_B \cdot \bar{h}$$

where $\bar{h}$ is the height of the center of mass above the base (≈ $0.6 H$ to $0.7 H$ for a uniformly distributed mass).

### 2.3 Combined Longitudinal Stress (Critical for KTU)

For a vertical vessel the **maximum longitudinal compressive stress** at the windward side (or leeward, depending on the wind direction) is:

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_{PL} + \sigma_{BL} \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}}$$

with

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{p_r (D_i + 0.6 t)}{4 t E_w} \quad \text{(from UG-23 internal pressure; corrected for joint efficiency)}$$

Wait — the *longitudinal* stress from internal pressure in a *vertical* vessel (where pressure is axial) is:

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{p_r \cdot D_i}{4 t \cdot E_w}$$

$$\sigma_{BL} = \frac{4 M_{\max}}{\pi D_i^{\,2} t} \quad \text{(bending — for thin shells)}$$

For *thick* shells or for rigorous analysis the ASME Code uses the section-modulus of the ring (UG-54). For Module 3 the thin-shell form is acceptable when $t/D_i \leq 0.05$.

The **allowable** (ASME VIII-1, UG-23 combined) is:

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 1.0 \cdot S E \quad \text{when wind/seismic is not the governing load}$$
$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 1.2 \cdot S E \quad \text{when wind/seismic governs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 1.2 factor?** The ASME Code permits a 20 % overstress for "occasional" loads such as wind and earthquake because their probability of simultaneous occurrence with maximum pressure is small — this is a classic *load combination* concept.

### 2.4 Leg Support — Design Procedure
Legs are **compression members**, often I-sections or pipe sections welded to a base ring on the vessel bottom.

**Axial load per leg:**

$$P = \frac{W}{N}$$

**Bending moment per leg** (eccentricity of vertical load from column centroid):

$$M_{leg} = \frac{M_{\text{wind}}}{N} + P \cdot e$$

**Compressive stress in leg:**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A_g} + \frac{M_{leg}}{Z_g}$$

**Slenderness check (IS 800 / AISC):**

$$\lambda = \frac{K L}{r_{\min}} \leq 200 \quad \text{(for bracing members)}$$

**Column buckling stress (IS 800 — secant / Perry-Robertson):**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{f_y}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_e^{\,2}}}$$

where $\phi = 0.5 \left[1 + \alpha (\lambda_e - 0.2) + \lambda_e^{\,2}\right]$ and $\lambda_e = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cc}}$.

### 2.5 Lug Support — Design Procedure
A **lug plate** carries **shear** $V$ and **tension/compression** $P$ from a horizontal vessel or a vertical vessel braced to a column. The critical checks are:

| Check | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Bearing on lug plate** | $\sigma_b = V / (d \cdot t_{lug}) \leq 0.9 S_y$ |
| **Shear in pin** | $\tau = 4V / (\pi d^2) \leq 0.4 S_y$ |
| **Bending of lug plate** | $\sigma = 1.5 V L / (t \cdot b^2) \leq 0.66 S_y$ |
| **Fillet weld to vessel** | $t_w = V / (0.707 \cdot S_{weld} \cdot 0.707 \cdot L_w)$ (E70XX, $S_{weld}$=320 MPa design) |
| **Tear-out (block shear)** | per AISC J4 |

### 2.6 Saddle Support — Zick's Analysis (Horizontal Vessels)
The classic Zick (1956) method for a horizontal vessel on **two saddles** gives:

**Longitudinal bending stress (at saddle horn, top of shell):**

$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{M_A}{K_1 \cdot \pi R^2 t}$$

**Longitudinal stress at bottom (between saddles):**

$$\sigma_2 = +\frac{M_C}{K_2 \cdot \pi R^2 t}$$

**Tangential shear at saddle horn:**

$$\tau = \frac{K_3 \cdot V}{R t}$$

**Circumferential bending at saddle:**

$$\sigma_3 = -\frac{K_4 \cdot V}{A} \leq 0.5 S$

**Stress intensification factor** at the saddle (K-factor from Zick chart) and the **stiffener ring** design (if $L/R > 8$).

### 2.7 Buckling — The Three Critical Checks

**(a) Column Buckling of Tall Vessel (treated as cantilever)**
Using Euler's formula (for long, slender shells):

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K H)^2}$$

The vessel must satisfy $P_{\text{actual}} \leq P_{cr} / F.S.$

**(b) Shell Buckling under External Pressure (ASME VIII-1 UG-28)**

For a cylinder, the critical pressure is:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D_o)^{2.5}}{(L/D_o) - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}} \quad \text{(long cylinders)}$$

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D_o)^{1.5}}{\frac{L/D_o}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{1.0}{(L/D_o)^2}}}} \quad \text{(intermediate)}$$

The **allowable** is $p_a = p_{cr} / F.S.$ with $F.S. = 3.0$ for vessels.

**(c) Wind-Induced Buckling (HTRI / Brownell)**
For tall columns under wind:

$$\sigma_c + \sigma_{BL} \leq \sigma_{cr,\text{shell}}$$

where $\sigma_{cr,\text{shell}}$ is the elastic shell-buckling stress (Donnell-Batdorf):

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{0.6 E \, t}{R}$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design wind speed at $z$ | $V_z = V_b k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4$ | m/s |
| 2 | Wind pressure | $p_z = 0.6 V_z^{\,2}$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | Wind force on strip | $F_i = C_f A_e p_z$ | N |
| 4 | Overturning moment | $M_w = \sum F_i h_i$ | N·m |
| 5 | Seismic base shear | $V_B = A_h W_e$ | N |
| 6 | Seismic moment | $M_B = V_B \bar{h}$ | N·m |
| 7 | Long. stress from pressure | $\sigma_{PL} = p_r D_i / (4 t E_w)$ | MPa |
| 8 | Bending stress in shell | $\sigma_{BL} = 4 M / (\pi D_i^{\,2} t)$ | MPa |
| 9 | Allowable combined | $1.2 S E$ (wind/eq governs) | MPa |
| 10 | Leg slenderness | $\lambda = KL/r_{\min} \leq 200$ | — |
| 11 | Euler buckling load | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / (KH)^2$ | N |
| 12 | External pressure (long cyl.) | $p_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D_o)^{2.5} / [L/D_o - 0.45(t/D_o)^{0.5}]$ | Pa |
| 13 | Shell buckling stress | $\sigma_{cr} = 0.6 E (t/R)$ | MPa |
| 14 | Saddle moment $M_A$ | $M_A = P L R (1 - 2a/L) / 4$ | N·m |
| 15 | Lug bending | $\sigma = 1.5 V L / (t b^2)$ | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** This design workflow is used in refinery columns (Indian Oil, BPCL), LNG storage tanks, ethylene cracking columns, and pharma reactors. The wind/seismic logic is also the backbone of *ASME STS-1* (Stainless Steel Chimney Liners) and *API 650* Annex E (Seismic Design of Storage Tanks).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overturning Moment from Wind

Consider a tall vessel divided into $n$ horizontal strips. The total wind force on each strip is:

$$F_i = C_{f,i} \cdot (D_o \cdot \Delta h_i) \cdot p_{z,i}$$

**Step 1.** Identify the *centroid height* $h_i$ of each strip. For a strip from $z_{i-1}$ to $z_i$:

$$h_i = \frac{z_{i-1} + z_i}{2}$$

**Step 2.** Each strip's force creates a moment about the base. The total overturning moment is the *sum* (since moments are vector quantities about a common point):

$$M_w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cdot h_i$$

**Step 3.** Substituting the wind force:

$$M_w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{f,i} \cdot D_o \cdot \Delta h_i \cdot 0.6 \cdot V_{z,i}^{\,2} \cdot h_i$$

**Step 4.** For *practical* KTU calculations the vessel is often modeled as 4–6 strips (e.g. 0–5 m, 5–10 m, 10–20 m, 20–30 m, 30–40 m, 40 m–top). The sum of each term gives the design moment.

**Step 5.** The *base shear* (horizontal force transmitted to foundation) is:

$$V_B^{(w)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Longitudinal Compressive Stress

**Step 1.** The vessel shell is a thin cylinder; internal pressure produces a *membrane* stress along the axis:

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{p_r \cdot r_i}{t} = \frac{p_r \cdot D_i}{2 t \cdot 2 \cdot E_w} \cdot 2 = \frac{p_r D_i}{4 t E_w}$$

Here $E_w$ is the *weld joint efficiency* (= 1.0 for full radiography, 0.85 for spot, 0.70 for none — UG-23).

**Step 2.** Bending due to wind/earthquake produces a linearly varying stress across the diameter. For a circular cross-section treated as a thin ring, the *section modulus* is:

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{\pi R^3 t}{R} = \pi R^2 t = \frac{\pi D_i^{\,2} t}{4}$$

**Step 3.** The bending stress is:

$$\sigma_{BL} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{M}{\pi R^2 t} = \frac{4 M}{\pi D_i^{\,2} t}$$

**Step 4.** Combining them, the *most compressed fibre* (windward) carries:

$$\sigma_{L} = \sigma_{PL} + \sigma_{BL}$$

**Step 5.** Code allowable (UG-23, including the 1.2 overstress for occasional loads):

$$\sigma_L \leq 1.2 \cdot S \cdot E \quad \text{when wind or seismic dominates}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Euler Buckling of a Cantilever Tall Vessel

A vertical vessel anchored at the base acts as a *cantilever* under axial compression. The effective length factor $K = 2.0$ for a fixed-free column, and the slenderness is:

$$\lambda = \frac{K H}{r}$$

where $r$ is the **radius of gyration** of the shell. For a thin cylindrical shell:

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi R^3 t}{\pi \cdot 2 R t}} = \frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$$

The Euler critical load is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K H)^2}$$

**Step 1.** Substitute $I = \pi R^3 t$ and $K = 2.0$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E \pi R^3 t}{(2H)^2} = \frac{\pi^3 E R^3 t}{4 H^2}$$

**Step 2.** Critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^3 E R^3 t}{4 H^2 \cdot 2 \pi R t} = \frac{\pi^2 E R^2}{8 H^2}$$

**Step 3.** Define a *buckling safety factor*:

$$\text{FS} = \frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_L} \geq 1.5 \quad \text{(typical KTU problem requirement)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** The $H^2$ in the denominator means that **doubling the height reduces the critical load by 4×**. This is why tall-vessel design is dominated by the moment and buckling check, not by the pressure check.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Shell Buckling under External Pressure (Long Cylinder)

For a *long* cylinder ($L > 10 D_o$), experimental and theoretical results (Von Mises) give:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 E}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \frac{(t/D_o)^{2.5}}{L/D_o - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}}$$

**Step 1.** For steels, $\nu = 0.3$, so $(1 - \nu^2) \approx 0.91$. The code form is:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 E (t/D_o)^{2.5}}{(L/D_o) - 0.45 (t/D_o)^{0.5}}$$

**Step 2.** Code allowable (UG-28, factor of safety 3):

$$p_a = \frac{p_{cr}}{3.0}$$

**Step 3.** The design check requires:

$$p_{\text{ext}} \leq p_a$$

where $p_{\text{ext}}$ = external pressure (= 1 bar for vacuum, or = design external pressure).

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Zick's Saddle Longitudinal Stress (Top of Shell at Saddle)

For a horizontal vessel of radius $R$, total length $L$, on two saddles separated by distance $2a$ (i.e. one saddle at $a$ from each head), with weight $W$ uniformly distributed:

**Step 1.** Reaction at each saddle (vertical):

$$R = \frac{W}{2}$$

**Step 2.** Bending moment at the saddle (over the support) — Zick's formula:

$$M_A = \frac{-W a}{2} \left(1 - \frac{a}{L}\right) = -\frac{W a (L - a)}{2 L}$$

**Step 3.** Bending moment at midspan (between saddles):

$$M_C = +\frac{W a^2}{2 L}$$

**Step 4.** Longitudinal stress at top of shell at the saddle (the *highest* compressive point):

$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{12 M_A \cdot R}{t^2 (3 L_1 + 1.56 \sqrt{R t}) \cdot K_1}$$

For a thin shell (K-factor approach):

$$\sigma_1 = -\frac{M_A}{K_1 \pi R^2 t}$$

with $K_1$ from the Zick chart (function of $L/R$ and $a/R$).

**Step 5.** Stress check:

$$\vert \sigma_1 \vert \leq 1.5 S \quad \text{(per UG-77 for local stress)}$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem for KTU)

**Problem:** A vertical distillation column is $H = 30$ m tall with $D_i = 2.0$ m, $t = 25$ mm, $W_{\text{empty}} = 250$ kN, $W_{\text{operating}} = 1200$ kN. $V_b = 39$ m/s, Zone III, $k_2 = 1.10$, $k_1 = k_3 = k_4 = 1.0$, $C_f = 0.8$, $S = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $E_w = 1.0$. Design pressure = 1.0 MPa.

**Step 1 — Wind load (one strip approximation, using average $V_z$).**
Take $V_z = 39 \times 1.10 = 42.9$ m/s; conservative uniform strip.

$$p_z = 0.6 \times 42.9^2 = 0.6 \times 1840 = 1104 \text{ Pa}$$

Wind force on shell (per unit length):

$$q = C_f \cdot D_o \cdot p_z = 0.8 \times 2.05 \times 1104 = 1811 \text{ N/m}$$

Overturning moment (uniform load, $q$):

$$M_w = \frac{q H^2}{2} = \frac{1811 \times 30^2}{2} = 815{,}000 \text{ N·m} = 815 \text{ kN·m}$$

Base shear:

$$V_B^{(w)} = q H = 1811 \times 30 = 54{,}330 \text{ N} = 54.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Seismic load (IS 1893).**
Take $A_h = 0.10$ (typical for Zone III, $R=3$, $I=1$, $S_a/g = 2.0$).

$$V_B^{(s)} = A_h W_e = 0.10 \times 1200 = 120 \text{ kN}$$

Seismic moment ($\bar{h} = 0.6 \times 30 = 18$ m):

$$M_s = 120 \times 18 = 2160 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Governing load (larger moment governs in this case, i.e. seismic).**
$M_{\max} = M_s = 2160$ kN·m.

**Step 4 — Bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{BL} = \frac{4 \times 2160 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (2000)^2 \times 25} = \frac{8.64 \times 10^9}{3.14 \times 10^9} = 2.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Pressure longitudinal stress:**

$$\sigma_{PL} = \frac{1.0 \times 2000}{4 \times 25 \times 1.0} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Combined:**

$$\sigma_L = 20 + 2.75 = 22.75 \text{ MPa} \leq 1.2 \times 250 = 300 \text{ MPa} \;\; ✓$$

**Step 7 — Check column buckling.** $K=2$, $r = R/\sqrt{2} = 1025/\sqrt{2} = 725$ mm.

$$\lambda = \frac{2 \times 30{,}000}{725} = 82.7$$

$\sigma_{cr}$ (Euler):

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \frac{9.87 \times 2 \times 10^5}{82.7^2} = 288 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{FS} = \frac{288}{22.75} = 12.7 \;\; \text{(very safe)}$$

**Step 8 — External pressure check (assume vacuum, $p_{\text{ext}} = 0.1$ MPa):**
$D_o = 2050$ mm, $L = 30{,}000$ mm, $L/D_o = 14.6$ — long cylinder.

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (25/2050)^{2.5}}{14.6 - 0.45 (25/2050)^{0.5}} \approx \frac{5.2 \times 10^5 \times 1.86 \times 10^{-6}}{14.5} = 0.0666 \text{ MPa}$$

This is **less than 0.1 MPa vacuum** → a *stiffener ring* is mandatory! (Discussed in module 3 syllabus under buckling.)

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tall Vessel — Free-Body Diagram of Loads

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV["Environmental Loads"]
        W["Wind Force F_w (horizontal)"]
        E["Seismic V_B (horizontal)"]
    end

    subgraph VSL["Tall Vessel (H/D > 10)"]
        P["Internal Pressure p_r (axial & hoop)"]
        G["Weight W_op (axial)"]
        M["Bending Moment M = M_w or M_s"]
    end

    subgraph SUP["Supports"]
        L["Leg / Lug / Saddle"]
    end

    subgraph FOUN["Foundation"]
        AB["Anchor Bolts"]
        R["Reaction: N, V, M"]
    end

    W -->|produces| M
    E -->|produces| M
    P -->|longitudinal stress| VSL
    G -->|axial compression| VSL
    M -->|transmitted to| SUP
    VSL -->|load path| L
    L --> AB
    AB --> R
```

### 4.2 Design Workflow for Tall Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Start: Vessel dimensions D, H, t"] --> B["Estimate W_empty, W_op"]
    B --> C{"Compute V_z & p_z<br/>per IS 875"}
    C --> D["F_i = C_f A_e p_z"]
    D --> E["M_w = sum F_i h_i"]
    B --> F["A_h = ZI/R x Sa/g<br/>per IS 1893"]
    F --> G["V_B = A_h W_e"]
    G --> H["M_s = V_B x h_bar"]
    E --> I["M_max = max of M_w, M_s"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["sigma_PL = p_r D / 4tE"]
    I --> K["sigma_BL = 4M / pi D^2 t"]
    J --> L{"sigma_L = sigma_PL + sigma_BL<br/>is it <= 1.2 S E?"}
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| M["OK for combined stress"]
    L -->|No| N["Increase t or stiffen"]
    M --> O{"Column buckling FS >= 1.5?"}
    N --> O
    O -->|Yes| P["OK"]
    O -->|No| Q["Add stiffener ring or<br/>increase t"]
    Q --> R["Re-check p_cr (UG-28)"]
    P --> S["Design supports<br/>(leg, lug, or saddle)"]
    R --> S
    S --> T["End"]
```

### 4.6 Buckling-Check Flowchart (UG-28 / Module 3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Given: D_o, t, L, E, nu, p_ext"] --> B["Compute t/D_o and L/D_o"]
    B --> C{"Is L > 10 D_o?"}
    C -->|Yes| D["Use long-cylinder<br/>p_cr = 2.6E(t/D)^2.5<br/>/(L/D - 0.45(t/D)^0.5)"]
    C -->|No| E["Use intermediate formula"]
    D --> F["p_a = p_cr / 3.0"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{"p_a >= p_ext?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Shell OK"]
    G -->|No| I["Add stiffener ring"]
    I --> J["Compute ring spacing<br/>L_eff = max(L_s, D_o)"]
    J --> K["Re-evaluate p_cr with L_eff"]
    K --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** The KTU answer-sheet evaluator often awards marks for a clean *flow diagram* of the design process. The four diagrams above are the most commonly drawn (free-body, design flow, support schematic, buckling flow). A student reproducing these in the exam using boxes and arrows (or an equivalent neat sketch) secures 1–2 extra marks per answer.
